Musk replied that total miles traveled would explode when people no longer have to drive themselves, traffic congestion would worsen even with autonomous cars, and therefore multi-level tunnel networks would be necessary — which is precisely what the Boring Company is building. The first major customer of the Boring Company's tunnels would effectively be Tesla's own robotaxi network.

The Dojo/FSD chip strategy follows the same internal-consumption pattern. Starting in 2026, Tesla's custom autonomous driving silicon will be embedded in hundreds of robotaxis. Excess compute capacity is funneled to xAI to train Grok. Tesla has offered to license FSD to other automakers, but the offer is essentially secondary — they don't need external licensees to justify the investment.

Optimus humanoid robots are being built first for Tesla's own factories, not for external sale. Giga Shanghai, Giga Texas, and Giga Berlin operate 24/7 and face constant labor shortages. Deploying even 100,000 Optimus units could save tens of billions in labor costs. Musk told Kathy Wood of ARK Invest that Optimus will begin transforming things in 2027, become obvious in 2028, and have massive impact by 2029. ARK's own research estimates humanoid robots are 200,000 times more complex to develop than robotaxis, but projected sufficient compute arriving by 2029 to enable full task capability.

The overarching vision reduces to three pillars: unlimited energy, unlimited intelligence, and unlimited manufacturing. Bacti argues this is essentially Musk's master equation. SpaceX's satellite infrastructure captures solar energy; xAI converts that energy into intelligence; Tesla and Optimus provide the manufacturing layer that turns any matter into any desired output. Bacti claims an MVP version of this system — capable of rapidly spinning up new manufacturing industries — could exist by 2030.

The "pioneer vs. merchant" framing explains why conventional business logic misreads Musk. A merchant mindset asks "who are the buyers?" before building. A pioneer mindset builds the infrastructure for a new civilization and becomes its own first consumer. Bacti compares this to early American settlers building banks and saloons for themselves before worrying about external customers. The US economy itself, Bacti argues, operates similarly — producing and consuming internally at massive scale.

Each component Musk builds is an accelerant for every other component. Better batteries improve bots, cars, and Megapacks, which improve AI data centers. Autonomous semis speed up Tesla factory deliveries. The system compounds: longer build times now produce faster acceleration later. The Amazon AWS parallel is cited — Amazon built cloud infrastructure for itself, then discovered it was a trillion-dollar external business.

The moon pivot is reframed as strategic acceleration toward Mars, not a retreat. Critics who say Musk abandoned Mars by refocusing on the moon are dismissed as lacking perspective. The argument is that everything built for moon infrastructure — rockets, life support, energy systems — will compress the timeline for building a sustainable Martian city, even if the first landing date slips.

Critics of incremental progress (e.g., "only 7 unsupervised robotaxis in Austin") are missing basic scaling math. You cannot go from zero to a million vehicles without passing through seven. The current Austin robotaxi deployment, however small, represents a necessary developmental stage, not a failure.
Elon to Kathy Wood: Optimus "will begin to transform things in 2027, be obvious in 2028, and have massive impact by 2029."
ARK Invest finding: Humanoid robots are "200,000 times more complex than robotaxis to develop."
Bacti's claim: "50% of all first principles of the entire universe are now owned by Elon" — meaning energy, intelligence, and manufacturing on the physical side.
Projected milestone: An MVP of the full energy-intelligence-manufacturing system by 2029-2030.
Bacti on shipping: Tesla could increase US shipping industry output by 200 times "very easily in 2030" using AI blueprinting and Optimus deployment.

title: Tesla Cybercab Quietly BREAKS The Mold

facts.text: The distinction between prototypes and production-line vehicles matters enormously. The 30-40 Cybercabs already spotted testing in various states — including Chicago and Alaska — were almost certainly hand-built units used for weather and road condition validation. The first vehicle off the new production line is different because it represents Tesla actually testing and proving the manufacturing process itself, not just the car.

The "unboxed" manufacturing model is unprecedented and is what makes this milestone significant. Tesla's approach involves building large modular sections separately and assembling them together — described as snapping four pieces together like Legos. This has never been done at scale by Tesla before, and the hosts note that Ford has since adopted a nearly identical approach, suggesting other automakers will follow. Tesla claims this process will eventually be five times faster than traditional manufacturing.

Production ramp will be painfully slow before it accelerates sharply. Drawing a direct parallel to the Cybertruck launch, Herbert predicts an S-curve trajectory: excruciating slowness for months, followed by a rapid spike once supply chains and parts are finalized. The hosts conservatively estimate around 20,000 to 30,000 units built by end of 2026, with Brian arguing the lower end of 20,000 is more realistic given the April-ish launch timing, compared to Cybertruck's January start date. Monthly output could reach 5,000 to 10,000 once the line is dialed in, possibly by October 2026.

Battery supply is not currently a bottleneck. For every Cybertruck Tesla produces, the same battery cells could power two or three Cybercabs. The potential future constraint is the Tesla Semi, which consumes the equivalent of roughly 15 Cybercabs worth of battery cells per unit. For now, cell supply is not the limiting factor for Cybercab deployment.

The geofenced rollout will expand city by city, not coast to coast. The hosts do not expect nationwide unsupervised FSD availability by end of 2026. Regulatory patchwork — individual states or even municipalities blocking approval — makes coast-to-coast coverage unrealistic in the near term. Elon confirmed that customer deliveries under $30,000 are expected in 2026, but those buyers will be restricted to approved cities and regions.

The first external buyers will almost certainly be fleet operators, not individual consumers. Herbert references Elon's earlier framing of buyers as "shepherds" — people responsible for managing a flock of Cybercabs, handling cleaning, maintenance, and vehicle upkeep. The hosts are skeptical that Tesla will sell to individual consumers who expect to drive the car anywhere they want. More likely buyers include fleet managers operating 10 to 100+ vehicles, or potentially large companies like Hertz managing fleets on Tesla's behalf. A niche use case floated is business travelers who want a dedicated, driverless car available on demand for a week at a time.

Insurance responsibility for unsupervised FSD will fall on Tesla, not the consumer. The hosts draw a parallel to Waymo, where the operator of the robotaxi network absorbs insurance liability. Revenue estimates of $30,000 per vehicle per year from the robotaxi network are cited. Tesla will only offer unsupervised FSD commercially when internal data confirms safety margins are sufficient to make insurance costs viable as a business model. The FSD subscription price for unsupervised operation is expected to be higher than the current $99/month supervised tier.

Safety data is trending positively and is not a showstopper. A PhD computer scientist named Reigns analyzed NHTSA monthly crash data and found Tesla FSD safety doubled from December to January. His modeling projects that Tesla FSD will surpass average human driver safety by March 2026, reaching approximately 700,000 miles between incidents. Of the 15 reported incidents since the Austin robotaxi launch in June, many involved the vehicle being parked, traveling under 2 mph in parking lots, or being struck by another party — categories that would not typically be reported for human drivers but are captured in Tesla's incident reporting.
Cybertruck production: 1 Model Y rolls off the line every 35 seconds; the goal for Cybercab is 1 every 10 seconds, eventually 1 every 5 seconds.
15 total incidents reported since Austin robotaxi launch in June.
Safety doubled from December to January; projected to surpass human safety by March 2026.
700,000 miles between incidents projected once minor/non-fault events are contextualized.
Estimated robotaxi revenue: $30,000 per vehicle per year.
One Tesla Semi consumes the battery equivalent of roughly 15 Cybercabs.
Conservative end-of-2026 production estimate: 20,000–30,000 units.

title: Tesla Boyz

facts.text: First Cyber Cab produced — on-time milestone reached. The hosts celebrate that Tesla has produced its first Cyber Cab in February 2026, ahead of an April production start date. Hans Nelson called it "about as close as we will ever get to a guarantee in Tesla land" that production is on track. Brian White draws a parallel to SpaceX's Starbase approach: delays during testing are productive, not idle, because they allow engineers to apply upgrades to the vehicle being prepared rather than waiting for the next one.

Production vehicles will initially be crushed, not sold. Brian recounts a conversation with a Cybertruck engineer he met at a California Supercharger, who confirmed that pre-production models are eventually crushed because they contain unresolved engineering issues. The same logic applies to early Cyber Cabs — vehicles built in Q1 2026 may never reach customers and could be destroyed. The advantage is that Tesla is its own first customer, meaning mistakes cost nothing externally.

The real bottleneck is FSD software, not manufacturing. Hans argues that the production line was ready before the firm April date was announced specifically because software milestones — not hardware — were the constraint. Unlike a manufacturing problem where you don't know how long it will take to solve, software milestones can be hit and then a delivery date confirmed with confidence. Nick adds that remaining FSD issues appear to be experiential (e.g., pulling into handicap parking spots) rather than safety-critical.

Supervised robotaxi fleet is growing rapidly, approaching 372 vehicles. As of the stream, Tesla's supervised robotaxi fleet stands at 372 vehicles: 283 in the Bay Area and 89 in Austin. The fleet added 42 units in the prior two days. Of those, only 7 are operating fully unsupervised (no safety monitor). The hosts see the growth trajectory as beginning to look exponential and cite Elon's stated goal of doubling the fleet monthly.

Cyber Cab cannot legally operate in San Francisco due to the absence of a safety driver position. Brian explains that San Francisco's regulatory framework requires a safety driver in the vehicle, which is structurally impossible in the Cyber Cab's two-seat design. Austin remains the only viable launch city for Cyber Cab robotaxi operations. The hosts estimate the first paid Cyber Cab rides will occur in March or May 2026, with Brian leaning toward March being achievable given the pace of testing already underway.

California regulatory timeline tied to miles-driven safety data. One host references analysis by "Reigns OnX" using NHTSA data, suggesting Tesla needs to reach the equivalent of 700,000 incident-equivalent miles to demonstrate safety parity with human drivers. Tesla was at 417 as of the stream (on a relative index), with the model projecting that threshold being crossed around March 15, 2026. However, the hosts note the data is noisy because human drivers don't self-report minor incidents (shopping cart clips, parking lot taps), making apples-to-apples comparison difficult.

MKBHD (Marques Brownlee) bet recap — head-shaving wager now live. The hosts play a clip of MKBHD from October 2024 saying there is "just no way" Tesla will deliver a Cyber Cab to a customer for $30,000 or less before 2027, offering to shave his head on camera if wrong. Elon Musk confirmed on X (the day prior to this stream) that a Cyber Cab will be sold to a customer before end of 2026 for under $30,000. Brian argues the $30,000 price is straightforwardly achievable given that a Model 3 — a far more complex vehicle — costs $36,000-$37,000, while the Cyber Cab has roughly half the battery, half the motors, less glass, and no steering wheel or pedals.

Fleet ownership model debate — individual owners vs. Tesla-owned fleet. The hosts debate who will own Cyber Cabs in the network. Nick predicts a model similar to Uber/Airbnb where individual owners contribute vehicles to the Tesla network. Brian notes 90% of all rides involve a single occupant, another 5% involve two people, making the Cyber Cab appropriate for 95% of use cases. Model Y variants without steering wheels or pedals are predicted as a likely future SKU for the remaining 5% requiring four seats. The Airbnb parallel is reinforced by the fact that Airbnb co-founder Joe Gabia (corrected mid-stream from an initial mistaken mention of Brian Chesky) sits on Tesla's board.

Why Tesla benefits from allowing third-party fleet ownership. Hans argues that beyond capital efficiency, distributed ownership reduces Tesla's antitrust and concentration risk. If Tesla owns the entire fleet and generates all the revenue, it becomes a regulatory target. Spreading ownership across many stakeholders creates a broader coalition of interests. The hosts also note that Tesla can still capture high-margin revenue from in-cabin experiences, transactions (Starbucks, McDonald's), entertainment, cleaning services, and charging — regardless of who owns the vehicle.

Tesla's shift to recurring revenue model is strategically bullish, not bearish. Brian pushes back on a critic who argued that discontinuing outright FSD sales in favor of subscriptions was a negative sign. The hosts explain that Wall Street assigns higher valuations to predictable, recurring cash flows than to one-time lump-sum payments. Herbert frames it as: the robo taxi model generates roughly $30,000 per vehicle per year in ongoing revenue versus a one-time $5,000-$7,000 profit on a car sale. Nick counts at least seven signs Tesla is transitioning its entire business toward a subscription model.

Cyber Cab FSD training expected to be fast due to simpler vehicle dynamics. Brian spoke with Tesla engineers who said adapting FSD to the Cyber Cab should be relatively quick because it is a simple, conventional vehicle. The Cybertruck by contrast required new training for variable steering, rear-wheel steering, and an unfamiliar spatial footprint. Engineers also indicated FSD could be ported to third-party manufacturers with relative ease.
"There are now 372 Tesla robotaxis out there. 283 in Bay Area, 89 in Austin. 42 units added in the last two days."
"To be as safe as a human, you need to be at 700,000 [incident-equivalent miles]. Reigns is expecting that to happen around March 15th."
MKBHD (October 2024): "If they do [deliver a Cyber Cab for $30,000 before 2027], I will shave my head on camera because I'm that confident they won't do it."
"The idea that you can't sell it for $30,000 is a little comical considering Model 3s, which are vastly more complicated, is $36,000-$37,000 with a good margin in it."
"90% of all rides is one person. Another 5% is two people. The rest — the final 5% — is more than two people."
"Why would you sell a car anymore? You just want to service it as a robo taxi. The robo taxi model generates closer to $30,000 per year ongoing versus a one-time $7,000 profit on a car sale."

title: How To TRADE Tesla Stock Options From Here

facts.text: Two core strategies: Alpha Churn and Alpha Roll (LEAPs). The churn strategy involves selling short-term, out-of-the-money puts — typically weeklies — to collect premium continuously. If Tesla is at $410, the host might sell $405 or $400 puts expiring in 5 days; as long as Tesla stays above the strike, the premium is pure profit. This works especially well in sideways or mildly bearish markets. The LEAP strategy is the opposite: buying long-dated (12–18 month), out-of-the-money calls, such as a $600 strike when Tesla is at $410, targeting a multi-hundred percent return if Tesla runs significantly higher.

How LEAP leverage works mechanically. The host uses the term "capital delta" to distinguish from the traditional options delta. If $100,000 is moved from Tesla stock into OTM calls and Tesla rises 10%, the call position tends to return roughly 2x what the stock would have returned. For example, a $600 LEAP purchased for $50 requires Tesla to reach $650 to break even at expiration; at $700 it returns 100%, at $750 it returns 200%, and at $800 it returns 300%.

The key advantage of LEAPs over margin and short puts: no squeeze risk. If Tesla drops 50% after you buy a LEAP, the position loses value on paper but you cannot be forced to close it. By contrast, selling deep in-the-money puts or trading on margin during a crash can force you to close at catastrophic losses that are unrecoverable. The tradeoff is time decay — LEAPs bleed value slowly when nothing happens, whereas selling short puts earns premium during sideways movement.

Optimal short put strike selection: always match the strike to where you think the stock will end up. The host explains that the theoretically perfect short put strike is the one that matches the stock's actual expiration price, because that maximizes the total premium collected (intrinsic gain plus time premium). A $440 in-the-money short put when Tesla is at $410 might yield $32 total but only $2 in time premium; a $415 at-the-money put might yield $10 in total but $5–7 in time premium. If Tesla ends at $415, the $415 put was the correct choice. Selling deeper in-the-money puts only makes sense if you have strong directional conviction and are willing to sacrifice premium income for potential upside capture.

When to buy LEAPs: forward-looking conviction about a major runup, not a stock being "cheap." The host explicitly rejects the idea of buying LEAPs simply because the stock has dropped. The correct trigger is a high-conviction belief that a significant price appreciation will occur within the first half of the option's life — ideally the first 6–9 months of an 18-month contract. He cites Tesla's drop to approximately $220 during tariff panic ("Liberation Day") as a case where he bought LEAPs not because the stock was cheap, but because he was confident in a V-shaped recovery.

Rolling LEAPs: always be forward-looking, not anchored to current losses. If a LEAP is at the 8-month mark with 30% losses, the host says he is uncomfortable holding anything with less than 12 months of remaining runtime due to accelerating time decay. He recommends rolling into a fresh 16-month contract. A 30% paper loss on a LEAP is described as "peanuts"; 50% is still manageable; 60–70% starts getting difficult. The cost of rolling (e.g., a hypothetical $2,100 per contract loss) is framed as the price of buying more time for the thesis to play out.

Rolling at a loss does not always result in worse outcomes — it is risk mitigation, not a mistake. If you roll and the stock then inflects within the original 8-month window, you will have made less money than if you had held. The host is emphatic that this kind of hindsight regret is "deadly thinking." The correct mental model is: you are purchasing safety against the scenario where the thesis takes longer to play out. If you are right and the stock reaches $750, even after one or two rollovers the gains easily absorb the rolling costs.

Benchmark everything against a 100% Tesla stock position, not against alternative option strategies. The host's personal rule: he will only execute an options strategy if he is highly confident it will outperform a simple buy-and-hold Tesla position. He explicitly prefers a near-certain 10–20% alpha over Tesla to a riskier strategy with potential 50% alpha but meaningful downside risk of underperforming the stock. This framework, he argues, naturally curbs greed and prevents reckless speculation.

title: Tesla Is Moving Faster Than Anyone Realizes

facts.text: Saudi Arabian AI firm Humane invests $3 billion into XAI's $20 billion funding round. The stake is structured to convert into SpaceX shares after a SpaceX-XAI merger closes. The hosts view this as validation that XAI can raise capital at will and that international partners, particularly in the Gulf, see Elon Musk's ventures as the highest-upside AI bet. XAI also separately announced a 500-megawatt data center in Saudi Arabia last November, alongside plans for Saudi rollout of Grok AI models.

XAI's Grok chatbot is expanding into Tesla vehicles in the UK and eight additional European markets. Brian White describes Grok in the car as transformative — functioning as a smart passenger that handles navigation by description rather than exact location names, finds stops along a route conversationally, and allows drivers to keep eyes on the road. He recounts using it to locate a Taco Bell en route, adding only six minutes to the journey. The hosts also discuss a coming "Hey Tesla" wake word feature and the Model Y L in China already identifying which seat a voice command originates from, enabling personalized control of seat warmers and other functions.

Tesla has hired 350 new employees at Giga New York (Buffalo) and invested $350 million in supercomputers at the site, while also beginning solar panel fabrication there. Brian White explains the backstory: Solar City made a deal with New York State, which paid roughly $1 billion to build the facility and leases it to Tesla for $1 per year, contingent on maintaining approximately 3,500 jobs statewide. Tesla has met this obligation in creative ways over the years — adding FSD data labeling teams, a Long Island service center, and now expanding solar manufacturing. Currently, Buffalo fabricates the housing and framing for solar panels (not the cells themselves, which Tesla hopes to manufacture soon) and assembles the inverters and circuitry for residential solar products.

Davis, California city councilors are preparing a resolution to ban contracts with Tesla, SpaceX, and other Musk-affiliated companies and to encourage CalPERS divestment from their publicly traded securities. The hosts note the city currently has no investments in Musk-controlled companies, meaning the resolution has no immediate fiscal impact and amounts to virtue signaling. They draw a parallel to Delaware, which reportedly faces a $500 million budget deficit partly because companies exited the state following perceived regulatory overreach. The broader point made is that cities and states blocking Tesla or autonomous vehicles will face competitive disadvantages — higher logistics costs, fewer business investments, and ultimately public pressure to reverse course.

Power availability is confirmed as the true bottleneck for AI data center expansion. Elon Musk publicly agreed with a report showing utilities estimate two to three years to deliver grid connections that data center developers expect in one year, with median developer expectations centering on 2027. Tesla's Megapack business is positioned as the solution — smoothing power delivery, handling time-of-use cost spikes, and providing the voltage stability that sophisticated AI chips require. A recent installation of 138 Megapacks at a former coal plant in Queensland, Australia (Clean Coast Swan Bank battery) is cited as one example of many such deployments now too frequent to individually track.

Tesla's converging strategy — what Brian White calls Elon being "a bit of a time traveler." XAI is building what will be the world's largest supercomputers, targeting the equivalent of one million H200 GPUs coherently linked across two facilities. Tesla Energy is scaling Megapack production with a new Houston factory coming online mid-2026. Solar panel manufacturing is ramping at Buffalo. A lithium refinery is under development. Terrabit (Terraab) infrastructure planning is underway. The argument is that Musk identified these bottlenecks five to ten years ago and built the company to own each layer of the stack before the demand materialized.
"$350 million invested in supercomputers at Giga New York; 350 new employees hired."
"In almost every country where Tesla sells Megapacks, they are number one — and it ain't even close. The exception being China, where Chinese companies do 12 times more."
XAI's Colossus supercomputer target: one million equivalent H200s coherently linked.
XAI 500-megawatt data center announced in Saudi Arabia, November 2024.
Delaware facing a $500 million budget deficit attributed partly to corporate departures following regulatory overreach.

title: Are You Seeing What Tesla Just Did?

facts.text: First CyberCab rolled off the production line, signaling April production start is on track.
CyberCab production ramp will be slow at first, then dramatically faster than anything Tesla has built before.
CyberCab involves an extraordinary number of first-ever technologies, which complicates the ramp.
The CyberCab is the first production vehicle ever designed to work in both right-hand and left-hand drive markets without modification.
The robo taxi fleet reached 383 vehicles (294 in the Bay Area, 89 in Austin), with 7 unsupervised, and is growing rapidly.
The 7 unsupervised vehicles are confined to a narrow geo-fenced zone, likely for PR and safety-data accumulation reasons.
Tesla's robo taxi hiring in 12 cities — full-time AI safety operators, rental readiness specialists, and robo taxi pricing engineers — signals imminent multi-city scaling.
Tesla's safety data is compelling and not being communicated effectively.
Tesla's rental program ($60/day for Model 3/Y, $70 for S/X, all-in with supercharging and FSD) is seen as a marketing and demand-generation tool, not primarily a revenue stream.
Tesla's lack of marketing and public awareness is viewed as a serious and growing problem.
Uber's $100 million supercharger purchase was dismissed as trivial.
Tesla Semi ramp is underway with new job postings, but autonomous trucking is 3-5 years out.

title: Tesla Cybercab Progress Is UNDENIABLE Now

facts.text: Regulatory hurdles are overstated for the US market. Nick and Brian explain that in the US, automotive products are largely self-certified — manufacturers don't need pre-approval the way European markets require. Robo-taxis are already de facto legal everywhere they aren't explicitly banned. States including California, Florida, Georgia, Texas, and Arizona have already granted explicit robo-taxi approval, collectively covering over 100 million people — more than a third of the US population. The main regulatory motivation for a federal framework is to prevent patchwork state-level rules (like hypothetical lighting requirements) from forcing costly retrofits later.

The 2,500-vehicle exemption cap is a manageable near-term constraint, not a hard ceiling. Current federal rules allow manufacturers to sell up to 2,500 non-compliant (pedalless/steeringwheelless) vehicles. Brian notes that Transportation Secretary Shawn Duffy streamlined the exemption application process, meaning Tesla could apply to raise the cap if production approaches that number. If a law like the SAFE CAR Act passes with a 90,000-unit allowance, that comfortably covers projected 2026 output. There's also an unresolved question of whether the cap applies to vehicles Tesla deploys in its own fleet versus vehicles it sells — potentially a significant legal distinction.

Crash statistics cited by critics are misleading due to over-reporting and lack of context. A commenter cited NHTSA data claiming Tesla's robo-taxis crash at four times the human rate. Nick counters that Tesla is required to report incidents that ordinary human drivers would never report — minor contact with shopping carts, slow-speed pole taps, parking lot grazes. These inflate the raw count dramatically. The more meaningful metric would be dollar value of damage or injury severity, not raw incident count, since a 2-mph bumper tap is categorically different from a serious collision. Nick acknowledges Tesla's software still has room to improve but says the commenter doesn't understand the reporting context.

The unsupervised Cybercab operation in Austin is real progress, not a gimmick. Nick visited Austin personally and confirmed Tesla vehicles are operating with no one in the driver's seat. He traces the evolution of skeptic goalposts: FSD was vaporware, then the "We Robot" event was just a stock pump, then early rides were staged for influencers, then it was only two cities, then there was a secret joystick — and now the objection is that the geofenced area is too small. He argues the trajectory over 18 months is objectively undeniable and predicts further expansion.

The bet about a sub-$30,000 Cybercab sale before end of 2026 is still on, with parameters being debated. Brian says a sale to Tesla's own subsidiary wouldn't count, but a sale to an independent fleet operator in Austin — even just five or ten units — absolutely would. He draws a parallel to Cybertruck, where employee deliveries counted as real deliveries. Coast-to-coast operation is not a requirement in his view.

Neural networks don't "read" signs the way humans do — the question misunderstands AI architecture. Nick explains that FSD's neural nets don't process language or text the way humans learn to read. They develop their own internal representations through reinforcement learning and backpropagation across network layers. A stop sign is understood through pattern and contextual training, not literacy. He describes it as the model "creating its own language" — decisions emerge from matrix math and probability weightings, which is why the system is often called a black box even to its own engineers.

Insurance costs will fall significantly but won't disappear entirely. Even with near-zero at-fault incidents, comprehensive and collision coverage will remain necessary due to uninsured/underinsured motorists (estimated at up to 50% of California drivers), weather events, road debris, and human drivers. However, Nick argues insurers will compete aggressively on price to capture volume, making coverage cheap enough that cities could potentially subsidize it for lower-income residents as a replacement for bus infrastructure.

Factory employment won't collapse immediately, and may never collapse as catastrophically as predicted. Nick pushes back on the idea that Optimus robots will reduce Tesla's factory workforce to 5-10%. He argues new job categories always emerge around transformative technology — social media managers and YouTubers didn't exist before the internet. He notes Nvidia, Anthropic, and OpenAI still employ large human workforces despite being AI-first companies. Brian adds that visible workers in the Cybercab factory footage include engineers like a product development lead he recognized, not just assembly line workers.
"90,000 is definitely enough for this year" — referring to the proposed SAFE CAR Act vehicle exemption limit.
On skeptics: "You are objectively wrong. We've seen this... over the span of 18 months. So where are we going to be in another 18 months?"

title: Why Tesla Stock Is DEFLATING Right Now

facts.text: Tesla stock is in a slow deflation phase from Q1 highs.: The host describes Tesla stock as a "hype balloon" that peaked around $490 and has since deflated to approximately $395–$400, a roughly 25% decline in Q1. He views this as a predictable air-gap period with no major catalysts, consistent with earlier warnings he says he made to his audience. The stock tested the $400 level but has bounced slightly, and he believes the maximum weakness of Q1 will likely occur in the first two to three weeks of March.

Robotaxi fleet growth is accelerating but has not yet moved the stock.: The host reports the Tesla robotaxi tracker is at 386 deployed vehicles as of the video date, and he expects it to cross 400 over the coming weekend. He lays out a doubling-rate trajectory: 450 by end of next week, 900 in March, and 1,800 in April — a target he says he predicted approximately 10 months prior. He notes the stock has shown zero reaction to the recent doubling of the robotaxi fleet, suggesting the market has not yet reached critical mass of attention on this metric.

The "forcing function" threshold is 1,800 robotaxis driving 200 miles/day at $1/mile.: The host clarifies his 1,800 robotaxi target is predicated on full commercial utilization — each vehicle averaging 200 miles per day at $1 per mile in margin. He acknowledges that if there is 50% underutilization, the functional equivalent threshold would be approximately 3,600 vehicles. He frames this as the point at which robotaxi revenue becomes a quantifiable, undeniable cash flow catalyst for the stock.

New Cybercab production-line units have been spotted.: The host references Joe Tegtmeyer capturing footage of new Cybercab production line vehicles, with approximately three additional units spotted "in the wild" that appear to be real production models. He describes this as "very exciting" but notes the stock did not react, consistent with his broader thesis that the market is not yet pricing in Cybercab/robotaxi progress.

Options strategy: selling short puts near $410, avoiding short calls.: The host describes his current tactical position as selling short puts at $410 and $405 expiring the following week, collecting approximately $8–$9 per contract. He frames this as a ~1% weekly alpha target if Tesla stays above $400. He is explicitly avoiding selling short calls because he remains bullish on the medium-term outlook and does not want to cap his upside if good news arrives unexpectedly. He states he has fully exited LEAP positions, having entered around $235–$240 and exited in the $420–$480 range.

title: Why Tesla Stock Is DEFLATING Right Now

facts.text: Tesla stock is in a confirmed downtrend but approaching a counterbalancing force. The speaker characterizes the stock as a hype balloon that peaked around $490-$490 and has steadily lost air. The current price around $400 represents a roughly 25% decline, which he says validated his Q1 caution call made weeks earlier. He believes maximum weakness in Q1 will occur in the first two to three weeks of March before a potential inflection upward.

The robo taxi deployment tracker is the central fundamental catalyst being watched. The tracker currently shows 386 deployed robo taxis, and the speaker expects to cross 400 over the weekend, with a target of 450 by end of the following week. His progression model — 450, then 900 in March, then 1,800 in April — mirrors a doubling rate he claims Elon Musk predicted, and which he himself called approximately 10 months prior.

The true forcing function threshold is 1,800 commercially operating robo taxis, not just deployed units. The speaker clarifies that the 1,800 figure only represents a meaningful cash flow signal if those vehicles are fully commercially operated, driving an average of 200 miles per day at $1 per mile. If utilization is 50%, the equivalent threshold would be 3,600 vehicles. He is transparent that this nuance matters for interpreting when the stock will actually respond.

The stock is not yet reacting to robo taxi growth, and the speaker doesn't know exactly when it will. Despite the doubling rate in deployments, the stock has shown zero reaction, meaning critical mass for market sentiment has not been reached. He estimates somewhere between 450 and 600 deployed robo taxis may be the threshold where growth starts to slowly counterbalance the ongoing deflation in stock price.

His current options strategy is selling short puts, not calls, reflecting a cautious bullish bias. He holds short puts at the $410 and $405 strikes expiring the following week, collecting roughly $8-$9 per contract. His break-even is approximately $401, meaning he profits if Tesla stays above that level. He explicitly avoids selling short calls because the slow deflation could be disrupted at any time by positive news, making upside risk asymmetric.

His four-part alpha generation framework is described in detail. The four pillars are: (1) precise fundamental prediction of Tesla's future including Cybercab production, Optimus, and margins; (2) macro geopolitical prediction covering Trump tariffs, Iran, Ukraine, and Europe; (3) the "alpha roll" strategy using long-dated LEAPS calls during major dips for 2x leverage, then rolling back into stock on the way up; and (4) the "alpha churn" strategy of systematically selling puts or calls depending on market positioning. He states he is currently out of all LEAPS positions, which he describes as a disciplined execution he is proud of.

He entered LEAPS around $235-$240 and exited systematically in the $420-$480 range. The majority of his outperformance relative to Tesla's stock last year came from these LEAPS positions. He acknowledges a poor execution period in December 2024 and Q1 2025 that cost him significantly, and he believes his current cycle has been executed with much more discipline.

Macro risks including Iran and the Supreme Court tariff ruling are assessed as low surprise factors. He believes Iran military action is unlikely to shock markets because it is too well-telegraphed, and that Trump faces internal pressure not to let any conflict hit markets. Regarding a Supreme Court ruling against Trump's tariffs, he argues that because these scenarios are so transparent and well-understood as risks, markets have already priced them in and will not react strongly.

Cyber cab production is showing early signs of real-world activity. Content creator Joe Techmire spotted approximately three production-model Cybercabs in the wild, which the speaker views as confirmation that something meaningful is happening in the manufacturing pipeline, even if the stock is not yet responding.

title: If You Buy a CYBERCAB How Much MONEY Could You Make?

facts.text: Elon Musk has publicly confirmed Cybercab will be sold to consumers for under $30,000 before end of 2026. Musk responded directly to a challenge from Dylan Loomis on X, confirming that individual customers would be able to purchase a Cybercab at that price point before 2027. This prompted Basher to model what the economics would actually look like for an individual owner.

Tesla has multiple strategic reasons to sell Cybercabs to individuals rather than operating the entire fleet itself. First, Uber and Lyft drivers could buy Cybercabs and quit ride-share, accelerating the transition away from human-driven ride-hail. Second, selling vehicles to consumers reduces Tesla's capital intensity — they build the car, sell it for a profit, and recycle that capital into more production. Third, and perhaps most importantly, creating thousands or millions of Cybercab owners creates a political constituency that will resist regulatory attacks on Tesla's autonomy business, mirroring the strategy Uber used with its drivers.

A key Tesla job posting confirms dynamic and surge pricing is planned for the robo-taxi network. Tesla is hiring a robo-taxi pricing software engineer tasked with designing algorithms incorporating base costs, taxes, tolls, market demand, and regional factors. The job description explicitly mentions "dynamic and surge pricing," signaling that a flat dollar-per-mile rate is a long-term aspiration, not a launch-day reality.

Current ride-share pricing context makes even a $1/mile Cybercab fare look dramatically cheap. A June 2024 study of Waymo, Uber, and Lyft pricing in San Francisco showed per-mile costs ranging from $4–$42 for short trips under a mile, and $4–$6 per mile even for trips between 2.7 and 5.8 miles. The model conservatively uses $1/mile, which Basher notes is already in use in Austin, Texas, and likely well below where pricing will actually start.

The core financial model uses specific assumptions to project owner income. Key inputs include: 25 mph average travel speed, 5-mile average trip length, 40% empty (repositioning) miles, 3-minute average loading/unloading time, Tesla's take rate fixed at 35%, 600,000-mile battery life (conservative versus a potential 1 million miles), $199/month FSD subscription, utilization rates ranging from 30% to 65%, $1 base fare plus $1/mile plus $0.42/minute idle charge, and home charging at $0.16/kWh.

At 30% vehicle utilization — the worst-case scenario — an individual owner generates roughly $32,000 in gross revenue annually, yielding approximately $14,000 in gross profit after all costs. Costs include FSD ($2,400/year), charging ($2,300/year), tires ($2,800/year), vehicle depreciation/financing, EV fees (~$1,300/year), and miscellaneous expenses. Cleaning and parking are zeroed out assuming home-based ownership but noted as variables.

Gross profit scales dramatically with utilization. At 35% utilization (8.5 hours/day active), gross profit rises modestly. At 65% utilization (15.6 hours/day), gross profit approaches $40,000–$41,000 per year. Variable costs like tires and vehicle depreciation increase with mileage, but fixed costs like financing remain constant, improving margins at higher utilization.

After a 35% tax rate, net profit at 30% utilization is approximately $8,000/year, meaning the car pays for itself in roughly 3.5 years. At 35% utilization the payback period drops to about 3 years. At 65% utilization, net profit approaches $25,000–$26,000 — nearly recovering the entire $30,000 purchase price in a single year.

The model also works in dense urban scenarios (15 mph, 3-mile trips, supercharger pricing at $0.35/kWh) and rural scenarios (45 mph, 25-mile trips, 100% empty return miles, $5 base fare). In urban settings, gross profit drops only slightly — from $14,000 to ~$13,000 at 30% utilization. In rural settings, gross profit at 30% utilization is approximately $21,000, rising to $57,000 at 65% utilization, demonstrating the model's robustness across geographies.

Tesla's optimal take rate, based on fairness modeling, appears to be 30–35%. At a 15% take rate, the owner earns 269% more than Tesla — clearly unsustainable for Tesla. At 25% (which Musk once cited), owners still earn 1.7x what Tesla earns. At 30–35%, the split approaches rough parity, with owners earning 0–25% more than Tesla depending on utilization. Basher argues this is the likely equilibrium range, with the rate potentially being dynamic rather than fixed.

FSD pricing is one of three levers Tesla can adjust to balance economics. Tesla could offer FSD for free and raise the take rate, charge $199/month and use a 35% take rate, or charge $999/month and lower the take rate to 15–20%. At $2/mile pricing, even a $1,000/month FSD fee with a 30–35% take rate remains fair for both parties. The key insight is that Tesla has enormous flexibility to optimize across vehicle price, FSD cost, and take rate simultaneously.

Tesla's fleet-scale economics are staggering even at $1/mile. With 10 million Cybercabs at 65% utilization, Tesla could generate approximately $250 billion in net income. At 50 million vehicles (estimated US need), net income approaches $1 trillion. At 100 million globally, net income could reach $1–2 trillion. Applying a conservative P/E of 30, that implies a valuation of $2.5 trillion to $70 trillion for the robo-taxi business alone — not including Optimus or other revenue streams.

A poll of 1,147 X users revealed extraordinary consumer demand. Only 7% said they would not buy a Cybercab. 20% would buy one, 37.5% would buy two to five, and 35% would buy more than five. Extrapolating, that sample of 1,000 respondents could have generated 3,000 to 8,500 individual orders if ordering were open today.

title: Tesla Pulls Cybertruck Surprise

facts.text: The new base Cybertruck is priced at $59,990 and retains the dual motor all-wheel drive powertrain. Unlike the failed rear-wheel drive variant from the previous year, this truck keeps the performance fundamentals intact. It still does 0-60 mph in 4 seconds and offers 325 miles of range.

What the base trim loses compared to the premium is relatively minor. Omissions include air suspension, the rear passenger touchscreen, leatherette upholstery (replaced with cloth), ventilated front seats, two in-cabin power outlets, and a downgraded 7-speaker sound system instead of 15. Active noise cancellation is also removed. Notably, the motorized tonneau cover and bed power outlets are retained, which the host flags as critical for utility buyers.

The towing capacity drops from 11,000 lbs to 7,500 lbs on the new base trim, though the reason for this reduction is unclear. Wheel size also goes from 20-inch to 18-inch, with a $2,500 upgrade option available if buyers want larger wheels.

When adjusted for inflation, the $60,000 price is actually cheaper than the original 2019 unveiling price. When the Cybertruck was revealed in 2019, the dual motor version was priced at $50,000, which equals approximately $63,500 in today's dollars. The host cites a post from Matthew Donigan Ryan to make this comparison. Given that the air suspension was never part of the original promise and the rear screen and ventilated seats were additions added later, the host argues buyers are essentially getting the originally promised truck for less than the originally promised price.

For additional context, the host compares to the Ford F-150 Raptor, which started at $47,000 in 2019 and now starts at $79,000, illustrating that all trucks have gotten significantly more expensive while the Cybertruck has held its price relatively well.

The offer is time-limited, potentially only 10 days. Elon Musk responded to a post on X stating "Only for the next 10 days," but when asked for clarification added "depends on how much demand we see at this price level." The host speculates the 10-day deadline may be a soft corporate pressure tactic rather than a hard deadline.

The host revisits and dismisses the failed rear-wheel drive Cybertruck variant launched and canceled within 6 months in the prior year. That truck was priced at $70,000, lacked bed outlets, had no powered tonneau cover, no adaptive suspension, no rear light bar, and only offered 350 miles of range on a single motor. Even Tesla employees the host spoke with couldn't articulate who the target buyer was. He contrasts this clearly inferior and more expensive option with the new base trim to make the point that this new version is far better executed.

The host challenges several claims made in media coverage, particularly from what he describes as poor-quality articles. He disputes the framing of Tesla facing "severe business struggles," noting that Cybertruck production capacity is around 250,000 units annually while only about 25,000 are being built. He also pushes back on claims of brand damage affecting Tesla in China, arguing that Chinese consumers don't consume Western media and that Giga Shanghai continues to perform strongly.

On the topic of Tesla's financials, the host objects to excluding regulatory credits from automotive margin calculations. He argues that regulatory credits have been a core part of Tesla's business model since the beginning and that stripping them out to show the automotive segment "dipping into the red" is analytically misleading.

Cybertruck sales data cited in the coverage showed approximately 5,400 units sold in Q3, a 62% year-over-year drop, compared to Ford's Lightning selling roughly 10,000 in the same period before discontinuation. The host notes Ford's figures represent dealer lot shipments rather than actual customer deliveries, characterizing it as channel stuffing.

title: Cybercab Charging Approved, Cybertruck Gets Cheap & Big Bad Tunnel Time - LIVE!

facts.text: Cybercab production has officially begun at Giga Texas. The first production Cybercab rolled off the line, with footage circulating of it going around the facility. The panel views this as a significant signal: starting a production line is not something done two years before launch — it indicates Tesla has sufficient confidence in the underlying autonomy software for imminent deployment. Production is on track to begin in earnest in April 2026.

The launch implies a potential for the fastest vehicle scale-up in Tesla's history. Once production is optimized and the robotaxi platform can operate without remote monitors, the panel argues there is essentially unlimited demand for Tesla to deploy these vehicles itself rather than sell them to consumers. One panelist speculates Tesla may only sell a handful publicly, perhaps geofenced to Austin, since operating a single Cybercab in a fleet could recover its cost in roughly six months.

A new, more affordable all-wheel drive Cybertruck variant was announced, starting at $59,990. This is $20,000 less than the premium version. Key omissions include air suspension, the vault bed cover, cloth seats instead of vegan leather, a seven-speaker rather than nine-speaker audio system, and no premium center console. Everything else — four-wheel steering, steer-by-wire, power share, AWD, the large lockable frunk storage — is retained. Elon Musk subsequently tweeted that the pricing is only available for 10 days, which the panel interprets as either a demand-sensing test or a signal that the price will increase by $3,000–$5,000 afterward.

The value proposition of this Cybertruck was extensively benchmarked against competitors. A comparable XLT F-150 Super Crew at a local Ford dealership was quoted at $67,000 MSRP before dealer fees. The F-150 Raptor, which was $47,000 in 2019, now costs $79,000. The original 2019 Cybertruck AWD announcement price of $49,900 is equivalent to roughly $63,000 in 2026 dollars. The panel argues Cybertruck pricing has risen less than comparable ICE trucks and that ongoing fuel savings make the total cost of ownership significantly lower, especially for contractors who would benefit from power share.

The FCC granted Tesla a waiver to use ultra-wideband radio technology for its wireless EV charging system, specifically designed for the Cybercab. Normally this technology must be handheld or installed in fixed outdoor equipment under specific conditions; Tesla's charging pad required an exception. The panel notes this is critical infrastructure for running a fully autonomous fleet — eliminating the need for human intervention to plug in vehicles. Matthew DR added important technical context: Tesla has been pursuing a wireless charging frequency different from the SAE international standard, which he believes is the real reason wireless charging was quietly dropped from the Cybertruck (not vehicle height as Tesla stated). This FCC approval covers the UWB alignment component; the charging frequency standard issue remains unresolved.

Tesla announced 8 billion cumulative FSD miles driven. The panel notes that Elon had previously cited 10 billion as a milestone number, meaning Tesla is within months of hitting it given current daily mileage accumulation rates. Brian argued the significance is less about any specific threshold and more about the continuous improvement in training data output.

Tesla's FSD version 14.2.2.5 was highlighted for navigating a large highway flood puddle spanning nearly two lanes. The panel contrasted this with Waymo, which stops functioning in heavy rain or snow due to LiDAR scatter — a point Elon Musk himself made publicly. The panel noted Waymo's accident rate has remained flat quarter-over-quarter rather than improving, and criticized the broader legacy autonomous vehicle approach of retrofitting off-the-shelf vehicles with expensive sensor arrays rather than designing purpose-built hardware.

Tesla Giga New York (the original SolarCity Buffalo factory) was confirmed job-compliant with its $1/year lease agreement with New York State. The facility received $350 million in state funding and a nominal lease in exchange for job creation targets. After Tesla wound down its auto-labeling team there, compliance was in question. The factory now manufactures Superchargers (and potentially Megachargers) and is ramping solar panel production again, with Powerwalls manufactured in Nevada. A service center in Long Island was also counted toward the statewide job threshold.

The Boring Company's Nashville tunnel was approved unanimously by city council after a public hearing where officials asked basic questions about EV fire safety and wheel integrity. The panel noted that Boring's proof rock boring machine can drill through any terrain — in Las Vegas they simultaneously pumped groundwater while drilling. The tunnel lining system is a 12–14 inch six-piece overlapping ring designed for global terrain variability. The project is entirely privately funded at no cost to taxpayers. John noted that of the three tunneling companies operating in the US, one was acquired by a Chinese firm and another went bankrupt, leaving the Boring Company as the primary domestic option.

Additional brief items covered: the Model 3 won Edmunds' top-rated electric vehicle award; the Model S yoke steering wheel option was quietly removed from the configurator (except on the Plaid); GM and Ford were criticized for continued mixed signals on EV commitment while writing down tens of billions in EV losses; Saudi Arabia's Public Investment Fund-backed AI company Humane took a significant stake in xAI prior to the SpaceX-xAI merger discussions; and the Napa, California police department acquired a Tesla for DUI enforcement.

title: Why Tesla Robotaxis Are CLOSE To Inflection - And So Is The Stock

facts.text: Current fleet size and trajectory. As of the video, Tesla has 386 total robotaxis deployed across Austin, Texas and the Bay Area, California. The fleet started with just 11 units in Austin on June 24th (2025) and 3 in the Bay Area around September 1st, making the current scale a meaningful increase from a very small base.

Operational differences between Austin and Bay Area. In Austin, Tesla deploys safety drivers in the passenger seat — not legally required in Texas, but a voluntary Tesla safety measure that can be removed at Tesla's discretion. In California's Bay Area, drivers must sit behind the wheel, and California regulations require a minimum threshold of supervised miles to be submitted to regulators before driverless operation is permitted.

The December inflection point in fleet data. Around December 30th, Tesla dramatically changed its deployment pace, growing the Bay Area fleet from 153 to 295 vehicles — nearly doubling in 6 to 7 weeks. The presenter interprets this as Tesla deliberately starting the official data collection window it intends to submit to California regulators, since operators can choose their starting point for the mileage count but cannot manipulate data after that point begins.

Tesla is in the endgame for California driverless permits. The fleet doubling strategy is consistent with wanting to accumulate the required 50,000 to 60,000 supervised miles as quickly as possible to submit for a driverless operating permit. The presenter believes Tesla is very close to receiving California driverless approval and speculates it could be announced around April 2026, which he notes could itself serve as a minor stock catalyst.

Unsupervised fleet in Texas is still tiny but about to expand rapidly. Only 7 vehicles in Austin are currently operating truly unsupervised. The presenter predicts this number will hockey-stick in April, driven by the launch of the Cybercab — a two-seat vehicle that physically cannot accommodate a passenger-seat safety monitor. This creates a forcing function: Tesla must have unsupervised operation approved and operational in Austin before Cybercab can carry paying passengers at all.

The 1,800 robotaxi prediction and its financial significance. The presenter made a prediction in June 2025 that Tesla would reach 1,800 fully commercial robotaxis by April 2026. He argues this threshold matters because it would generate approximately 5% of Tesla's 2024 annual free cash flow on an annualized basis — enough to be a meaningful, measurable driver of cash flow that would show up materially in Q2 2026 earnings.

The stock-versus-fleet divergence is the core alpha opportunity. The Tesla stock price has been sliding downward since approximately December 19th, following a pattern similar to the prior year's post-peak drift. Meanwhile, the robotaxi fleet count has been climbing sharply. The presenter argues these two trend lines are fundamentally incompatible over the medium term and will have to converge — either the stock stops declining or it begins rising as the fleet growth becomes impossible for the market to ignore.

Near-term stock trajectory and reversal timing. The presenter expects the stock to continue drifting toward the $400 level or possibly lower in the near term, with the slide reversing when the fleet roughly doubles again from current levels to somewhere around 600 to 800 vehicles. He estimates this reversal will occur within 2 to 3 weeks of the video's recording, with the larger hockey-stick move happening at the 1,800 vehicle inflection point in April.

Macro risks acknowledged. The presenter notes that broader macro conditions — specifically referencing tensions involving Iran — could introduce additional pressure on the stock in March and potentially disrupt the timing of this thesis, even if the underlying robotaxi story remains intact.
Fleet growth from 153 to 295 Bay Area vehicles in approximately 6-7 weeks starting December 30th — roughly a 93% increase.
Only 7 truly unsupervised robotaxis currently operating in Texas.
89 supervised vehicles currently in Austin, for a combined Austin total near 96.
Regulatory mileage threshold for California driverless permit estimated at 50,000 to 60,000 miles.
1,800 commercial robotaxis projected to equal approximately 5% of Tesla's 2024 annualized free cash flow.

title: Elon Just Showed Us His Entire Playbook

facts.text: Optimus timeline correction (Friday post). Cathie Wood of Ark Invest posted that Tesla's Optimus robot would begin transforming factory and home life in 2028-2029, noting humanoid robot development is 200,000 times more complex than robo-taxis. Elon replied directly, pushing the timeline earlier: transformation begins in 2027, becomes obvious in 2028, and reaches massive impact by 2029. The host emphasizes that publicly correcting one of Tesla's most prominent bulls — and accelerating her already-bullish timeline by a year — is not something done casually. It represents a firm public commitment, not a casual boast.

Autonomous fleet declaration (Saturday post). Elon posted that Tesla will have "the largest fleet of autonomous vehicles as far into the future as I can imagine," using declarative language ("will have," not "might have"). For context, Waymo currently operates roughly 3,000 robo-taxis across six U.S. cities after more than a decade of operation and billions in Google funding. Tesla has approximately 383 robo-taxis — 89 in Austin and 294 in the Bay Area — but only launched the service about eight months prior. The host also notes a significant revelation about Waymo this same week: Waymo confirmed it maintains roughly 70 remote operators on duty at any given time, with half based in the Philippines and half in the U.S. The host argues this shows Waymo still depends on human infrastructure, while Tesla is pursuing true edge autonomy with no remote operators, which it claims will allow superior long-term scaling.

Grok 4.2 announcement (same weekend). Elon announced that Grok 4.2 was "finally" shipping the following week, describing it as a significant improvement over 4.1. The host acknowledges this is technically an XAI announcement, not a Tesla one, but argues the two companies are increasingly intertwined. SpaceX and XAI merged earlier in March 2026. The Colossus data center in Nashville — described as the world's largest AI training cluster — benefits both companies, and neural network expertise flows between Tesla's FSD development and XAI's Grok research. The word "finally" is highlighted as suggesting prior delays, but the fact that it is now shipping signals momentum. Additionally, Tesla's own Cortex 1 and Cortex 2 data centers are in development, with Colossus 2 projected to reach 1 million H100 equivalents.

First Cybercab production unit and $30,000 pricing confirmation (Tuesday post). Elon congratulated the Tesla team on the first production Cybercab rolling off the line at Giga Texas — not a prototype, but an actual production unit with no steering wheel and no pedals, designed from the ground up for full autonomy. He called this historic. The host connects this to a public bet: tech reviewer MKBHD (Marques Brownlee) had challenged in 2024 that Tesla would never pull this off and pledged to shave his head on camera if wrong. When someone on X asked Elon directly whether Tesla would sell a Cybercab for $30,000 or less before 2027, he replied yes. The host treats this as a particularly strong commitment because Elon was responding to a bet challenge, not just a neutral question, suggesting he has genuine confidence in the timeline.

title: Tesla’s Robotaxi Timeline Just Accelerated

facts.text: Tesla has now accumulated 8.2 billion FSD supervised miles, with 10 billion miles cited as Elon Musk's threshold for unsupervised self-driving. The counter jumped 500 million miles in a single day due to recalibrated usage tracking. Analyst "Cern" projects the 10 billion mile mark will be reached by June 22, approximately four months from the recording date of early March 2026. Both hosts note Musk likely intended this as a rough approximation rather than a precise cutoff.

Tesla's supervised FSD is already dramatically safer than human driving by every major metric. The data shows 7 times fewer major collisions, 7 times fewer minor collisions, and 5 times fewer off-highway collisions compared to average U.S. driving. A Tesla running FSD supervised travels 5.3 million miles before a major collision, versus 660,000 miles for the U.S. average. The hosts argue this supervised safety record is a strong predictor of unsupervised performance.

Tesla's Austin robotaxi fleet has logged 14 total NHTSA-reported incidents since launching in June 2025, across 225,000 miles driven in the final two months of 2025. The five most recent incidents (December–January) all occurred at extremely low speeds: 0, 1, 2, 4, and 17 mph. There were zero hospitalizations, zero airbag deployments, and no towing required. One incident was not Tesla's fault at all; two were minor parking lot contacts. NHTSA mandates that all incidents be reported and categorizes them as "crashes" regardless of severity, which the hosts say is being exploited by outlets like Bloomberg for misleading headlines.

FSD supervised usage is skyrocketing, with Tesla owners averaging more than 20 million miles per day in Q1 2026, a new all-time high representing a 30% increase from just a few months prior. This translates to over 13,000 miles per minute. Both hosts attribute this surge to the quality of FSD version 14, which has become smooth enough that even skeptical passengers no longer object to it being engaged.

Larry Goldberg rode both the Tesla robotaxi and Waymo during a recent Austin visit and found the Tesla dramatically smoother. He described the Waymo experience as physically uncomfortable — jittery around corners, inconsistent braking — while the Tesla was smooth enough that on a prior visit he forgot he was in a robotaxi mid-conversation. This echoes Tesla community analyst Omar's observation that riding Waymo now feels like going back to FSD version 10 or 11.

Goldberg's Uber receipts from the Austin trip illustrate the pricing vulnerability Tesla will exploit. Over 32 miles of Uber rides, he paid $177.83 — a cost per mile of $5.44. A 3.2-mile round trip to a barbecue restaurant during peak hours cost $84.94. He argues Uber's entire profit model depends on peak-hour and premium surges above $4 per mile, and Tesla will undercut this substantially. His estimate is Tesla will price robotaxi rides at roughly $2 per mile or less — less than half of comparable Uber pricing — while Tesla's internal cost target is reportedly 25–50 cents per mile.

Tesla's structural advantage over both Uber and Waymo in peak demand is the ability to dynamically pull from its retail owner fleet. When demand spikes, Tesla can offer owners real-time incentives (e.g., $2/mile) to dispatch their personal vehicles autonomously. The car would go to a Tesla facility for cleaning and inspection, complete rides, and return to the owner's home by morning. Waymo cannot scale elastically this way, and Uber depends on human drivers voluntarily coming online for surge pay.

Larry Goldberg predicts that by mid-2026, FSD unsupervised will be demonstrably safer than any human driver, and the robotaxi fleet will be expanding at a "dizzying rate." He adds that the Model Y and Model 3 are essentially already there on safety, while the Cybertruck still needs work. He anticipates the full fleet being "dialed in" by end of June 2026.

Both hosts argue Tesla urgently needs to advertise FSD capabilities to a mainstream audience. Goldberg recounted lunch guests — educated, attentive people — who were completely unaware of FSD's current capabilities. He and the host suggest Tesla's existing video content and community-created footage could form highly effective ads, but Tesla currently limits distribution to X, which they argue only reaches already-converted followers.
"7 times fewer major collisions, 7 times fewer minor collisions, 5 times fewer off-highway collisions" — Tesla FSD supervised vs. average U.S. driving
"5.3 million miles before a major collision" with FSD supervised vs. 660,000 miles for the U.S. average
"$5.44 per mile" — Goldberg's effective Uber cost per mile in Austin over 32 miles, $177.83 total
"$84.94 for a 3.2-mile round trip" to Terry Black's BBQ during peak hours
"An average of 110 people die every day" on U.S. roads from human driving
"32,000 lives could already be saved per year if supervised FSD were universally available"
20 million FSD miles per day in Q1 2026, up 30% in a few months; 13,000+ miles per minute

title: Tesla's Lessons Echo through China - XPeng Follows the Lead

facts.text: XPeng is introducing Extended Range EVs (EREVs) for northern China markets. Originally a pure electric vehicle company, XPeng made the strategic decision to add a combustion engine to some models. Brian explains the reasoning: in northern China, temperatures compromise battery range, and very few consumers have access to home charging. He distinguishes EREVs from plug-in hybrids by framing it clearly — a plug-in hybrid is essentially a gas car with an electric motor added, while an EREV is fundamentally an electric car that uses a gas engine as a range extender, yielding significantly more electric range.

XPeng's factory uses giga castings and a structural battery pack, directly mirroring Tesla's approach. Brian notes that XPeng's front and rear castings are actually larger than those Tesla uses. He frames this as deliberate adoption of proven Tesla manufacturing innovations rather than coincidence. However, he also notes XPeng's software is meaningfully different, and the UI similarity across Chinese EV brands is simply a function of limited ways to present vehicle information clearly.

The assembly line uses a sophisticated dual-robot system for panoramic roof glass installation. One robot laser-scans the exact surface of each individual vehicle, while a second robot applies a precisely calculated amount of adhesive based on that scan. This addresses a historic weak point in automotive manufacturing — panoramic sunroof leaks — by compensating in real time for any stamping irregularities unique to that specific car. Brian had a robotics expert (Jake, formerly of Faraday Future) review the footage, who confirmed the robots are standard ABB units and the line looks entirely conventional and professional.

Brian asked XPeng's CEO a pointed question about Full Self-Driving performance, referencing David Moss's 11,000-mile U.S. drive with zero disengagements. He used this real-world Tesla FSD benchmark to press the CEO on how XPeng's XNGP (referred to as "Eli 2.0" and "BLA 2.0" in context) would compare. The CEO's response was candid: city driving will be sorted out far sooner than rural or cross-country driving, and achieving coast-to-coast (or full-width-of-China) autonomous capability could take up to 5 years due to poor road infrastructure and greater hazards outside major cities. Brian called this "a very bold admission."

XPeng holds a robotaxi permit in Guangzhou and plans to begin operating robotaxis by August 2025, with a confident end-of-year target. They have not yet begun commercial robotaxi operations as of the time of filming, but the permit is already secured. This places them in direct competition with other Chinese autonomous vehicle players racing toward commercialization.

The event celebrated the 100,000th unit of a specific model rolling off the Guangzhou assembly line. Brian was skeptical in the moment about whether the milestone was real or ceremonial marketing, but attended the full CEO presentation, which included real-time English translation via an earpiece fed by a bilingual employee standing at the back of the room with a transmitter — a detail Brian found notable and charming.

XPeng's influencer marketing culture was on prominent display at the product event. Brian described a large semicircle of influencers simultaneously reading from their phones while presenting the car — a sharp contrast to Western automotive press events and a reflection of how Chinese EV brands approach consumer-facing promotion.

title: Why Tesla Cybercab Is ON TRACK to BOOST The Stock

facts.text: First production Cybercabs spotted in Austin, Texas. A community drone operator and Tesla observer known as "Joe Techmire" captured footage of the first serially produced Cybercabs emerging from the factory. The vehicles are identifiable as production units by the stickers on the windows, though they were missing wheel covers at the time of spotting. The speaker treats this as a landmark moment — the first tangible evidence that Cybercab has moved from prototype to actual manufacturing output.

The final production line is complete and live. Tesla confirmed that the first Cybercab produced on the final, completed production line has rolled off. The speaker references commentary from supply chain and production expert Jeff Lutz, who appeared on the channel and described this as the "decisive moment" — meaning the manufacturing infrastructure is no longer in setup mode but is operational and ready to scale.

The "unboxed" manufacturing system could produce up to 5 million units per year. The speaker describes Tesla's revolutionary unboxed production method as capable of producing one vehicle every six seconds, compared to a conventional rate of one every 30 seconds — roughly a 5x improvement in throughput. This single production line, he claims, could theoretically output around 5 million Cybercabs annually, which he argues is the volume that will ultimately be needed.

The ramp-up trajectory runs from now through April 2026. The speaker outlines a stepped production increase: a few units per day currently, scaling to tens, then hundreds per month through March, with the full ramp beginning in April. He notes that the first several hundred units will be used for crash testing, quality inspection, gap and adhesive analysis, and disassembly — standard validation processes — with some also deployed for real-world testing to fine-tune the production feedback loop.

The Cybercab has only one possible purpose: unsupervised robotaxi service. Because the vehicle has no steering wheel, no pedals, and no side mirrors, the speaker argues there is literally no other use case for it. This is not a consumer car that could be sold to individuals as a personal vehicle — it must function as an autonomous robotaxi. He positions this as the key insight the market is missing, framing it as a clash between skeptical Wall Street analysts and the undeniable physical reality of what Tesla is building.

The market is pricing in a much slower rollout than what Tesla is signaling. The speaker contends that Wall Street's current valuation assumes perhaps 1,000 Cybercabs operational by December 2026, whereas the physical evidence — billions invested, a fully live production line, an entire factory workforce dedicated to this — suggests tens of thousands of units, potentially 20,000 to 30,000, could be deployed by year-end. If the higher figure is correct, he argues the stock would move dramatically upward.

Tesla's investment commitment itself signals confidence. The speaker makes a logical argument: Tesla has spent billions of dollars, vast management attention, and enormous human resources building a dedicated manufacturing line for a vehicle that can only serve one purpose. This level of commitment, he says, would be irrational unless Tesla's leadership was certain that autonomous, unsupervised robotaxi operation was achievable at scale in the near term.

A brief question is raised about regulatory risk. A viewer named Shane posed a question in the speaker's community platform about whether the Self-Drive Act and new federal regulations could slow Tesla's scaling ability. The speaker acknowledges this is a legitimate and interesting question he plans to address in a future video, but does not answer it in this video.

title: Tesla and Elon Musk Just Dropped New Announcements

facts.text: CyberCab production vehicles spotted at Giga Texas. Drone footage captured by Joe Techmire showed three CyberCabs outside the Giga Texas facility, confirmed as production versions based on tire stickers and wheel designs matching those used in the production area. The vehicles had no steering wheels and no side mirrors, consistent with the final production configuration, and were observed near the crash testing facility, suggesting they are being prepared for structural and reliability validation ahead of the April start of production.

The pre-SOP manufacturing process explained. Jeff Lutz described the current phase as a convergence period between first-off-production and start of production, during which small "steel safe" changes are made to supplier tooling and factory integration simultaneously. These changes are batched into "recipe" builds — a small number of cars assembled under a defined set of modifications — which are then run through testing to chip away at remaining issues before the line rate is formally increased at SOP.

Voice commands coming to FSD; Grok integration underway. Elon Musk confirmed that FSD will soon accept natural language voice commands, allowing drivers to say things like "find a parking spot near the door" or "turn right at the next block" rather than using manual inputs. Jeff framed this as Tesla potentially being the first automaker to deploy a real large language model integrated into vehicle controls, with Grok already partially integrated; he expects Tesla's implementation to have lower latency and tighter safety guardrails than competitors, preventing commands that would cause unsafe maneuvers at speed.

FSD Supervised already recognizes hand gestures. Both Tesla Europe and Elon Musk confirmed that FSD Supervised responds to hand gestures from people outside the vehicle. A video from the Netherlands showed a CyberCab-era Tesla squeezing through a tight lane as the car responded to a person's hand signal. Jeff noted he has personally observed the system responding to police officers directing traffic, with the car waiting and then proceeding when the officer signals.

Robotaxi fleet at 395 vehicles, 8 unsupervised. The total fleet grew from roughly 390 to 395 over just a few days, with 306 cars operating in the Bay Area and 89 in Austin. The subset of fully unsupervised vehicles grew from 7 to 8, representing 18% of the 44-vehicle active fleet. Jeff noted that for Tesla to meet Elon's earnings call commitment to roughly double the fleet by end of quarter, they would need to reach just over 500 total — meaning a significant push is still required this week.

Why fleet growth is deliberately slow. Jeff explained that Tesla's internal constraint is its own probabilistic safety model: as fleet density increases, the probability of an incident rises unless software capability improves faster than the density. The team is actively crushing edge-case bugs before expanding further. A secondary constraint is operational capacity — charging logistics, vehicle cleaning, and service infrastructure all need to scale in parallel with the number of cars.

Misleading January crash headlines flagged as a reputational risk. Five low-speed incidents in January — all under 4 miles per hour and all parking-related — were reported by media as crashes, with some outlets characterizing Tesla as four times more dangerous than human drivers. Herbert and Jeff argued these were minor contact events, not meaningful safety failures, but expressed concern that the narrative is spreading and could deter riders from using the service. Both called on Tesla's PR team to respond more aggressively, noting that silence risks the misleading headlines becoming accepted public knowledge.

Hiring now spans 14 cities. Tesla posted a new Robotaxi Operations Manager role in Tampa, Florida, and additional AI Safety Operator positions in Vienna, Virginia, bringing the total number of cities where these roles are being filled to 14, up from 12 the prior week. Cities now include locations like Draper, Utah and Peabody, Massachusetts that had not previously been discussed. Jeff noted that many of these roles are full-time, suggesting employees will be transitioned into other service functions once the supervised phase gives way to fully unsupervised operations.

New Cybertruck trim at $59,990 offered for 10 days only. Tesla launched a lower-cost Cybertruck configuration at $59,990, with 325 miles of range, 7,500-pound towing capacity, and rear-wheel steering, but with notable deletions including smaller wheels, no rear screen, no vault storage compartment, and cloth seats. Elon stated the price is only available for 10 days and that continuation depends on demand levels observed during the window.

Jeff's supply chain read on the Cybertruck pricing. Jeff interpreted the 10-day window as likely serving two simultaneous purposes: clearing upstream piece-part inventory (what he called excess and obsolescence management) tied to supplier commitments, and price discovery to determine whether meaningful demand exists at the $60K level. If demand proves strong and sustained, Tesla could recalculate factory fixed-cost absorption and decide to make the price permanent. If not, Jeff speculated Tesla may redirect Cybertruck production toward an autonomous delivery or "robo-truck" use case, given Cybertruck's prior underperformance against sales forecasts at higher price points.

title: The Robotaxi War Is Really About Scale

facts.text: Infrastructure is the decisive competitive moat, not fleet size.
