Waymo has announced 3,000 robotaxis, Zoox has a few hundred, and Tesla has several hundred — but the number of vehicles is a misleading metric. What matters is who can support tens of thousands of vehicles with charging, servicing, and cleaning at scale. Waymo, for instance, must park vehicles in public lots where they are vulnerable to damage, subcontracts charging infrastructure, and relies on third-party partners for maintenance — all of which adds cost and friction.

Tesla received an FCC waiver for ultra-wideband wireless EV charging positioning technology. The FCC granted Tesla an exception to standard rules that prohibit ultra-wideband radio from being installed outdoors on fixed equipment. The system uses a radio transceiver in the vehicle and one embedded in a ground-level charging pad to guide the Cybercab into a precise position over the charger — critical for maximizing charging rate and minimizing time on the charger. The signal is low-power, activates only briefly while parked, and operates at very short range, which satisfied FCC interference concerns. Larry speculates this regulatory delay may have been one reason wireless charging rollout has been slow.

Wireless charging precision is a fleet operations requirement, not a convenience feature. Unlike consumer phones where imprecise pad placement only adds a few minutes, a robotaxi fleet requires exact positioning to achieve maximum charge rates and rapid vehicle turnover so the next car can immediately take the charger's spot. This automation of the charging process — removing any human intervention — is described as a "game changer" for fleet operations, particularly during peak demand hours when surge capacity is critical.

Tesla's infrastructure advantage is structural and multi-layered. Tesla already has service centers in both major and minor cities across the United States, the world's largest supercharging network, and purpose-built vehicles designed for minimal servicing. Larry argues Tesla actually needs less infrastructure than competitors because its vehicles are more self-contained. Adding wireless charging pads to existing supercharger stations is expected to be relatively straightforward, leveraging the same deployment capability that built out the supercharger network at a pace nobody thought possible.

Uber's $100 million investment in autonomous charging hubs signals it is in catch-up mode and faces structural challenges. Uber announced it will build DC fast charging stations at its autonomous depots and pit stops throughout cities, partnering with more than 20 charging operators including EVgo (New York, Los Angeles, San Francisco, Boston), Electra (Paris, Madrid), and Ionity (London). Larry's critique: at even $50,000 per charging station, $100 million only buys roughly 2,000 units. More critically, Uber's existing business relies on owner-drivers for roughly 80% or more of its logistics — a model that disappears entirely in full autonomy. Reinventing that entire operational stack through 50 different partners is viewed as unworkable.

ARK Invest research pegs the US robotaxi market at $3.9 trillion annually at equilibrium, or $2.8 trillion even if users never give up their personal vehicles. ARK analyst Brett Winton's work shows that at $3 per mile (roughly current Uber pricing), the total addressable market is under $500 billion. As price per mile drops to $1.50, the TAM reaches approximately $1 trillion. At $0.50 per mile, the TAM approaches $3 trillion. ARK's research indicates Tesla can earn a solid profit at $0.50 per mile, creating a 6x market expansion versus today's ride-hail pricing.

The market opportunity extends beyond ride-hail into "substitution value" — the economic cost of time spent driving. Americans spend $1.6 trillion annually on direct driving costs, but ARK calculates they devote an additional $4.4 trillion worth of productive labor time to the manual task of piloting vehicles — a total effective cost of approximately $6 trillion per year. This time cost is currently invisible in GDP accounting. As robotaxi prices fall to $2 per mile or below, people who already own cars will increasingly choose to be driven rather than drive, not to save money on transport but to recapture productive work time.

High-income earners are the primary target market and paradoxically are the heaviest manual drivers. ARK's income decile analysis shows that the wealthiest workers — executives, doctors, professionals — both do the most driving and have the highest willingness to pay for robotaxi service because their time carries the greatest economic value. Larry notes that currently only the very wealthy hire drivers, but robotaxi could democratize this from middle management upward. The willingness-to-pay clearing price scales from fractions of a cent per mile at the lowest income decile to roughly $2.40 per mile at the top decile.

Macroeconomic implications are significant but currently unrecognized. The $4.3 trillion in labor cost absorbed by manual driving is invisible to GDP accounting. Freeing that time for productive work would add measurably to national economic output. The hosts note that New York's governor has moved to pause robotaxi rollout, likely due to pressure from taxi driver unions, and argue that politicians will need to weigh this productivity data against labor concerns.
US robotaxi TAM at equilibrium: $3.9 trillion annually; $2.8 trillion even without car ownership changes
Americans spend $1.6 trillion annually on direct driving costs; $4.4 trillion in forfeited labor time — total $6 trillion effective cost
Uber's average revenue: approximately $4 per mile; profit is concentrated in the $8+ per mile rides
ARK finds Tesla can profit at $0.50 per mile
TAM at $3/mile: under $500 billion; at $0.50/mile: approximately $3 trillion — a 6x expansion
Uber's $100 million charging investment: at $50,000 per station, yields roughly 2,000 charging units
On Uber's 50-partner strategy: "You do not reinvent things with 50 different partners."

title: INSANE used prices, Cybertrucks on Screen, MKBHD Beef & MORE

facts.text: Tesla Model Y L (longer wheelbase, six-seat variant) is expanding globally after Australia certification. Tesla launched the YL in China in August 2025, where it was well-received. It has now been officially listed in Australia's automotive regulator database under the designation Y L5NDB, serviced by the Shanghai factory. Brian personally reviewed the car in China the prior month and noted the 2+2 configuration is more comfortable than the standard 7-seat Model Y layout. A European launch also seems likely given that the YL was spotted testing at the Nurburgring and seen at Giga Berlin. Brian believes a US launch from the Texas factory is coming by late 2026, and hints at having some basis for that confidence beyond public information.

Tesla used car prices are rising while other EVs depreciate sharply. Brian debunks the persistent narrative that Tesla has terrible resale value, tracing it to the pandemic-era pricing distortion when dealers were marking up vehicles like a $35,000 Toyota to $40,000-$45,000 over MSRP. When the $7,500 federal EV tax credit disappeared, it lifted the floor on used Tesla prices because new Teslas became comparatively more expensive, while other EVs that previously benefited from the credit saw steeper used-market drops. As a concrete example from the used market aggregator Plat Auto, a 2025 Equinox EV with only 1,600 miles was listed at $26,000, representing brutal depreciation. By contrast, a refreshed 2024 Model Y with 15,000 miles was listed at $35,000, which Brian called a strong deal.

The MKBHD-Tesla "beef" is overblown media hype. Marques Brownlee (MKBHD, 20.8 million YouTube subscribers) mentioned in his new Model Y Performance review that Tesla no longer proactively sends him vehicles, and he had to source the car himself from a local dealership. Brian explains this is simply because Tesla has no standing PR department — when they have a product to push, they push it; otherwise, there's no one making those calls. He notes Tesla had previously given Brownlee early Cybertruck access and that Brownlee called the Model S Plaid the best car of the decade, so there's no acrimony. Brownlee also sold his Cybertruck (he had an R1T already) and cancelled one of his two Roadster reservations, keeping one.

Uber's robotaxi strategy is more about platform than technology. The Uber CEO claimed Uber will handle more EV trips before the end of the decade than Tesla, and the company launched a new suite of services to let autonomous vehicle developers plug into its ride-hailing network. Brian is skeptical, noting Uber previously had its own AV project that it sold off. The actual pitch is essentially: "If you build a self-driving car, bring it to us." The realistic current partner is Waymo. Brian also corrects a common misconception, noting that Nvidia's Alamo (Drive) is a tool for building AV systems, not an autonomous driving system itself, contrary to speculation that legacy automakers like Ford could simply plug it in for FSD-equivalent capability.

Ford shut down its $5.8 billion Kentucky battery factory just months after opening, with 1,600 workers losing jobs. Brian frames this as partly a self-inflicted wound, noting Ford spent two decades telling customers EVs wouldn't work, then was surprised customers didn't buy their EVs. Ford's secretive next-generation EV pickup is rumored to feature 48V architecture, a structural battery pack, front and rear castings, and a small footprint similar to a Cybertruck — but has no release date and almost no public information. Brian is skeptical of Ford's broader EV commitment.

California's CARB voucher program for electric Class 8 semis is drawing competitor complaints. California earmarked $165 million in vouchers supporting Tesla Semi purchases, with the standard voucher worth $120,000 off the sticker price — enough to bring the Tesla Semi near price parity with diesel trucks on day one. Critics from competing EV manufacturers claim Tesla received preferential treatment and hasn't met certification standards. Brian dismisses this, noting the certification is about clean air compliance, which an all-electric vehicle automatically satisfies. He points out hydrogen trucks receive double the subsidy ($240,000+, up to $420,000 in pilot programs), which he notes was essentially Nikola's entire business model.

JD Power's 2026 EV Experience Ownership Study found 96% of current EV owners would consider another EV for their next vehicle. Brian attributes this largely to the fact that even mediocre EVs offer a fundamentally better drivetrain experience than equivalent gas vehicles, illustrating the point with his family's test drive of a Chevy Blazer EV — not a great car, but better than the gas Blazer it replaces.

Robo taxi accident statistics comparing AVs to human drivers are misleading. Brian has addressed this in multiple videos: the higher reported accident rate for robotaxis includes incidents that humans would never report, such as 1 mph parking lot contact with no damage, which AVs are legally required to log. It also includes not-at-fault incidents such as a bus striking a stationary vehicle.
96% of current EV owners would consider another EV (JD Power 2026 study)
Tesla Semi CARB voucher: standard $120,000 off sticker; up to $420,000 in pilot/small business programs; hydrogen trucks get double the base subsidy
2025 Equinox EV with 1,600 miles listed used at $26,000; 2024 Model Y with 15,000 miles at $35,000
MKBHD has 20.8 million YouTube subscribers
Ford's shuttered Kentucky factory cost $5.8 billion and employed 1,600 workers
Brian jokes that hitting 100,000 subscribers will earn viewers a "baby seal clubs me" moment

title: Why is Tesla Stock CRASHING Again?

facts.text: The selloff is tariff-driven noise, not a fundamental shift. The NASDAQ fell 1.13% and the Dow Jones fell 1.66% on the day. Tesla dropped up to 4.6% at its lowest point. Joe characterizes Trump's tariff announcements as "hot air" that the market is slowly learning to discount, noting the European Union and broader markets are also reacting with what he considers excessive fear.

Tesla has already absorbed significant downside from its peak. The stock fell roughly 25% from approximately $490 to under $400 since late 2024. Joe argues this was foreseeable — he claims to have warned viewers back in December about Q1 weakness and the "air gap" between the Robotaxi Day event and actual large-scale cybercab deployment. Because so much damage has already been done, he questions whether a dramatically worse leg down is likely.

He is short-term cautious but expects a rally once April catalysts materialize. Joe expresses concern about the remaining weeks of March, saying Tesla could dip below $400 again before recovering. However, he believes the key catalyst — cybercab deployment at hundreds of units, necessarily unsupervised because the vehicles have no steering wheels — will begin at meaningful scale around end of April. Once that happens, he predicts Tesla could reach $500 very quickly and potentially $600 shortly after, given the stock's historical pattern of sudden explosive moves after sideways consolidation.

The robotaxi tracker is showing incremental but real progress. At time of recording, the tracker showed 396 cybercabs with 8 unsupervised units, up from 7 the previous day. Joe acknowledges the tracker is imperfect — not all vehicles listed may be active, and the total fleet size could be larger or smaller than indicated — but defends it as the best available approximation of progress. He had predicted Tesla would hit 400 robotaxis that day and viewed the 396 figure as close confirmation.

Pioneer Alpha fund performance update. For the prior week, the fund was down only 0.17% versus Tesla down 1.35%, generating approximately 1.18% alpha. Year to date, the fund is down 2% while Tesla is down 8.43%, representing 6.31% alpha over Tesla. Joe notes that over a 6-month and 1-year horizon, Pioneer Alpha has dramatically outperformed even the NASDAQ, citing 84% cumulative alpha over the NASDAQ across longer timeframes. The month-to-date picture is hurt by a Robinhood position that fell 10% after earnings, though Joe says he does not regret that position.

Pioneer Alpha is now open to qualified investors internationally. Previously limited, the fund has expanded eligibility to Canada, the United Kingdom, the European Union, Switzerland, and the United States. Investors must complete a qualification process specific to their region. Joe clarifies the structure is bonds rather than an open fund, with a close expected before April. He emphasizes he cannot publicly discuss terms for non-US regions but can allow qualified investors to see them after completing eligibility verification at pioneeralpha.org.

Two new channel themes are being launched with distinct visual branding. "Pioneer Lands" (brownish/gold thumbnails) will cover building future civilization, technology, and society. A third theme in magenta will cover politics, including Germany, Europe, tariffs, Trump, and UK affairs. This segmentation is designed to help viewers self-select content and avoid frustration when investment content mixes with broader civilization or political topics.

The broader AGI context underpins his long-term bullishness. Joe argues we are in an age of AGI where productivity is surging and the "Magnificent 7" companies are seeing revenue and profitability climb. He dismisses talk of an AGI bubble, pointing out the market has been trying to sell off since November without success. He plans dedicated videos on what he calls the potentially serious societal consequences of AGI, while simultaneously arguing this is the force preventing a sustained market decline.
Tesla drop from peak: "490 to under 400... we have dropped 25%"
Year-to-date alpha: "We are up 6.31% over Tesla. Tesla is down 8.43% since January 1st. We are only down 2%."
Long-term benchmark comparison: "84% alpha" over the NASDAQ across longer timeframes
Price targets on a breakout: "We go to 500 in no time and probably to 600 in absolutely no time"
On Tesla's price behavior: "Tesla is a stock that goes sideways and sideways forever... and when it happens, you add 50 or 100% in super short periods of time with total FOMO"
Robotaxi tracker at time of recording: 396 total, 8 unsupervised units

title: They’re Trying To Stop Tesla Now

facts.text: Cyber Cab spotted on public roads in Palo Alto, California for the first time, with no steering wheel or side mirrors — previously sightings had been limited to Giga Texas.

Influencer Joe Techmire received a private tour of Giga Texas and confirmed hundreds of Cyber Cabs inside the factory, that production is already underway, and that the "unboxed" manufacturing method (five main structural components assembled separately) is being used.
The robotaxi tracker shows approximately 31 Cyber Cabs in deployment and 8 unsupervised units, with the count on a steep upward trajectory. Volume production is targeted for April 2026.

Joe Bacti argues that Cyber Cab production itself is not the bottleneck — the real question is whether Tesla's FSD software is ready for unsupervised deployment at scale. He outlines three investor scenarios: smooth April launch, dramatic delay followed by eventual deployment with a stock dip to around $200 before a major recovery, or a deeply depressing long-term delay.

Waymo submitted a proposal to California's DMV to ban Tesla from using the words "driverless," "self-driving," or "robo taxi" in marketing. This follows a December ruling that previously forced Tesla to drop "autopilot" and "full self-driving" terminology. Tesla simply changed its marketing language to comply and has now filed a lawsuit against the California DMV to reverse the original ruling.

A narrative claiming Tesla has four times more crashes than human drivers circulated widely, based on five January incidents. The hosts dispute this characterization, noting all five were minor fender-benders at under 4 mph, one involving a stationary Tesla struck by a bus.

Uber announced "Uber Autonomous Solutions," a comprehensive service suite for AV partners including training data collection via a Lucid vehicle fleet, data-enriched mapping, regulatory support, fleet financing, and remote operations. The hosts view this as a desperate strategy entirely dependent on Tesla failing, as Tesla robotaxi scaling would likely devastate Uber's business model.
Tesla is hiring Optimus motor and actuator design engineers in Athens, Greece, signaling continued Optimus robot development expansion.

The Cybertruck at a new $59,000 price point sold out through September within roughly one day of announcement, pushing new order delivery estimates out three to four months. This is interpreted as a demand elasticity test, with the possibility of future cost reductions using 4680 batteries enabling further price cuts.

title: Tesla’s Smartest Expansion Yet

facts.text: The Tesla Model Y L has been certified for sale in Australia. A user discovered the certification, which is significant because Australia is served by Tesla's Shanghai factory, making export straightforward. This certification is treated as a strong signal that global expansion is beginning, with Europe and eventually the US likely to follow.

The Model Y L has been spotted testing at the Nürburgring and at Tesla's Berlin Gigafactory location. This suggests Tesla may be planning to manufacture the Y L in Berlin rather than solely shipping it from China, which would make European distribution more efficient and cost-effective at scale.

Brian argues the cancellation of the Model X creates a gap in Tesla's lineup that the Y L must fill. With no three-row Tesla option remaining after the Model X was discontinued, there is both a product gap and a customer expectation gap that the Y L is uniquely positioned to address, particularly since it's already a proven, designed platform requiring minimal additional tooling.

Randy and Brian both expect the Y L to be produced at Tesla's Texas Gigafactory, which has surplus capacity. Brian favors the Y L over a Cyber SUV for this role, citing the difficulty of manufacturing stainless steel vehicles at scale and the Y L's manufacturing readiness — the retooling during recent factory downtime may have already prepared the line.

The Cybertruck has underperformed relative to original production projections of 250,000 units per year, currently selling around 20,000 units annually. However, both speakers see a path forward if the price drops to around $60,000 — a level that would unlock commercial buyers for whom the $80,000 price point was prohibitive. The 4680 battery cost reductions may make this feasible.

Brian makes a strong argument that car size preferences are driven by price, not cultural need. He cites the European example where the Model Y outsells the Model 3 despite conventional wisdom that Europeans prefer smaller cars. His thesis: people don't want small cars, they want affordable ones, and when a big car is priced like a small car, buyers choose the big car.

The average new car price in the United States is now approximately $50,000. This reframes Tesla pricing as mainstream rather than luxury, with Randy noting the Model Y L at that price point would be competitive and accessible to the average American buyer financing at roughly $750–$1,000 per month.

Three strategies are identified to improve Tesla's US sales: the new lower-cost standard versions of the Model Y and Model 3 (with the IRA tax credit gone), the lower-priced Cybertruck variant, and the potential launch of the Y L. Randy ranks the Y L highest in terms of sales impact — particularly because of its showroom upsell potential — followed by the Cybertruck if Tesla ever invests in advertising.

Tesla's lack of advertising is identified as a major missed opportunity for the Cybertruck. Randy suggests an ad campaign highlighting specific standout features — steer-by-wire, 48-volt architecture, bed outlets, tonneau cover, ride quietness — using existing reviewer quotes or footage. Both agree this would not be a difficult or expensive campaign to execute.

The EV total cost of ownership argument is made with personal anecdotes. Brian notes 35,000 miles on one Tesla and 13,000 on another with zero maintenance beyond washer fluid. Randy references a Nissan Leaf owner at an auto show with 130,000 miles and brakes still at 50% capacity. The point is that reduced maintenance costs and time savings compound significantly over a vehicle's lifespan, especially as modern cars can now last 20 years.

title: FSD Momentum Just Went Global

facts.text: NHTSA public meeting on automated vehicle safety, March 10, 2026. The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration held an all-day public meeting (9 a.m. to 4:30 p.m.) at US DOT headquarters to provide updates on ongoing and upcoming vehicle automation activities. This builds on an ADS workshop held November 20th, and the hosts view it as a potential regulatory turning point. There is a current 2,500-vehicle exemption cap for autonomous vehicles, though additional exemptions are possible — a process that was streamlined last June — and the meeting could yield clearer guidance on future rules.

Elon Musk confirms Cybercab will be sold to consumers in 2026. Musk confirmed in response to skepticism from tech reviewer MKBHD (Marques Brownlee) that the Cybercab will be delivered at under $30,000 before 2027. The hosts debate what "sold" means — Brian argues that even a single sale to a fleet manager would technically count — and separately, journalist Joe Tegmire reportedly observed hundreds of Cybercabs already parked inside the assembly facility, suggesting production is well underway.

Tesla's updated safety report shows a widening gap between AI and human drivers. Tesla's global FSD fleet has surpassed 8.2 billion miles driven. The crash data is segmented into four categories: FSD supervised (1 crash per 5 million miles), Tesla driven manually with active safety (1 crash per 2 million miles), Tesla driven manually without active safety (1 crash per 855,000 miles), and the US national average (1 crash per 600,000 miles). The definition of "crash" is strict — requiring airbag deployment, seatbelt pyrotechnic activation, or high-voltage battery pack fuse triggering — making the comparison meaningful and conservative.

Xiaopeng secures Volkswagen as first commercial customer for XIMO 2.0 smart driving. Xiaopeng's second-generation vision-language-action smart driving system (VA 2.0) has been licensed to Volkswagen, marking the first large-scale export of this core Chinese autonomous driving technology. VW had been working with Xiaopeng since 2023 and also has a separate software partnership with Rivian (for North America and parts of Europe). Mark had already seen VA 2.0 in beta and was scheduled to evaluate the consumer release version the following week. Importantly, like Tesla's FSD, Xiaopeng dropped LiDAR and runs on vision-only — a validation of that architectural direction.

BMW removing Level 3 autonomy from the refreshed 7 Series. BMW is eliminating its Level 3 autonomous driving feature from its flagship sedan, following a similar retreat by Mercedes, which temporarily scrapped its eyes-off Level 3 system because almost no one was buying it. The hosts explain that both automakers attempted to handcode narrow edge-case scenarios — such as Mercedes requiring a car ahead, speeds limited to 35-40 mph on highways posted at 60-80 mph, daytime only, and no sun glare — and while those cases may have worked reliably, the on-board hardware cannot scale to broader use cases like lane changes or urban driving. The fundamental issue is that off-the-shelf hardware plugged into vehicles cannot handle the "march of nines" (ever-increasing reliability thresholds) that true autonomy demands.

FSD subscription terms allow Tesla to change pricing and features at any time. A news item generated concern that Tesla's FSD subscription terms include standard language permitting price and feature changes. The hosts dismiss this as entirely normal for any subscription product, comparing it to Netflix's terms of service, and note it only becomes a concern if the agreement explicitly promises a fixed price for life.
Tesla FSD supervised: 1 crash per 5 million miles vs. US average of 1 crash per 600,000 miles — roughly an 8x improvement.
Tesla's global FSD fleet has surpassed 8.2 billion miles (compared to Voyager 2's total travel of 13 billion miles for scale).
Cybercab confirmed at under $30,000 before 2027, per Musk's direct reply to MKBHD.
Xiaopeng targets 1 million overseas EV sales by 2030.
Current US autonomous vehicle exemption cap: 2,500 units, with additional exemptions available through a streamlined process updated in June 2025.

title: Why 2,000 Robotaxis DRIVE Tesla Stock To The MOON

facts.text: The creator originally identified 1,800 fully deployed commercial robotaxis as the inflection point for Tesla's stock, now updated to approximately 2,000 after revising some assumptions.

The core revenue assumptions are $1.60–$2.00 per mile in revenue (not the widely cited $1/mile), supported by real-world data showing Tesla currently charges approximately $1.99 per kilometer (~$2.50/mile) in San Francisco, with average fares around $8.17 per ride versus competitors' ~$11.
Operating cost (COGS) is assumed at $0.50 per mile, producing a margin of $1.10–$1.50 per mile. Each robotaxi is modeled driving 200 miles/day for 26 days/month.

Under these assumptions, a single robotaxi generates approximately $5,720–$7,800 in monthly margin, or roughly $69,000–$93,000 annually — equivalent to the margin from selling 12 Model Y vehicles every year, repeating indefinitely.

The original 5% free cash flow benchmark was compared against 2024 Tesla free cash flow of $3.58 billion, not 2025's $6.2 billion — a key clarification correcting the community member's math. At 2,000 robotaxis, the model produces approximately 5.23% of 2024 free cash flow.

Scaling projections show dramatic impact: 20,000 robotaxis by December 2026 adds ~50% to Tesla's annual free cash flow; 200,000 robotaxis in 2027 would increase free cash flow by 522%, reaching ~$18.7 billion from robotaxi alone; 500,000 units in 2028 could add ~$50 billion in free cash flow, a ~1,300% increase.

Waymo is dismissed as unable to scale and therefore not a real competitor. Uber is identified as the primary threat being displaced, but Uber cannot lower prices ~30% without going bankrupt since it must pay drivers.
The inflection point prediction of April–May 2026 was made roughly a year before this video, and the creator notes it remains plausible given current circumstances.

title: Tesla Expands as Rivals Go All In

facts.text: NHTSA has announced a major autonomous vehicle safety public meeting on March 10th at the US Department of Transportation headquarters. The full-day event will include keynote addresses from DOT leadership, three executive panel discussions, and workshops on potential federal frameworks for safe AV development, testing, and deployment.

Tesla is currently operating robotaxis in the San Francisco Bay Area (with safety drivers) and Austin (approximately 8 unsupervised vehicles). The company plans to expand to seven additional cities before July, with validation vehicles spotted in Miami and multiple other regions nationwide. Tesla holds explicit regulatory approval in California, Texas, Arizona, Nevada, Florida, and Georgia.

Waymo now operates in 10 US cities after simultaneously launching invite-only service in Dallas, Houston, San Antonio, and Orlando — the first time the company opened multiple cities at once. Waymo has roughly 3,000 vehicles but faces a structural business model problem: analysts at New Street Research note Waymo loses money on each car beyond approximately 500 per city, limiting its ability to scale within individual markets. Waymo has raised $16 billion but remains unprofitable. Future expansion targets include Denver, London, and Washington DC.

Waymo's operational inefficiencies are noted, including all vehicles returning to base simultaneously at end of day rather than trickling back based on demand patterns, suggesting a lack of network-level intelligence.

Wave AI, a UK-based startup founded in 2017, raised $1.2 billion (some figures cited as $1.8 billion in the discussion) from Nvidia, Uber, Nissan, Mercedes-Benz, and Stellantis. Wave uses an end-to-end neural network approach similar to Tesla's and has tested in over 500 cities across Europe, Asia, and North America, integrating into 10 different vehicle types.

Wave's business model is distinctly different: it plans to license its AI software to automakers and fleets rather than operate its own robotaxi service or sell vehicles. This positions Wave as a non-threatening supplier for automakers who are reluctant to license from Tesla or Waymo, both seen as potential competitors.
A parallel development in China: Xpeng (Xiaopeng) has been announced as the FSD software provider for Volkswagen, and Huawei's system is already licensed by multiple Chinese automakers.

Mercedes-Benz's Level 3 autonomy feature was discussed as an example of marketing over substance — restricted to straight highways, speeds under 40 mph, daytime, and requiring a lead vehicle. Mercedes has since withdrawn the feature.

title: This Is Why Tesla Set a Deadline on CYBERTRUCK Price

facts.text: The $59,990 price represents what was originally promised. When the Cybertruck was first unveiled, the dual motor variant was slated for $49,990. Adjusted for inflation — particularly accounting for the unexpectedly high inflation of 2021 and subsequent years — that original price translates to approximately $63,000 today. At $59,990, the hosts argue buyers are actually getting a better deal than originally promised.

The current package outperforms original specifications in several ways. The original promise was 300 miles of range; this model delivers 325 miles. Additional features not originally promised include a rear interior screen, rear-wheel steering (described as an expensive add-on), and high-quality coilover springs. The only promised feature missing is air suspension. Both hosts agree this is a well-balanced package that avoids the mistake of the stripped-down $70,000 version introduced last year, which they describe as a "crash and burn" due to too many feature removals.

Demand responded immediately and strongly. Delivery estimates shifted from June/July to September/October within days of the announcement, indicating a surge in orders. Brian notes anecdotal evidence from social media showing buyers quickly filling referral codes. Referral code users receive free Full Self-Driving (FSD) for one to three months, though the $1,000 discount reportedly attached to some codes was not widely advertised.

Three plausible reasons are offered for the 10-day deadline structure. First, Tesla may have had supplier commitments based on projected sales volumes that fell short, requiring a demand boost to fulfill those obligations. Second, Elon Musk himself framed it as a market test — a deliberate price elasticity experiment to gauge demand at this level. Third, analyst Jeff Watts suggested it may reflect a need to move existing inventory or pre-built units. Herbert leans toward the market-testing explanation as the most credible.

The price is expected to increase only modestly after the deadline. Herbert predicts a $3,000 increase, framing the 10-day window as price discovery — Tesla identifying the optimal price point that sustains both volume and margin. Brian estimates $3,000–$5,000 as a reasonable range. Both agree that jumping back to $70,000 would reintroduce the same problem as last year, effectively cutting off the newly-engaged buyer segment.

The early high-priced Cybertruck phase was a deliberate technology testbed, not a failure. Herbert argues the $100,000–$125,000 Cybertruck served a specific strategic purpose: validating new technologies including steer-by-wire, 48-volt architecture, and the unboxed manufacturing model — all of which are foundational to Tesla's robotaxi and autonomous vehicle ambitions. Testing these systems on a low-volume, premium-priced vehicle minimized risk before deploying them at mass scale.

Tesla's discontinuation of the Model S and Model X reflects a broader strategic pivot toward autonomy. Both hosts connect this to Tesla's stated direction: the Roadster has been described by Elon Musk and Lars Moravy as potentially the last "drivable" car Tesla will build, with future focus on fully autonomous platforms including the Cyber Cab, a Robovan, and other autonomous form factors. The S and X are characterized as low-volume, high-cost legacy products inconsistent with this direction.

A Model Y Long (Y L) is viewed as far more likely than a new full-size SUV for the US market. The Y L is already being produced in Europe and China and has received regulatory approval in Australia. The hosts dismiss speculation about a "Cyber Van" or other new SUV form factors as requiring entirely new engineering that Tesla is unlikely to prioritize. They expect Tesla will not announce the Y L for the US until deliveries are imminent, to avoid cannibalizing existing Model Y sales.

Cybertruck sales data will remain difficult to track. Tesla lumps Cybertruck, Cyber Cab, and Semi under "other models" in delivery reports. Cox Automotive quarterly data can provide rough estimates for Cybertruck, while Semi numbers can potentially be reconstructed through state vehicle registration databases. Cyber Cab will be the most opaque, as many units will be retained in-house for Tesla's own robotaxi fleet.
Original dual motor price at announcement: $49,990; inflation-adjusted equivalent today: approximately $63,000
Early Cybertruck pricing range: $100,000–$125,000
Current price: $59,990; delivery window pushed from June/July to September/October within days of announcement
Average new pickup truck price cited: over $60,000, making the Cybertruck below average for the segment
Tesla's internal sales target referenced: 125,000 units per year described as the "bottom end" of their expected range, a figure they are currently well short of

title: JUST RECORDED: Elon Musk Drops News About CyberCab, FSD and Optimus

facts.text: Full Self-Driving (FSD) Europe Expansion: Musk stated FSD is expected to receive approval in the Netherlands around March 20th. He predicted that technically, by the end of this year, drivers will be able to "fall asleep in a Tesla and wake up at your destination."

CyberCab Production Timeline: Production has already started at Giga Texas. Volume production begins in April, with significant production output expected by the end of the year. Musk indicated potential European manufacturing of the CyberCab if things go well.

Optimus Robot: Musk described Optimus as the first truly useful humanoid robot, noting the hands are among the hardest engineering challenges. Near-term tasks will be simple, gradually becoming more complex. Long-term, he envisions Optimus performing surgery and providing world-class medical care to everyone globally. He also predicted work will become "optional" within roughly 10 years due to AI and robotics.

Five Major Production Lines in 2025: Tesla has five factories ramping to volume production this year, including battery cell production at Giga Berlin. Tesla's lithium refinery and nickel cathode refinery have both started up in Austin, Texas.

Giga Berlin Vision: Musk expressed ambition to expand Giga Berlin into potentially the largest factory complex in Europe, adding battery cell production, and eventually manufacturing CyberCab, Optimus, and the Tesla Semi. He cautioned that expansion depends on supportive local authorities, implying opposition from outside organizations could limit growth.

Legacy Automakers: Musk was blunt, comparing non-electric, non-autonomous vehicles to "riding a horse and using a flip phone." He said legacy automakers have strategically headed "in the direction of the dinosaurs" by resisting electrification and autonomy.
Tesla Semi: Expected to reach Europe "hopefully next year."
Advice for Young People: Be optimistic, read widely, try many things, and find work that provides genuine satisfaction.

title: Tesla’s Path to $5 TRILLION Market Cap

facts.text: Path to $5 trillion: Matt's financial model suggests Tesla needs roughly $16 earnings per share with a ~75x multiple to reach $5 trillion, which his model projects around 2029. However, earlier milestone achievements — particularly Optimus and robotaxi scale — could accelerate that timeline. Tesla's current ~200 P/E reflects anticipated future cash flows from autonomous travel, not its existing car business generating roughly $2 EPS in 2026.

Cybercab production on track: Joe Techmeer (drone journalist) toured the Giga Texas facility and confirmed Cybercab uses the "unboxed" manufacturing method, with approximately 100 units already inside in various stages. Jeff interprets this as consistent with a pilot phase typically 6-8 weeks before start of production, suggesting an April production ramp remains on schedule.

Robotaxi scale bottlenecks: Tesla currently has approximately 400-500 robotaxi vehicles across Austin and the Bay Area. The panel identifies two likely constraints: improving FSD software to statistically minimize accident probability before expanding the fleet, and operational infrastructure (staffing, charging logistics) needed before expanding to new cities. Tesla has stated a goal of nearly a dozen cities in the first half of 2025.

Regulation outlook: NHTSA announced a major public event on March 10 at the U.S. Department of Transportation headquarters to discuss a framework for autonomous vehicle deployment. Panelists see this as a positive step but note a federal bill remains uncertain. Elon Musk previously suggested regulation would keep pace with Tesla's deployment schedule, implying he expects a state-by-state rollout rather than a federal framework. Separately, Elon announced FSD supervised will be approved in the Netherlands around March 20, marking Tesla's European FSD entry.

Bear case benchmarks for robotaxi: A third-party analyst (Jenma JP) identified four bearish triggers: two serious Tesla-at-fault accidents; no Cybercab in customer service by end of April; fewer than 100 unsupervised robotaxis by end of May; and no new city deployments by summer. Panelists largely view these as overly short-term, though Jeff emphasizes Tesla needs to start hitting near-term commitments for credibility.

Cybertruck $59,950 variant: Tesla ran a 10-day test of a lower-priced Cybertruck. Delivery estimates rapidly moved from June-July to September-October, then to April 2027, suggesting either surging demand or supply constraints (possibly tied to 4680 battery production readiness). The panel notes original expectations of 500,000 units annually at sub-$50,000 were far too optimistic; current volumes are roughly comparable to Model S/X levels.

Public image problem: Five January robotaxi incidents — all low-speed parking lot fender-benders under 4 mph — generated global negative headlines. Panelists argue Tesla needs proactive PR, potentially including advertising that frames autonomous driving as a moral issue given 43,000 annual U.S. traffic deaths and 150,000 serious injuries. Elon's current approach of ignoring critics is seen as insufficient.

Optimus: Jeff sees no competitor with Tesla's combination of manufacturing capability, internal AI, and a competitive product. Matt believes Optimus won't be economically useful (cheaper than human labor in real factory conditions) until approximately 2027. Elon recently indicated verbal training commands will be possible, which panelists flag as a significant capability differentiator.

title: One Decision Could Change Tesla’s Europe Plan

facts.text: The Cybertruck is technically impossible to buy officially in the EU, but Norton successfully imported and registered one in Prague. To comply with EU regulations, the vehicle required changes including converting rear lights from red to amber, repositioning lights to meet placement rules, adding a fog light, and covering or rounding sharp edges to meet pedestrian safety standards. Norton notes more Cybertrucks are slowly making their way to Europe through similar case-by-case processes.

Tesla is unlikely to ever officially sell the Cybertruck in Europe through standard manufacturer channels. Norton and Brian both agree that the extent of modifications required, combined with the European market's general disinterest in large trucks, makes it economically and logistically impractical. Elon Musk himself reportedly doesn't want to invest the effort given that even US Cybertruck sales face challenges.

FSD supervised mode is on the verge of regulatory approval in the Netherlands, likely by April 2026. The Dutch vehicle certification authority, the RDW, was originally expected to approve FSD supervised in February 2026. That deadline passed, and March approval is also looking unlikely. Norton's contacts within the Tesla Owners Netherlands community suggest April is the realistic window for an actual software update enabling supervised FSD for existing Dutch owners who have already purchased the feature.

FSD in Europe is specifically classified as Level 2 autonomy, meaning the driver remains responsible. Norton clarifies the important regulatory distinction: Level 3 autonomy (eyes-off in designated areas) requires the manufacturer to assume legal responsibility, and BMW is already pursuing Level 3 certification in Europe. Ford's BlueCruise is also reportedly undergoing the process. Tesla's FSD supervised does not claim Level 3 status, keeping liability with the driver, which is a key reason why its regulatory path differs from competitors.

Czech Republic and Germany have already passed laws allowing certified Level 3 systems on highways. This creates a regulatory framework that is gradually opening up, but certification is mandatory and manufacturer liability is the central sticking point that determines which systems qualify.

FSD ride-alongs are currently booming across Europe, with demo sessions in countries including the Czech Republic booking up completely and being extended. Norton is personally attending a ride-along the following day. Feedback from Dutch participants is that FSD drives reliably and safely, handling construction zones, pedestrians, and cyclists correctly, though it is described as "boring" because it simply does what it is supposed to do without drama. The system is still being tuned for European road conditions.

Robotaxi approval in Europe is tied to new UNECE (UN Economic Commission for Europe) legislation being developed to create a global framework for autonomous driving. Several European countries are already piloting autonomous vehicles, but these are limited to controlled environments and pre-set routes rather than open-road, general-purpose operation. A broader robotaxi rollout is likely years away pending this legislation.

Tesla is in its 13th consecutive month of year-over-year sales decline in Europe. Brian notes that overall European automotive sales were up approximately 1 to 1.3 percent last year, and EV market share is growing due to upcoming mandates. Tesla's decline is happening against a rising tide, meaning it is actively losing share. Germany is cited as particularly difficult, where Volkswagen, Audi, Porsche, Mercedes, and BMW all have home-field advantage with competitive EV lineups.

BYD is the most disruptive competitive threat in Europe. BYD sold approximately 18,000 vehicles in Europe in a recent quarter and is building a factory in Hungary that will allow it to produce very cheap EVs within the EU, potentially bypassing import tariffs. Norton draws a comparison to BYD's strategy in Nepal, where it patiently built a dealer network and now accounts for roughly 60 percent of all new car sales in that country. The same patient market-entry strategy is playing out in Europe.

Chinese brands are gaining ground partly through legacy brand acquisitions. Volvo, owned by Geely, continues to perform well in Europe because consumers still associate it with Swedish safety and premium quality. MG, a defunct British sports car brand bought by a Chinese company, sells compact SUVs successfully in the UK largely on brand nostalgia, even though buyers understand the cars are Chinese-made. The MG example is presented as evidence that brand memory confers a measurable sales advantage.

Legacy European automakers have squandered their transition window. Both speakers argue that companies like Volkswagen wasted time and capital on hydrogen development and by delaying full EV commitment in favor of hybrids. The Nokia comparison is raised as a cautionary analogy for automakers that fail to adapt quickly enough to platform shifts.

title: Why Nvidia Is a WARNING About Tesla, AI and The Stock Market

facts.text: Nvidia beat earnings dramatically, growing over 70% year-over-year, forecasted even higher growth for the next quarter, and reported being sold out with no end in sight. Despite this, the stock dropped approximately 5% after results.

The creator had predicted this sell-off in advance, arguing that once investors decide something is a bubble, strong earnings paradoxically reinforce their belief rather than dispel it. He predicts Nvidia will surpass $200 per share in short order.

Block (the fintech company, ~$30 billion market cap) announced layoffs of 4,000 employees — nearly half its workforce — citing AI automation enabling "smaller, highly talented teams." The stock rose 24%, generating approximately $7 billion in market cap in a single day.

The creator uses Block as a financial case study: if employees represent ~60% of operating expenses and a company cuts half of them, a 10-20% operating margin can jump to roughly 50%, potentially growing the bottom line by 150%.

Block is framed as a preview of what will happen across all white-collar industries — Ernst & Young, Deloitte, Accenture, JP Morgan are cited as inevitable next candidates for similar mass layoffs within roughly 12 months.

The AI-is-a-bubble thesis rests on the claim that AI generates no real value. The creator argues Block's market cap event directly refutes this, as concrete, measurable value was created through AI-driven cost reduction.

The creator forecasts a severe white-collar unemployment crisis: a "poop show" by end of 2026, panic in 2027, and serious widespread unemployment by 2028, affecting the core of the American middle class.

He acknowledges a paradox: AI will produce the greatest era of corporate profitability and capital gains in history, while simultaneously devastating employment. He argues the economy can still function if wealth concentrates among a few large buyers of AI infrastructure, even without mass consumer spending.
The Fear and Greed Index stood at 44 (fear territory) at the time, which he considers absurd given underlying fundamentals.

He raises unresolved political questions: whether mass unemployment will fuel left-wing socialism (citing AOC and similar figures), right-wing populism, oligarchic control, or authoritarian AI-enabled governance — and acknowledges these outcomes are genuinely difficult to predict.

title: Will Nvidia Stock DRAG DOWN Tesla And The Market?

facts.text: Nvidia fell roughly 3.55% on the day covered, down approximately 6.79% from the start of that week, despite reporting what the creator calls one of the best earnings quarters in stock market history. The drop is attributed to sentiment-driven skepticism rather than any fundamental weakness.

Block (Jack Dorsey's company) fired 40% of its workforce citing AI productivity gains, and its stock surged. The creator acknowledges the behavior was tasteless but argues the underlying rationale — AI enabling massive workforce efficiency — is valid and is playing out across Amazon, Salesforce, and other tech companies.

OpenAI closed what the creator calls the largest funding round in history at $110 billion, backed by Amazon ($50 billion), Nvidia ($30 billion), and SoftBank ($30 billion). This directly removes a previously cited bear-case risk that OpenAI couldn't afford to keep paying Nvidia for chips.

The NASDAQ has been essentially flat since September 22nd at roughly 22,788, representing about six months of sideways movement even as AI company fundamentals continue accelerating. The creator argues this consolidation builds pressure for an upward breakout, projecting a catch-up rally within roughly four to eight weeks — potentially April or May.

The AI rally is characterized as fundamentally driven, not a bubble. Evidence cited includes Anthropic being described as the fastest-growing revenue company in history, the OpenAI funding round, and the Block layoff story illustrating real-world AI productivity deployment.

Tesla is trading at a forward PE of approximately 374 compared to Meta's 24, justified only if Robotaxi and Optimus unlock massive future cash flows. The creator warns this thesis faces a critical test in April and May when Cybercab production ramps and the question of FSD's commercial deployment readiness becomes unavoidable.

Two binary scenarios for Tesla by April-May: the stock could surge toward $680 if Robotaxi scales successfully, or it could face serious downside pressure if Cybercabs pile up in parking lots unable to be commercially deployed due to FSD limitations.

title: Elon Doubled Down On Tesla Again

facts.text: Elon's recent bullish statements: In a recent interview, Musk said "Hold on to your Tesla stock. It's going to be worth a lot. That's my bet." He also replied "wise choice" to an investor who disclosed a $2.2 million all-in Tesla position with no other stocks.

Musk backed words with money: On September 15, 2025, Elon purchased approximately 2.5 million Tesla shares for roughly $1 billion, at an average price just under $390 — close to where the stock trades at the time of this video.

Tesla's key near-term catalysts cited by Musk: FSD approval in Europe expected around March 20th (Netherlands first), Cybercab volume production starting in April at Giga Texas, battery cell production beginning at Gigaberlin, and lithium and nickel cathode refineries now operational in Austin.

Long-term valuation targets from Musk: Robo taxi autonomy alone could make Tesla a $5 trillion company. Optimus humanoid robots could push it to $25 trillion. Musk has stated 80% of Tesla's future value will come from Optimus — a figure Basher's own models corroborate, though virtually no Wall Street firms factor Optimus into their valuations.

Basher's present value estimate: Using conservative discounted cash flow modeling that includes Optimus, Basher calculates Tesla's present value in the $2,000–$3,500 per share range, roughly 10x current prices.

The "all-in" debate: No financial advisor would formally recommend full concentration in one stock, but asset concentration is historically how outsized wealth is created. The key distinction is that passive investors cannot control company direction the way founders like Musk can.

Stock behavior pattern: Research shows nearly all of Tesla's historical price appreciation occurred in approximately 42 key trading days. Most gains happen outside market hours. This makes market timing extremely difficult and favors long-term holding.

Asymmetric robo taxi catalysts to watch: Removing safety drivers from vehicles, Cybercab production scale-up, national autonomous vehicle legislation, FSD unsupervised rollout to AI4 hardware, and international regulatory approvals each represent discrete events that could cause sudden large stock moves.

SpaceX/XAI ecosystem synergy: Starlink revenue is projected to be comparable in scale to robo taxi revenue. XAI's AI data center business is comparable in scale to the Optimus TAM. Tesla and SpaceX function as major customers of each other, reinforcing both businesses.

Investing philosophy takeaway: Stock market returns are driven by a tiny number of "lottery winner" companies (research by Hendrik Bessembinder at Arizona State University). The average stock over its lifetime generates a negative return. If you identify a winner, selling too early is a major risk.

title: Tesla Quietly Solved Its Battery Problem

facts.text: Giga Nevada's actual output is likely far higher than reported. The official figure cited for Giga Nevada is around 37 gigawatt hours annually, but Brian argues this number is understated — "sand in the bags," as he puts it. He believes the true figure is closer to 80 gigawatt hours when Panasonic's full contribution is accounted for. Tesla and Panasonic have not publicly disclosed this, but he contends the math of existing vehicle production makes it a necessary conclusion.

The Reno LFP factory is already online and producing 10 gigawatt hours. This facility was built using secondhand equipment purchased from CATL. Critically, Brian emphasizes these are stationary-grade LFP cells, not automotive-grade — meaning they are not suitable for vehicles that experience vibration and mechanical stress. Their designated purpose is stationary energy storage, likely feeding into the Houston Megafactory's Megapack production.

Giga Texas is beginning 4680 cell production at approximately 10 gigawatt hours. This initial output is expected to scale considerably. The 4680 cell, Tesla's in-house cylindrical format, is described as a "game-changer in the making," with the Texas facility theoretically capable of reaching 100 gigawatt hours at full buildout. Near-term production is expected to supply the Tesla Semi and Cybertruck.

An LG Energy Solution contract is strongly suspected to be in place for around 20 gigawatt hours of LFP cells. LG built a 50 gigawatt hour plant in Lansing, Michigan, originally intended to supply a range of American automakers. With US legacy automakers pulling back significantly from EV commitments, LG is left with enormous excess capacity. Brian argues Tesla is the only logical buyer at scale, estimating they would take roughly 40% of that plant's output — about 20 gigawatt hours — to start, with strong likelihood of purchasing more if the cells perform.

The demand side requires approximately 180 gigawatt hours across all Tesla segments. Brian breaks this down as roughly 40 gigawatt hours for Megapack stationary storage (Megapack Lathrop factory), 40 gigawatt hours for automotive, 50 gigawatt hours for the new Houston Megafactory, and up to 50 gigawatt hours for the Semi program at scale, though he rounds and notes some of these are aspirational figures tied to production ramp timelines.

The Semi program can draw from multiple fungible cell formats. The Semi has historically used 2170 cells and can interchange with 4680s depending on range requirements. Short-range variants could use one chemistry while long-range variants use another. Brian notes that third-party manufacturers of 4680-format cells could also serve lower-drain applications like the Cybercab, since the Cybercab does not require the high-discharge performance enabled by Tesla's tabless electrode design.

Leithrop (Megapack) automotive battery supply confusion has been resolved. Brian admits he previously believed the Lathrop Megafactory's automotive line was using LFP cells, but discovered they are actually using 2170 cells sourced from Nevada. This means the existing Nevada supply chain already accounts for those vehicles, and the cell count previously thought to be a gap is already covered.

Panasonic plans to expand Nevada production and begin making 4680 cells there. Though the announcement dates to 2023, this remains an important component of the longer-term supply picture, adding another domestic source of the larger-format cells and further reducing Tesla's dependency on any single supplier.

Samsung is also reportedly in talks to supply batteries to Tesla. This is mentioned briefly as further evidence that multiple major cell manufacturers are competing to supply Tesla, giving the company leverage and redundancy in its sourcing strategy.

title: Cybertruck Surge, Berlin Going Forward, Tunnels in Nashville, and... wait, WHAT?!?

facts.text: Elon Musk's internal Berlin video. An internally produced video was released on X in which Elon answered questions from Giga Berlin employees. The hosts note that some questions appeared to genuinely surprise Elon, suggesting the questions were curated by a Tesla team rather than hand-picked by him. The core message was that Tesla remains committed to Germany, wants to expand, but is constrained by European regulation. The hosts argue that critics focusing on Berlin's declining sales volume are misreading the situation, since Berlin is not Tesla's production bottleneck, making its output figures low signal-to-noise for evaluating Tesla's overall health.

FSD expansion to the Netherlands and beyond. FSD supervised driving is set to launch in the Netherlands on March 20th, and has already officially begun in the UAE. The hosts also note FSD is active in Korea, Australia, and China. Brian frames FSD approval in the Netherlands as significant because EU countries tend to follow each other's regulatory lead, and FSD is described as "the gateway drug to robo taxi," which is Tesla's ultimate goal in Europe. The hosts criticize European media and analysts for ignoring FSD, robo taxi, Megapack, Optimus, and Solar when evaluating Tesla's European position.

Cybertruck demand surge at $60,000. The price drop to $60,000 for a dual-motor Cybertruck triggered what the hosts describe as the highest order volume since the truck's launch. Deliveries are now backed up to April 2027. The hosts expect the price to rise once the promotional window closes, with guesses landing around $65,000–$69,420 (the latter offered as a joke). Joe and Brian both emphasize this validates the thesis that demand is highly price-elastic for the Cybertruck. Brian notes the average new truck sells for $66,000 and the Ford Raptor has climbed from $47,000 in 2019 to roughly $80,000+, making the Cybertruck competitively priced with far superior technology. The hosts argue that as Tesla continues down the cost curve, it will increasingly undercut legacy truck makers whose manufacturing costs are rising, not falling.

Tesla's Giga Texas north campus expansion. Based on reporting from Joe Tegtmeyer, who recently toured the Cyber Cab production line, the north campus expansion at Gigafactory Texas is widely expected to house Optimus production. Brian states that pilot-scale Optimus production will begin at Fremont (replacing the Model S/X lines when they end in June), with the Texas facility targeting up to one million units annually, with Elon having referenced 100 million units as a longer-term capacity goal. Brian also speculates Cortex 3 (the next AI training data center) is a likely tenant, and raises the possibility of solar panel manufacturing given the 100% import tariff on Chinese-made cells, which effectively doubles the price of competing Chinese panels in the US market. Chip fabrication is considered unlikely given a recent fab cost cited at $165 billion.

Model Y L safety and global rollout. Tesla released footage of the Model Y L featuring new curtain airbags covering rear windows for second-row protection and an additional airbag in the third row, along with heated third-row seats and dedicated ventilation — features notably absent from the standard Model Y's afterthought third row. Regulatory approvals for the Y L have been seen in Australia and Germany. Brian makes a strong case for bringing it to the US, pointing to surplus production capacity in Austin and a large American market for three-row SUVs. The hosts also muse about a potential Cyber SUV, arguing it would not need stainless steel and could reuse front castings and battery packs from the Cybertruck platform.

Tesla sues California DMV over false advertising ruling. Tesla's legal team is challenging the California DMV's ruling that Tesla engaged in false advertising regarding Full Self-Driving. The key legal argument is that the DMV never demonstrated actual consumer confusion about whether the cars required a human at the wheel. The hosts praise the legal framing as a clever and narrow argument, noting Tesla has a strong track record in litigation. The case is expected to take years but could establish important legal precedent for how autonomous driving features are regulated and described in California.

Boring Company tunneling in Nashville. The Boring Company received permits for Nashville tunnels and immediately began drilling — reportedly already 2.5 feet in before the ink was dry. Hans highlights that permitting is the main bottleneck, not engineering, since tunnel entrances and exits must interface with existing urban infrastructure. Brian recalls how Tesla used the same "go the moment you get permission" approach at Giga Berlin's land clearing, bringing in logging contractors from Ukraine and the Czech Republic and clearing hundreds of acres in three days. The hosts also note that underground property rights are legally undefined in a way that airspace is not, which is both a regulatory challenge and an opportunity.

Uber's autonomous vehicle marketplace play. Uber announced a new platform for autonomous vehicle operators to list rides on the Uber network. Brian dismisses this as a company "grasping at straws," noting Uber sold its own AV development for scrap and has no proprietary technology to offer. He argues Uber's business model — extracting margin from both drivers and customers while owning neither cars nor employees — is fundamentally threatened by Tesla's robo taxi network and Waymo's growing direct consumer presence.

Lamborghini abandons EV plans. Lamborghini's CEO cited weak EV demand as justification for shelving its electric vehicle program. Brian reframes this as Lamborghini failing to make an EV compelling at their price point, not evidence that people don't want EVs. He also suggests it reflects a bet that Tesla's Roadster 2 will never materialize as a performance competitor.
Tesla stock at approximately $405, down ~7% on the day of recording.
Average new truck price in the US: $66,000. Average new car: $53,000.
Ford Raptor was $47,000 in 2019; now approaching $80,000+.
Cybertruck new entry-level dual-motor price: $60,000 ($62,000 with delivery fee). Deliveries now backed to April 2027.
Netherlands ranks first globally with approximately 5 EVs per public charger, driven by a demand-request-based infrastructure strategy.
A recent chip fabrication factory was cited as costing $165 billion, making in-house chip fab unlikely for Tesla near-term.
Chinese solar panels face a 100% import tariff in the US, meaning US-made panels only need to cost less than double the Chinese price to win on price.

title: IRAN! What Happens to the Stock Market on Monday?

facts.text: The host served in the US Navy and spent significant time at the Naval Support Activity base in Manama, Bahrain between approximately 2011 and 2019, associated with the USS George H.W. Bush, the last Nimitz-class supercarrier (nuclear-powered, 5,000 personnel, 60+ aircraft).

He recounts a real incident of Iranian naval vessels attempting to breach a US fleet security perimeter ("the box") during an underway replenishment operation. Fighter jets were launched from the George Bush to deter them.

His read on the geopolitical situation: the current US administration (Trump, Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth) is better suited for this engagement than past administrations because they served in uniform, define clear success criteria, and have demonstrated ability to conduct quick operations — pointing to the prior 12-day strike on Iranian nuclear sites as evidence.

He distinguishes between the Iranian government/Ayatollah and the Iranian people, expressing hope that the strike creates an opening for Iranians to reclaim their own government, aided by Starlink satellite communications.
Iran was firing missiles into Bahrain, Kuwait, Qatar, UAE, and Saudi Arabia to hit US bases — all of which condemned the attacks. The EU condemned both sides, which he called weak.

His bold market prediction: stocks may open GREEN on Monday, not red. His reasoning mirrors what happened in February 2022 when Russia actually invaded Ukraine — markets initially sold off during the uncertainty buildup, then rose once the situation was known, because certainty replaced ambiguity.

He uses Bitcoin as a leading indicator: it dropped to around $63,000 at the start of the attacks but was recovering by the time of recording (around 2:16 PM Eastern Saturday), which he views as a positive signal for equities.
He believes markets are currently oversold generally and plans a separate Monday video covering Nvidia and broader market positioning.

title: I Wasn't Going to Post This..
categories: Autonomous Driving, Robotaxi, Electric Vehicles, Market & Competition

facts.text: AI Doomsday Narrative Dismissed.: Nick addresses a widely circulated "Centuri Research" report — described as a hypothetical fictional scenario rather than a formal research paper — that painted a doomsday picture of AI agents automating away all jobs and crashing GDP within a few years. He argues this report, alongside bearish commentary from figures like Michael Burry, is primarily clickbait fear-mongering. His counterargument: none of the major AI companies (Anthropic, OpenAI, Nvidia, Tesla) have laid people off due to AI, and historically every major technology — from automobiles to the internet — has created more jobs than it displaced. He urges investors to avoid SaaS/software stocks in the near term not because he believes the doom narrative, but because the cultural sentiment is currently negative on that sector and can cause irrational price swings.

Macro Environment: Bifurcated Data and Opportunity.: Nick describes a confusing macro backdrop: CPI at 2.4% but PCE running hotter than expected; solid jobs numbers alongside downward revisions to prior-year figures; and whipsawing tariff policy. He notes the 30-year fixed mortgage rate dropped back below 6% — the lowest since 2022 — and believes the U.S. is approaching a deflationary inflection point that would force the Fed to cut rates meaningfully. He argues the broad selloff in high-capex companies like Nvidia, Amazon, Microsoft, Google, and Meta is irrational given their strong revenue and profit growth, and represents a buying opportunity. He points to the VIX sitting just below its six-year average as evidence that institutional "smart money" (visible via 13F filings) is not actually panicking — they are buying.

Tesla Narrative vs. Reality.: Nick argues Tesla's current weakness around $394–$410 is a "manufactured" narrative problem rather than a fundamental one. He identifies four bearish narratives being pushed against Tesla: (1) Elon is always late, (2) Tesla vehicles can be unsafe, (3) the robotaxi program is exaggerated, and (4) Tesla sets expectations it doesn't meet. He counters each by pointing to concrete evidence of scaling: Tesla is now hiring safety monitor / AI robotaxi operator roles in approximately 14 cities including a new Tampa Bay, Florida listing. He views the discontinuation of outright FSD purchases in favor of subscriptions as a strong signal that a major software release is imminent. He predicts Tesla will reach a $500 handle and possibly $600 by year-end.

Personal Robotaxi Experience in Austin.: Nick shares a firsthand account of riding Tesla robotaxis during a recent Austin trip. Positives: the ride itself was described as "flawless" and "perfect" — smooth with zero issues. Negatives: significant availability problems, including being unable to hail a robotaxi from his hotel due to excess demand, waiting 25 minutes for a vehicle near downtown, and having to walk a block and a half to the pickup point. He had to use Uber for at least one trip. He interprets availability constraints as a supply problem (not enough vehicles deployed in Austin yet) and expects this to change as the fleet scales, particularly around April when Cybercab production ramps.

Cybercab and Robotaxi Scale-Up Expectations.: Nick states the robotaxi fleet has grown to over 400 vehicles (up from ~372 noted in prior data), with approximately 8 operating unsupervised. He expects April to be a major inflection point: Cybercab production beginning at Giga Texas, multi-city robotaxi launches, and a significant FSD software update enabling new voice/conversational interfaces with the vehicle (e.g., "pull over here," "find a parking spot," "back into the driveway"). He also notes the Cybertruck dual motor at $59,990 is already backordered into September/October, pushing back against the "demand problem" narrative.

Institutional Smart Money Buying Tesla.: Nick highlights that 13F filings show major institutional investors buying Tesla aggressively, and notes that even George Soros has taken a position in Tesla stock — which he characterizes as a particularly notable contrarian signal given Soros's political distance from Elon Musk.

LEAPS Management Strategies.: For investors holding underwater Tesla LEAPS (e.g., $430 strike bought when stock was near ATH, now at $394), Nick outlines four tactical options: (1) Cut the loss entirely if the original thesis is invalidated. (2) Sell shorter-dated calls against the LEAPS ("poor man's covered call") to collect premium while waiting for the thesis to play out — ensuring the short call strike is above the long call's break-even at expiration to avoid being underwater on both legs. (3) Roll the LEAPS out to a later expiration and down closer to the money to reduce cost basis and improve probability of profit. (4) Scale out partially — reduce from, say, 10 contracts to 6–8 — to take some risk off while maintaining bullish exposure, optionally combined with selling calls against the remaining position.
Tesla stock trading at approximately $394–$410 at time of recording (stated as a Tuesday in late February/early March timeframe based on context)
Robotaxi fleet described as "over 400 vehicles," ~8 operating unsupervised
Cybercab production expected to begin April 2026 at Giga Texas
Cybertruck dual motor at $59,990 backordered to September/October 2026
30-year fixed mortgage rate dropped below 6% — lowest since 2022
Nick's personal yield: ~98% of his trading profits come from selling options, not buying LEAPS
Tesla hiring robotaxi safety monitor roles in ~14 cities, including new Tampa Bay, FL listing
Nick's year-end Tesla price target: ~$600 (self-described as "just some idiot on YouTube")

title: Tesla Just Revealed Their Entire 2026 Roadmap

facts.text: Lithium refinery, Corpus Christi, Texas — Operational as of January 2026. Largest and most advanced in the U.S. Ground broken 2023, targeting 50 GWh of battery-grade lithium annually. Processes raw spodumene ore domestically, reducing China supply chain dependence.

Megapack factory, Brookshire, Texas — Under construction, production targeted late 2026. $200 million invested across 1.6 million square feet. Employment projected at 375 by end of 2026, scaling to 1,500 by 2028. Revenue projection: $8–10 billion annually from this single facility.

Semi-truck factory, Giga Nevada — Construction complete, production line installation underway. Volume production targeted for 2026, originally planned for late 2025. Capacity of up to 50,000 semi-trucks annually.

Cybercab manufacturing line, Giga Texas — Production line being built. First unit rolled off in February 2026. Scale production targeted for April 2026. Over 50 job openings including body fit technicians, signaling imminent production.

Optimus robot factory, Giga Texas — Ground broken November 2025, construction underway. Phase one: pilot production at Fremont targeting 1 million units per year by late 2026. Phase two (2027): dedicated Giga Texas facility with 8 million square feet and 10 million unit annual capacity. Currently 110 open positions spanning AI, reinforcement learning, mechanical design, and actuator integration.
Giga Texas expansion — Adding 5.2 million square feet by end of 2026 for body-in-white, stamping, plastics, and paint operations supporting Cybercab and general capacity growth.

Cybercab — Two-passenger autonomous vehicle, no steering wheel or pedals, 200-mile range, 35 kWh battery, inductive charging, target price under $30,000. Scale production April 2026. Elon cited a 10-second cycle time target, with an eventual goal of one unit every 5 seconds per line.
Tesla Semi — Class 8 electric truck, 500-mile range, 0–60 in 20 seconds loaded, megawatt charging capable. Volume production ramping toward 50,000 units annually in 2026.
Optimus V3 — Production-intent prototype expected February–March 2026. Target price $20,000–$30,000. Initial internal factory deployment in 2026 with limited external sales.
Roadster — Demo event scheduled April 1, 2026. Full production estimated late 2027. Specs include 0–60 under 2 seconds, 250+ mph top speed, 620-mile range, optional SpaceX cold gas thruster package.
Megapack (energy storage) — Tied to the Brookshire factory coming online late 2026.

title: EV Makers Are Burning Billions w/ @RandyKirk_YouTubeChannel

facts.text: Lucid's Q4 results were deeply mixed and broadly missed expectations. The company beat revenue forecasts by 12% but massively missed on earnings, posting a loss of $3.62 per share versus the expected $2.62 — a full dollar per share worse than anticipated. Lucid has also lowered its 2026 production targets to between 25,000 and 27,000 units. Despite this being a year-over-year increase, the hosts note it is modest for a company expecting a second vehicle (a midsize model) to rescue its fortunes.

Lucid's survival depends almost entirely on Saudi Arabia's Public Investment Fund. The company holds just under $1 billion in cash on hand, which Brian describes as dangerously low because suppliers begin demanding less favorable terms once cash reserves shrink. However, when cash equivalents and other liquid assets are counted, the figure rises to roughly $2 billion, and with undrawn Saudi credit facilities included, Lucid has access to approximately $6 billion total. Randy points out that if the Saudis draw down their remaining $4 billion in loans, they would go from majority to overwhelming control, inflicting severe dilution on existing shareholders.

Randy argues that companies with unlimited financial backing often underperform cash-strapped ones. He cites a business theory suggesting startups without financial pressure lack urgency and execution discipline. He draws a contrast with Elon Musk, who came close to bankruptcy simultaneously at both Tesla and SpaceX, and credits that existential pressure with forcing excellence. Lucid, by contrast, appears to have good engineering and design but cannot translate that into meaningful sales volume, which Randy finds puzzling and deeply concerning.

Lucid's stock has fallen roughly 55-56% over the past year, and the company has not conducted significant layoffs despite severe cash burn. Both hosts express bewilderment at the lack of aggressive cost-cutting measures. Brian questions what exactly Lucid's management believes it is doing, given that burning cash at this rate without restructuring is difficult to justify.

Rivian holds the title of the largest cumulative cash-burning pure EV company, with total losses reaching negative $24.4 billion. A chart discussed in the video shows Rivian dwarfing all competitors in cumulative losses, including Tesla (which reached approximately negative $9 billion before turning profitable) and Workhorse (which has lost only around $0.7 billion over a longer period). Rivian's stock, however, is up approximately 31-32% over the past year, which the hosts attribute to anticipation around the upcoming R2 vehicle.

Rivian's R2 is seen as the potential turning point for the company but comes with significant conditions. The R2 is expected to be priced in the $40,000-$50,000 range, with Brian suggesting that a price starting with a "4" would be a meaningful demand driver. He expects a higher-priced launch edition for early adopters. The R2 occupies a distinct segment — a smaller vehicle with genuine off-road capability — which Randy believes could avoid cannibalizing Rivian's existing R1T/R1S lineup. Randy estimates that if costs are controlled and the ramp goes well, Rivian could begin pulling per-vehicle profit from the R2 division within a year, potentially changing the company's financial narrative.

The current R1T and R1S are described as aging relative to competitors, with price being the primary barrier to higher sales. Brian notes the vehicles are "long in the tooth" post-refresh compared to newer entrants. Randy reiterates his frustration that neither Rivian nor Lucid seems to be doing enough creative sales and marketing work — suggesting aggressive leasing, niche targeting, or other tactics — to move more units.

Stellantis posted its first-ever annual net loss of approximately $26 billion, driven by a nearly $27 billion writedown. The company has suspended its dividend. Management cited overestimating the pace of EV transition, but the hosts suggest the more accurate explanation is that Stellantis's EV products are simply not competitive, noting that global EV sales overall continue to grow.
