Tesla's European registrations rose 10% in February, and Tesla surpassed the entire Volkswagen Group (which includes Volkswagen, Skoda, Audi, Seat, Cupra, and Porsche) in quarterly sales in Norway. Brian explains the recurring "delivery wave" pattern: Tesla batches cars onto ships at quarter's end, so early-quarter data always looks weak before a large surge arrives. He stresses this is a predictable, structural phenomenon, not a sign of weakness.

Global sales data for January and February shows Tesla up 7,500 units year-over-year. Standout growth markets include South Korea up 342%, Japan up 200%, Australia up 62%, Germany up 32%, Spain up 73%, and France up 24%. Declining markets such as UK (down 41%) and Taiwan (down 99%) are attributed to ships not yet having arrived with inventory rather than demand collapse.

Xpeng has introduced its second-generation VALA 2.0 autonomous driving system and plans to launch fully driverless robotaxis in Guangzhou later in 2026. Brian, who personally drove a car running VALA 2.0, rates it roughly equivalent to Tesla FSD version 13, noting Xpeng's training data benefits from extremely chaotic Chinese road conditions — including driving the wrong way against 30 scooters — which essentially force edge-case learning at scale.

FSD supervised road trials have expanded globally, with active testing now in Abu Dhabi, Tokyo (ahead of a 2026 launch), and Norway, which has approved public road testing. Brian uses this multi-country expansion to argue that autonomous driving is an unstoppable trend regardless of opinions about Tesla or Elon Musk specifically.

CATL leads global EV battery market share at well over one-third, with BYD in a distant second at 13.8%. CATL is now deploying sodium-ion batteries in a mass-produced passenger vehicle for the first time, with 248 miles of range. Brian clarifies a common misconception: because manufacturing costs dominate battery pricing — not raw materials — sodium-ion batteries will diversify the supply chain but not dramatically cut costs. He also notes a volumetric energy density drawback: a sodium-ion battery may weigh the same as a lithium pack but occupy nearly twice the physical space.

BYD's Blade 2.0 battery can charge from 10% to 80% in approximately 5 minutes and is rated to last 600,000 miles. It survived being pierced with a spike during high-rate charging without fire or smoke. Brian notes these cells could theoretically accept charge from a Tesla Semi charger, underscoring the scale of power involved.

Tesla is preparing its largest Supercharger station yet in Yermo, California, with 400 V4 stalls across multiple phases. Brian connects this to grid-scale battery storage, noting that China deployed more stationary energy storage in December 2025 alone than the United States deployed for the entire year, making megawatt-scale charging more feasible there currently.

A skeptical take on Doughnut Labs and solid-state battery claims. Brian says their behavior — vague promises, red flags, a "available today" website claim since January now pushed to late April — is consistent with a product that does not yet exist. He acknowledges the odds are "not zero" but stands by his skepticism.

Tesla is quietly building the most vertically integrated EV battery supply chain in the Western world, handling lithium refinement, anode and sometimes cathode production, and running three separate cell manufacturing facilities: Austin, Kato Road in Fremont, and a new LFP plant in Nevada. Giga Nevada cells are made by Panasonic and are counted separately.

The new BYD Seal (refreshed model) is priced at approximately $24,600 in China versus the Model 3 at $34,100, and BYD includes its autonomous driving tech at no extra cost — a significant competitive advantage in markets where Tesla charges for FSD.

title: OIL IS SKYROCKETING - WHAT NOW?

facts.text: Iran conflict and the Strait of Hormuz. Iran named a new supreme leader — the son of the previous Supreme Leader Khomeini — who is described as aligning with the old regime. The US and Israel have reportedly called him a "non-starter." Nick tracks Iran's declining military output: ballistic missile launches dropped from 350 per day on day one to 15 by day eight, and drone swarms fell from 294 to 12 over the same period. Ship traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, which had been at roughly 140 vessels per day as of February 24, dropped to zero by March 6, though Secretary of Energy Chris Wright indicated vessels were beginning to cross again. Nick's view is that Iran's military capacity is being rapidly depleted and the US-Israel coalition is simply too large to contend with. He identifies three goals the White House and Israel need to declare victory: eliminating Iran's nuclear development capability, neutralizing long-range ballistic missiles, and installing or ensuring leadership that won't restart nuclear programs.

Oil hitting $100+ per barrel. WTI crude surged to $108 at the time of recording, a level Nick says he hasn't seen since working in the oil field over a decade ago. Dow futures were tumbling 900 points as markets opened. Nick offers a contrarian take on inflation: while conventional wisdom says high oil causes inflation, he argues persistently high energy prices are more likely to cause deflation by suppressing consumer spending on discretionary items like vacations and travel. He acknowledges energy costs do flow into groceries, furniture, and transportation, so goods inflation is real, but he doesn't expect it to hit housing. His trading response is to begin selling calls on USO (United States Oil Fund), viewing current oil prices as unsustainable and near a top.

Private credit market fraud and gating. Starting at the end of 2025 and continuing into 2026, several companies have been caught double-pledging collateral — using the same asset to secure loans from multiple lenders simultaneously. Nick uses a simple car analogy: pledge the same vehicle to two separate banks without disclosure, and if you default, both banks claim the same asset. Some companies have now gone bankrupt after being caught. On top of the fraud, major firms including BlackRock, Blackstone, and Blue Owl have begun "gating" — restricting investor withdrawals from their private credit funds. Nick explains that private credit markets are inherently illiquid, and when many investors try to exit simultaneously, fund managers slow or freeze redemptions. He draws a direct parallel to trading illiquid options: getting in is easy, but getting out at a fair price can be near-impossible.

February jobs report miss. The February jobs report came in at negative 92,000, far below the expected gain of 50,000 to 60,000. The unemployment rate ticked up from 4.3% to 4.4%. Nick is dismissive of the signal value of the number, pointing out that last year's figures were revised by approximately one million jobs, making the raw monthly prints essentially noise. Explanations offered by others — a nurses strike, bad weather — he characterizes as irrelevant excuses. His practical advice: use knowledge of scheduled economic events (jobs reports, CPI, PPI, Fed speakers) as a risk management tool. For example, if you hold profitable options positions heading into a known data release, consider closing them beforehand to lock in gains and free up buying power.

OpenAI and Oracle Stargate pullback. OpenAI and Oracle announced at the end of Friday that they are canceling or scaling back their Stargate data center expansion plans. Nick attributes this to OpenAI being "wishy-washy" about energy needs and possible private credit market financing issues. He is skeptical of the AI doomsday narrative that Wall Street will run with, pointing out that Anthropic, OpenAI, Nvidia, and Tesla are all still actively hiring — arguing that if AI were truly eliminating jobs at scale, those companies would be the first to show it through mass layoffs. However, he does believe the capex spending is real and represents genuine productivity investment. He notes that Meta is rumored to be moving to fill the gap left by OpenAI/Oracle, and Nvidia reportedly put down $125 million as a retainer to secure data center capacity and keep AMD out, which he views as a healthy sign.

Tesla analysis and Mark Newton prediction update. Tesla is currently trading around $396 at the time of recording. Nick calls out Funstrat's Mark Newton, who had previously predicted Tesla would reach $500–$600 in March 2026. Nick challenged Newton publicly, and Newton responded saying it won't happen by end of March, that he's "hopeful for $450–$470 initially," and that $413 is a key trigger level. Nick found the response a non-answer because Newton didn't explain what changed in his model. Nick's own view remains bullish long-term, still targeting $500–$600 by end of 2026, characterizing the weak start as part of Tesla's usual annual pattern. On the positive side, Tesla Cybercabs are rolling off the line for crash testing, FSD approval in the Netherlands appears imminent (which could unlock other European countries), hiring is underway across Europe including Greece and Italy, and Tesla won a key regulatory battle in Berlin. A NHTSA event was scheduled for March 10 (two days from recording), and Nick references a legal analysis suggesting Tesla may not be bound by the 2,200-vehicle cap for autonomous vehicles without steering wheels if it can pass safety requirements. Despite the bullish operational picture, Nick says he is cautious about holding overnight covered calls on Tesla given the risk of a sudden gap up.
WTI crude hit $108 per barrel intraday, with Brent at $109. "I haven't seen that since I worked in the oil field — that's what, 10 years ago, maybe more."
Iran missile launches: 350 per day on day one, down to 15 by day eight. Drone swarms: 294 day one, 12 by day eight.
Ships through Strait of Hormuz: approximately 140 per day on February 24, dropping to zero by March 6.
February jobs report: negative 92,000 vs. expected positive 50,000–60,000. Unemployment ticked from 4.3% to 4.4%.
Tesla trading at $396 versus Mark Newton's $500–$600 March target.
Nvidia reportedly put down $125 million to retain data center capacity.
Block (formerly Square) announced 4,000 layoffs, which Jack Dorsey attributed to AI. Nick calls this "a convenient excuse for screwing up."
"$1,000 a day keeps W2 away" — attributed to a trading friend, referencing zero-DTE options income.

title: Why BUYING Cybercab Could MASSIVELY DERISK Tesla Stock

facts.text: Chamath Palihapitiya's public commentary on Cybercab is a significant signal. Palihapitiya stated he would buy and deploy large fleets of Cybercabs across the country when possible, projecting positive free cash flow in under two years. The speaker views this as important validation because Palihapitiya is a credible investor with close ties to Elon Musk and the broader tech-finance establishment, and his endorsement suggests the fleet ownership business model has real merit.

Tesla's $4 billion manufacturing investment in the Cybercab line implies high internal confidence in demand. The speaker consults supply chain and manufacturing expert Jeff Lutz and concludes that no company at Tesla's level would bring a production line of this scale online without near-certainty that the vehicles can be sold or deployed. This first-principles argument underpins the speaker's broader bull case, even as he adjusts specific predictions.

The Cybercab has no steering wheel, which creates a deployment constraint if FSD is not commercially ready. Because the vehicle cannot legally or practically be driven by a human operator, Tesla's ability to generate revenue from produced units is entirely contingent on unsupervised FSD being approved and operational. The speaker acknowledges this creates a genuine risk if regulatory or technical readiness lags behind the manufacturing ramp.

The speaker retracts his earlier specific prediction of 1,800 fully commercial unsupervised robotaxis operating by April or May 2026. After reviewing the robotaxi deployment tracker and current real-world progress, he concludes that scaled unsupervised commercial operation in the hundreds or thousands by May is unlikely. He describes a more realistic near-term trajectory as: dozens of unsupervised units in April, roughly 100 in May, 200 in June, 300 in July, growing to a few thousand by year-end through consistent doubling.

Consumer or investor sales of Cybercabs before full deployment is a viable and strategically smart buffer for Tesla. If Tesla sells units to private buyers — individuals or fleet investors like Palihapitiya — those buyers can take delivery and hold the vehicles for six months or more without the financial strain that Tesla itself would face sitting on thousands of undeployed units. This mechanism would allow Tesla to keep the manufacturing line running at target volumes (30,000–40,000 units in 2026) without creating a costly inventory buildup.

This consumer sales strategy would still be highly bullish for the stock, though not the maximum re-rating scenario. The speaker distinguishes between two outcomes: a "brutal repricing" driven by immediate large-scale unsupervised robotaxi deployment (which he now considers less likely in the near term and which he associates with a path toward $1,000+ per share), versus a strong but more measured re-rating driven by confirmed Cybercab deliveries at scale, which he believes could rapidly push Tesla toward $600 per share.

The Russia-Ukraine war (referred to as the "Iran war" in context, though likely meaning broader geopolitical conflict) is described as a major macro overlay for Tesla stock. The speaker argues that the NASDAQ's Magnificent 7 stocks, Nvidia, Bitcoin, and Tesla are all acting as "coiled springs" suppressed by geopolitical uncertainty, and that when that conflict ends, the resulting macro relief could be the single biggest short-term alpha catalyst — potentially more impactful in the immediate term than even the Cybercab launch dynamics.

title: Tesla's 2026 Milestones Will Blow Your Mind

facts.text: FSD International Expansion — Europe and Japan. Tesla's FSD Supervised is expected to receive Dutch RDW approval around March 20th, making the Netherlands the first European country to approve the system. The rollout will be an over-the-air update, though Phase 1 is restricted to highways, major intercity roads, and pre-validated urban routes — not rural roads or construction zones. Critically, the approval mechanism being used is an EU Article 39 exemption, which allows other EU member states to recognize the Dutch approval under mutual recognition frameworks, meaning Belgium, Germany, and Nordic countries could follow rapidly. A price of €9 per month was found in hidden code on Tesla's European website, suggesting backend infrastructure is already prepared. Tesla is also actively hiring autopilot vehicle operators in Croatia, Greece, Portugal, Slovenia, and the Netherlands. For Japan, Tesla's country president has confirmed FSD Supervised is targeting a launch by end of 2026, with testing active in Tokyo since August 2025 and roughly 40,000 eligible vehicles already on Japanese roads.

FSD Data Advantage. Tesla has logged approximately 8.5 billion FSD miles as of early March 2026, adding 20 million miles per day. At that pace, the cumulative total will cross 10 billion miles by midsummer. The host emphasizes no competitor is remotely close to this data scale, which compounds Tesla's training advantage over time.

Robotaxi Expansion. Tesla launched unsupervised robotaxi rides in Austin on January 22nd, a genuine milestone, but the host is careful to note the current reality: only about 18% of Austin rides are fully unsupervised, the fleet is estimated at roughly 40 vehicles, and Bay Area rides remain supervised under California law. Combined, the fleet has logged nearly 700,000 paid miles as of early February. Tesla announced at the Q4 earnings call on January 28th plans to expand to seven new cities in the first half of 2026 — Dallas, Houston, Phoenix, Miami, Orlando, Tampa, and Las Vegas — though no specific launch dates are confirmed for any of them. The host considers getting three or four cities live by summer a realistic partial win. Morgan Stanley projects roughly 1,000 robotaxis operating by end of 2026. Pricing as of March 7th stands at $3.25 base fare plus $1 per mile.

Cybercab Production. The first production Cybercab rolled off the Giga Texas line on February 17th, several weeks ahead of schedule, with 25 units visible by drone footage as of early March, though approximately 10 were at the crash testing facility for validation rather than consumer delivery. Continuous production is targeted for April. The vehicle has no steering wheel, no pedals, seats two passengers, and uses Tesla's new unboxed manufacturing process with a target cycle time of 10 seconds per vehicle at scale. Elon has confirmed it will be sold directly to consumers for under $30,000. A regulatory complication exists: current NHTSA rules limit vehicles without steering wheels to 2,500 units per year without an exemption, but the host references a separate analysis suggesting Tesla may be able to bypass this limit entirely through full safety certification. Board chairwoman Robyn Denholm has confirmed a fallback plan to add a steering wheel and pedals if regulators require it. Giga Berlin has been identified as the next Cybercab production site, with the first hardware engineering role posted March 4th.

Tesla Energy — The Underappreciated Division. In 2025, Tesla deployed 46.7 gigawatt hours of energy storage, up 49% year-over-year, generating $12.77 billion in revenue at approximately 30% gross margins, making it Tesla's most profitable segment by margin. Tesla currently operates two Megapack factories: California at 40 GWh annual capacity and Shanghai, which came online in 2025 and is ramping toward 40 GWh. The major 2026 milestone is the third factory in Brookshire, Texas (Houston area), with over $200 million already invested, production starting in the second half of 2026, and a target annual capacity of 50 GWh. This factory will produce two new products: the Mega 3, a 5 MWh unit offering 28% more capacity than the Mega 2, and the Mega Block, which stacks four Mega 3 units with transformer and switchgear into a single 20 MWh AC system that Tesla claims can commission 1 GWh in 20 business days. When Houston reaches full capacity, Tesla's combined global manufacturing could reach approximately 130 GWh per year. The order pipeline is substantial, including a $2.7 billion order from Georgia Power pending commission approval and a $275 million order from Scotland.

Deferred Energy Revenue. Tesla has $4.96 billion in deferred energy revenue scheduled to be recognized in 2026 — more than double what was recognized in 2025. The host identifies this as one of the most underreported figures in Tesla analysis, noting the revenue is already contracted and simply hasn't yet hit the income statement.

Optimus Humanoid Robots. Elon Musk admitted on the Q4 earnings call that Optimus robots are not yet doing useful work in Tesla factories in any material way — they are present for learning and data collection only. A full Gen 3 robot reveal was targeted for Q1 2026, potentially before the end of March. Musk has stated a production target of 50,000 to 100,000 units in 2026, but the host flags that the 2025 target of 5,000 to 10,000 was missed by a wide margin. What is confirmed: Model S and Model X production ends at the end of Q2, and those Fremont lines will be converted to Optimus manufacturing with an eventual target of 1 million robots per year. Land is being cleared at Giga Texas for a dedicated Optimus facility targeting 10 million units annually, but construction has not started and production from that facility is not expected until 2027. External sales to the public were floated at Davos as a 2027 possibility, conditional on achieving very high reliability and safety. Target cost of goods is $20,000 per robot at scale.

Tesla Semi Mass Production. The semi factory at Giga Nevada spans 1.7 million square feet and began producing trucks in early March 2026, with journalist Ashley Vance describing the interior as "enormous and quite spectacular" after a March 5th tour. Full ramp is expected before the end of June, with a designed annual capacity of 50,000 units. Long range variant: 500 miles, $290,000. Standard range: 325 miles, $250,000. Both variants feature three motors, 800 kilowatts, over 1,000 horsepower, and charge to 60% in 30 minutes at up to 1.2 megawatts. The $290,000 price point is approximately 40% cheaper than the average competing zero-emission Class 8 truck at $435,000. Customers beyond PepsiCo now include DHL, Walmart, Costco, Sysco, US Foods, and Hyatt Logistics. Tesla opened its first public Megacharger station on March 8th in Ontario, California, with 66 mapped locations across 15 states and 37 stations targeted for 2026.

Custom AI Chips and Supercomputing. Cortex 1 at Giga Texas currently runs 67,000 H100-equivalent GPUs at 130 megawatts. Cortex 2 Phase 1 activates in April at 250 megawatts, reaching full 500 megawatt capacity by mid-2026, for a combined total of roughly 100,000 Nvidia GPUs backed by 2.3 GWh of on-site Megapack storage. Tesla's proprietary AI5 chip delivers 2,000 to 2,500 TOPS, roughly matching a single Nvidia H100, at claimed 10 times the performance per dollar and three times the performance per watt versus Nvidia. Samsung's Taylor fab began EUV lithography equipment trials in March, with limited AI5 production targeted for late 2026. Samsung also secured a $16.5 billion contract for the next-generation AI6 chip, with Tesla reportedly negotiating to more than double the order from 16,000 to 40,000 wafers per month. Elon additionally announced the restart of Dojo 3, purpose-built for space-based AI compute, and floated the concept of a Tesla-owned semiconductor fab eventually scaling to 1 million wafer starts per month — described as aspirational, not a committed project.

Additional Rapid-Fire Milestones. The 4680 battery achieved full dry electrode production for both anode and cathode at Giga Texas, with four new cell variants planned for 2026 and roughly 40 GWh of installed production capacity. The Texas lithium refinery in Corpus Christi is fully operational as of January, the largest in North America, using a proprietary acid-free process capable of supplying battery-grade lithium for approximately 1 million EVs per year. The Tesla Roadster production version unveils April 1st, with production expected 12 to 18 months after reveal. The Model Y Long, a six-seat long-wheelbase variant, is confirmed for 2026 launch in Europe, Australia, and South Korea. Tesla's new TSP 420 solar panel began shipping in Q1 from Giga New York at initial capacity of 300 megawatts per year.

title: Elon Says Tesla AI Progress Is Exploding!

facts.text: Tesla's Cortex 2 supercomputer at Giga Texas is progressing rapidly toward a late spring launch. Drone journalist Joe Techmire has documented that just under half of the megapacks are installed, with temporary chillers being set up to allow an accelerated startup before permanent cooling systems are complete. Elon confirmed directly that the first half of Cortex 2 will be active "next month," which both hosts frame as a significant milestone because it will serve as the training ground for the Optimus humanoid robot program. Tesla's signature approach — moving in and operating before full construction is complete — is described as a key competitive advantage.

Elon Musk has publicly endorsed the idea that AGI could arrive by end of 2026. When a user named Bojan stated he had a "fact-based feeling" that full AGI would arrive by year-end, Elon replied "feels like it" and "yeah." This is notable because mainstream expert consensus had been placing AGI arrival around 2029 or 2030. Elon also confirmed that AI capabilities are "doubling in months, not years" and called the pace "insanely fast."

AI task complexity has been doubling roughly every 7 months since 2019. The transcript cites a specific progression: in 2020, AI could write a short email (a 15-second task); a year before the video, it could fix a bug; now AI can code an entire application from scratch. Today's AI can handle tasks 100 times longer than five years ago, and projections suggest tasks 10,000 times more complex will be manageable within another five years.

Elon dismissed Google's data center investment plans as "rookie numbers," insisting the real figure needed is $10 trillion. Google's AI infrastructure chief announced plans scaling from $90 billion in capital spending in 2025 to $185 billion in 2026, with a projected $1.9 trillion over 10 years. At a sustained $185 billion per year, Google would spend roughly $1.5 trillion over eight years — a figure Elon characterized as insufficient. Jeff Lutz argues this is at least partially serious, not just posturing, because the envisioned end state of AI — transforming healthcare, education, manufacturing, and robotics — requires compute infrastructure far beyond what any lab is currently building.

Concerns that xAI has "fallen behind" due to not achieving recursive self-improvement (RSI) are rebutted on infrastructure grounds. Some critics argued that since Anthropic's Claude Code and OpenAI's Codex have demonstrated RSI — using AI to improve its own code iteratively — xAI and Grok are now in a permanently widening gap. Elon responded that "it's a long way from over." A counterpoint raised in the video is that no lab has definitively proven true RSI, and that xAI currently leads all AI labs in coherent compute infrastructure at 2 gigawatts of dedicated cluster capacity, compared to OpenAI at 1.9 GW and Anthropic at 0.8 GW, which remains cloud-dependent on Amazon and Google.

xAI has filed a permit for a $659 million expansion to its Colossus 2 site in Memphis. The expansion involves a 312,000-square-foot, four-story building on a 79-acre parcel adjacent to the existing Colossus 2 data center. A third data center is also reportedly in the works just across the state boundary in Mississippi. Elon has stated xAI will eventually have access to 2 gigawatts of compute power from the cluster, and that figure does not yet include any space-based data center plans.

The debate over whether AGI requires embodied physical systems — not just large language models — is increasingly resolving in favor of the embodied camp. A year prior, experts were roughly split 50/50 on whether LLMs alone could reach AGI or whether physical, real-world interaction was required. Lutz and Herbert argue the field is now shifting toward the view that embodied systems are necessary for true AGI, which uniquely positions Tesla. Tesla vehicles are described as "baby AGI" because they navigate and process the real world in real time. With plans for 20,000–30,000 Optimus robots generating novel real-world training data, Tesla is the only company with a serious pipeline of embodied AI experience that cannot be replicated by scraping the internet.

Current AI chatbots still exhibit significant reliability problems in complex reasoning tasks. Lutz notes that while Claude Code and OpenAI's tools perform well on coding and research assistance, they fail on integrated financial tasks — producing business plans and tax calculations with systematic errors that reappear even after correction. He contrasts this with Tesla's vehicle AI, which operates at single-digit parts-per-million accuracy for billions of instructions. He estimates current chatbot accuracy on major reasoning tasks at roughly 90–95%, which he considers insufficient for high-stakes applications.

AI's public perception problem is a growing strategic risk. A poll cited in the video shows AI's approval rating sits at approximately 26%, lower than Congress. Lutz argues that AI CEOs have failed to proactively shape the narrative around abundance and societal benefit, leaving the messaging vulnerable to political and media distortion. Both hosts stress the need for a federal — not state-by-state — regulatory framework, and for public-private collaboration on transition planning to manage job displacement.
Elon on Google's $1–2 trillion data center plans: "Rookie numbers. 10 trillion or you're not even trying."
Elon on AGI timeline: "Feels like it" — responding to a claim that full AGI arrives by end of 2026.
AI task complexity has been doubling approximately every 7 months since 2019; today's AI handles tasks 100x longer than five years ago.
xAI compute: 2 GW dedicated coherent cluster, vs. OpenAI 1.9 GW and Anthropic 0.8 GW (cloud-dependent).
Google capex: $90 billion in 2025, scaling to $185 billion in 2026.
xAI Colossus 2 expansion permit: $659 million, 312,000 sq ft, 79-acre site in Memphis.

title: Why Tesla Sales “Crashes” Keep Fooling Everyone

facts.text: Year-over-year European sales data for January–February 2026 shows a net gain of approximately 7,500 units across the countries Roland Percher tracks. The hosts note that the green (positive) entries on the chart visually outweigh the red, and that this is a year-over-year comparison against a period in early 2025 when the Berlin factory was being retooled, which suppressed baseline numbers and makes the gain even more meaningful.

Several major European markets posted strong percentage gains: Germany up 32%, Spain up 73%, France up 24%, Portugal up 60%, Finland up 211%, Italy up 20%, and Romania up 134% (though to only 243 total units). These gains are notable because political headwinds related to Elon Musk's public profile were expected to suppress European demand, yet France and Germany — two markets most exposed to those sentiments — still showed growth.

Some markets declined: Denmark was down 16%, Ireland down 44%, and the UK down 41%. The hosts suggest Denmark's dip is likely a shipping timing artifact that will correct in March when a vessel arrives. The UK decline is flagged as unusual and potentially related to tariff or policy changes, with a note to watch whether it persists through the rest of Q1.

Norway's March data (available only about one week into the month at the time of recording) showed Tesla roughly doubling its year-to-date sales total in just those early March days, once a ship arrived. This is presented as a textbook example of Tesla's lumpy, ship-arrival-driven sales pattern, which causes apparent "collapses" and "surges" that are artifacts of logistics, not demand signals.

Asia-Pacific markets are performing strongly and expanding. South Korea is cited as selling 6,000–7,000 units in a strong period, Japan is described as coming on strong with FSD approval potentially imminent, and Australia continues to perform well. Newer markets including India (recently opened), Chile, and various other export destinations are adding incremental volume that, while small individually, accumulates meaningfully.

FSD regulatory approvals are advancing internationally. Norway is working on FSD approval, Tokyo road testing is underway, and the UAE is conducting FSD testing. The hosts argue that dismissing FSD's impact on future sales figures is analytically indefensible, since the feature is not yet available in most of these markets and its introduction will be a new demand catalyst.

FSD pricing is a significant barrier to adoption and needs restructuring. The US subscription is $99/month (approximately $108 with state taxes). FSD take rates reach roughly 50% when offered free but drop to 10–20% when priced, implying 30–40% of owners would use FSD if cost were not an obstacle. Both hosts agree a tiered pricing model is needed: a low-cost supervised tier for casual personal users, a mid-tier for unsupervised personal use, and a commercial/robotaxi tier at premium pricing.

Insurance bundling could unlock mass FSD adoption. Lemonade Insurance has announced a 50% discount on premiums when FSD is in use. The hosts speculate Tesla could bundle FSD with Lemonade or its own insurance product, potentially making the combined FSD-plus-insurance monthly cost lower than what drivers currently pay for insurance alone — removing the subscription pricing objection entirely. The caveat is that California bans telematics-based insurance pricing, which would exclude that market.

Chinese competitors are offering autonomous driving features for free, which creates a competitive threat in markets outside the US. Chinese automakers have adopted Tesla's over-the-air update model and are closing the technology gap. If Tesla prices FSD as a premium subscription in emerging and international markets, buyers will choose a free alternative that is slightly inferior rather than pay, and Tesla risks ceding those markets.

Randy makes a longer-term prediction that human driving will eventually be prohibited on public roads, similar to how seatbelt mandates and indoor smoking bans faced fierce initial resistance but became standard. Brian agrees, drawing the analogy to racetracks and off-road venues where recreational activities that are too dangerous for public spaces get contained.

title: Major Tesla Cybercab Developments Just Happened!

facts.text: Cybercab units spotted with robotaxi validation gear near San Jose, California. This is described as the final step before active deployment — validation rigs are used to confirm that all onboard cameras are capturing data as accurately as possible. Similar setups have previously been seen in Austin, Dallas, and other markets, and the fact that multiple units are rigged up across a wide variety of geographies is interpreted as a strong signal of imminent launch.

Large-scale Cybercab production activity observed at Giga Texas. Joe Tagmire, a well-connected Tesla observer, spotted 30 Cybercabs being loaded into transport trucks for nationwide testing, and 15 units being sent to crash test facilities. Brian White emphasizes that companies do not conduct this level of multi-city validation testing unless they are planning to roll out the vehicles immediately.

US Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy hosted a forum at DOT headquarters with Waymo, Zoox, and Tesla. Duffy framed autonomous vehicle development explicitly as a national security and economic competitiveness issue, stating that he wants American-made technology — not Chinese — to be deployed globally. His most significant statement was questioning whether autonomous vehicles even need steering wheels, and saying the department needs to "rethink some of our requirements" to enable innovation while maintaining safety.

Clarification on the regulatory path — the 2500-vehicle exemption cap is widely misunderstood. The hosts correct a common misconception: the 2500-vehicle NHTSA exemption is only for vehicles that cannot meet existing safety certification standards. Since 2022, NHTSA rules already permit vehicles without steering wheels and pedals as long as they pass all safety requirements. Tesla has not filed for the 2500 exemption because they intend to fully safety-certify the Cybercab. Zoox filed for the exemption because their bench-facing seat configuration could not meet traditional crash safety standards. The hosts argue that Waymo, Cruise, and Tesla would not have invested tens of billions of dollars if the legal path to launch didn't already exist.

New Cybercab interior features revealed from the unit displayed at the DOT headquarters. Tesla brought a production version of the Cybercab to the forum, giving observers a close look at several details. The seats slide forward and backward as a bench unit on floor tracks, giving the Cybercab more legroom per occupant than any other Tesla despite being the smallest model. A 21-inch center screen dominates the interior. The interior camera is significantly larger than those in current Tesla consumer vehicles, likely to help identify forgotten belongings and enable interior surveillance. The rear storage area is not carpeted, suggesting it is designed for easy cleaning. There are two USB-C charging ports in the center console on the prototype version. Window controls have been moved to the center console rather than the door, which shortens the wiring harness and removes potential failure points in a moving door component.

Cybercab designed from the ground up for accessibility, including visually impaired riders. Hazard buttons and interior door releases include Braille markings. The hazard/four-way flasher button is also combined with the SOS emergency stop function — if a passenger presses it, a voice immediately asks whether they are having an emergency, which is intended to deter misuse. Brian White notes this design came partly from feedback provided by a visually impaired rider who tested a Tesla in Texas and communicated suggestions to the robotaxi engineering team. The app is also designed to assist visually impaired users: it can verbally guide them to the vehicle, honk on command, and use lights for identification.

Camera washer system confirmed on Cybercab side and rear cameras. A video showed all three exterior camera positions — side fenders and rear — simultaneously spraying and cleaning the lenses. This addresses a previously unresolved question about what happens when cameras get dirty from mud, road spray, or rain during unsupervised operation.

California AI safety operator job postings were re-listed after briefly disappearing. The hosts issued a partial retraction of a previous segment that suggested the removal of those job postings indicated Tesla was ready to launch fully unsupervised. The postings were simply rewritten with refined descriptions, likely to meet regulatory language requirements. Positions are listed across Santa Clara, Palo Alto, Sunnyvale, Fremont, and San Francisco.

Miami's mayor publicly praised Elon Musk and Tesla, citing FSD, Grok, and Starlink as technologies that will "slash commutes and turbocharge Miami." The hosts interpret this as a strong indicator that Miami is positioning itself as one of the first cities for the robotaxi rollout, consistent with prior announcements naming Miami as an early launch market.

Discussion of whether personal Tesla vehicles (Model 3, Y, etc.) can participate in the robotaxi fleet. Brian White's view is that the Cybercab's purpose-built features — camera washers, larger interior camera, hard easy-clean surfaces — are helpful but not critical. He argues that personal Tesla owners should still be able to add their vehicles to the fleet on most days, with perhaps some weather-related limitations. He references Elon Musk and Tesla executive Ashok Elluswami's prior statements about a fleet of roughly 3 million Model Y and 3-series vehicles being incorporated into the network.
30 Cybercab units spotted being loaded into transport trucks at Giga Texas; 15 sent to crash test facilities.
2022 NHTSA rule change already permits no-steering-wheel vehicles if safety certification is achieved.
2500-vehicle exemption cap applies only to vehicles that cannot meet standard safety certification — not to Tesla's Cybercab.

title: Major Tesla Cybercab Developments Just Happened!

facts.text: Cybercabs spotted with robotaxi validation gear near San Jose, California. This is described as one of the final steps before a robotaxi launch — the validation rigs allow Tesla to confirm camera accuracy and sensor performance. The hosts note this is the same process previously observed in Austin and Dallas, and seeing it in multiple new markets simultaneously signals imminent deployment rather than ongoing experimentation.

Joe Tagmire (a well-sourced Tesla tracker at Giga Texas) observed 30 Cybercabs being loaded into transport trucks for nationwide testing, and 15 being sent to crash test facilities. This scale of logistics activity, combined with production volume at Giga Texas, reinforces the argument that a commercial launch is close. Brian White says, "You wouldn't do this test if you weren't planning to roll them out immediately."

Cybercab camera washer system confirmed operational. Video footage shows washer jets cleaning the side and rear cameras simultaneously — addressing a previously open question about how dirty cameras would be handled in a driverless vehicle. The hosts call it not the most elegant solution but a practical one, comparing it to the problem of spray from trucks on muddy roads.

US Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy held a forum at DOT headquarters with Waymo, Zoox, and Tesla present. Duffy explicitly questioned whether autonomous vehicles need steering wheels at all and stated that existing regulatory requirements need to be rethought to allow innovation while maintaining safety. The hosts interpret this as a major signal of regulatory tailwinds, noting they have been advocating for this shift for approximately five years.

Important clarification about the NHTSA 2500-vehicle exemption rule. The hosts correct a widespread misunderstanding: the 2,500-vehicle exemption is only required when a manufacturer cannot get a vehicle safety-certified through normal channels — it is not a mandatory cap on autonomous vehicle deployments. Tesla has not filed for this exemption, which the hosts argue is because the Cybercab is being crash-tested and designed to meet standard safety certification requirements. Zoox, by contrast, filed for the exemption after NHTSA rejected their attempt to self-certify due to the non-standard bench-seat configuration.

New interior features revealed at the DOT forum. Tesla brought a production-version Cybercab to DOT headquarters, allowing closer inspection. Key findings include: seats that slide forward and back as a bench unit, providing more legroom than any other Tesla despite the car's small size; a 21-inch center screen; an interior camera noticeably larger than those in current Tesla vehicles, likely to detect forgotten belongings and for surveillance; a rear storage area without carpet (facilitating easy cleaning); and two USB-C charging ports in the center console. Window controls have been moved to the center console rather than the door, which Brian White explains shortens the wiring harness and removes failure points from a moving part.

Cybercab designed specifically for accessibility, including visually impaired users. The hazard button and interior door releases feature Braille markings — confirmed by Eric, a member of Tesla's robotaxi team. The hazard button doubles as an emergency/SOS button; pressing it triggers a voice prompt asking if the passenger is having an emergency, which also deters misuse. The app is designed to guide visually impaired users to the vehicle through audio cues and vehicle light signals.

California AI safety operator job postings were removed and then reposted. The host issues a partial retraction of a previous segment — the job listings for safety operators in San Jose, Santa Clara, Palo Alto, Sunnyvale, Fremont, and San Francisco were briefly taken down but have been reposted with refined descriptions, likely to meet updated regulatory requirements. The host cautions against over-interpreting the original removal as a sign of imminent unsupervised launch.

Miami mayor publicly praised Elon Musk and Tesla, citing FSD, Grok, and Starlink as technologies that will "slash commutes and turbocharge Miami." Miami has already been announced as one of the first cities targeted for Cybercab robotaxi rollout, and the mayor's public enthusiasm is read as a positive indicator of local government cooperation.

Debate on whether personal Tesla vehicles will join the robotaxi fleet. One observer argued the widening feature gap between the purpose-built Cybercab and regular Model 3/Y vehicles means personal Teslas may not be viable for the fleet. Brian White disagrees, saying features like the larger camera and non-carpeted trunk are helpful but not critical, noting that Uber and Lyft drivers already operate in regular consumer vehicles. He estimates personal Teslas could be fleet-eligible on most days, with possible restrictions during heavy weather conditions.
30 Cybercabs observed being loaded for transport; 15 sent to crash test facilities on a single day.

title: Tesla Just Made A Curious Robotaxi Move

facts.text: Tesla has stopped posting AI safety operator job listings for all California cities, while still hiring for those roles in Virginia, Florida, and Texas. This is interpreted as either a very positive sign (Tesla no longer needs supervised drivers because it's approaching unsupervised operation) or a negative sign (Tesla is retreating from California entirely). Joe argues the positive interpretation is more probable based on the math of California's regulatory requirements.

California's regulatory framework requires three phases of proof that vehicles can drive themselves before unsupervised operation is permitted. Joe calculated that with 100 robotaxis, this process takes roughly three months depending on daily mileage. Tesla has approximately 355 vehicles tracked in the robotaxi tracker, and likely over 500 according to statements from Elon Musk and Tesla's Ashok, meaning they may be nearing completion of required miles as early as April 2026. The tracker is considered an undercount because it excludes employee-driven Tesla test vehicles.

Over 30 Cybercabs were spotted via drone footage at Giga Texas being loaded onto transport trucks. They have steering wheels, which is required to drive them onto the trucks, and Joe Techmire (who filmed the drone footage) believes they are being distributed to cities around the country for expanded testing. Some of these Cybercabs were also spotted at a crash testing facility nearby, which Herbert frames as a positive sign indicating production readiness and final certification work.

A separate analyst, Cernan Basher, published a finding that Tesla does not need a federal exemption for the first 2,500 autonomous vehicles if they can pass standard safety certification and crash testing. This explains why Tesla is conducting so much crash testing — passing those tests removes the numerical cap on how many Cybercabs can be deployed without steering wheels. Herbert notes this is why Tesla shifted away from a more futuristic form factor to the Cybercab design, which resembles a conventional car and can pass standard crash certification.

The hosts debate whether Tesla will meet its stated goal of launching in seven new cities before the end of June 2026. No new cities have been added as of the time of filming (March 2026), and they acknowledge this is a red flag worth watching. However, Joe argues that adding cities is operationally straightforward once FSD unsupervised is cleared; the harder and more decisive question is whether FSD 14.3 performs well enough to trigger unsupervised deployment.

Alphabet approved a $130 million performance bonus for CEO Sundar Pichai tied specifically to Waymo's performance, with the potential to reach $260 million depending on Waymo's per-unit value increase over a three-year evaluation period. The hosts interpret this as a clear signal that Alphabet is deeply committed to Waymo and willing to invest at scale, regardless of current unprofitability.

Joe's theory on Waymo is that it will be the number-two player in the robotaxi market but that its current tech stack — described as a "crappy half-manual mapping thing" relying on LiDAR — is a long-term liability. He argues Waymo is deliberately building out 9 out of 10 components of the operational infrastructure (regulations, fleet management, rider support, city approvals) while knowingly deferring the technology pivot. The plan is to eventually replace the LiDAR-dependent stack with a neural net, vision-only approach — something Waymo researchers have already been exploring internally.

Waymo's strategy of absorbing enormous losses is framed as rational because it's outsourcing a large share of those losses to outside investors, limiting Alphabet's direct exposure. Joe estimates the total loss burn could be tens of billions of dollars before the business model works, but says Google is effectively socializing much of that risk. A video clip of a Waymo vehicle getting dangerously stuck mid-turn attempting an unprotected left turn is shown as evidence that the LiDAR-heavy stack doesn't solve core intelligence problems — both hosts note that LiDAR is useless when the fundamental issue is anticipatory judgment and contextual understanding, not object detection.

Waymo received a shipment of its sixth-generation robotaxis (built by Zeekr in China) and installs its own hardware at a factory in Arizona, adding cost due to import tariffs. The hosts note Waymo is capped at around 500 vehicles per city due to unit economics — exceeding that number makes the operation even more unprofitable.

Zoox announced expansion of testing to Dallas and Phoenix, bringing its total U.S. testing footprint to 10 cities. It also launched a new "Fusion Center" command hub in Scottsdale, Arizona to manage fleet operations, remote guidance, and rider support. However, the hosts emphasize that this is testing only — Zoox is not yet operating commercially in most cities, and in Las Vegas it runs a fixed loop route rather than a full on-demand service.

Zoox's fundamental obstacle is NHTSA approval. Its purpose-built vehicle (facing passengers toward each other, with no traditional crumple zone) failed to gain full certification. NHTSA questioned how airbags function and how passenger safety is demonstrated in a crash. Zoox operates under an exemption capped at 2,500 vehicles, which makes commercial scaling impossible in its current form. Amazon is building a factory with a target of 10,000 units, but without NHTSA certification, those vehicles cannot be put on public roads. Joe emphasizes that lacking OEM manufacturing capability and automotive safety engineering expertise is a structural disadvantage that no amount of capital easily solves.

Joe argues that Tesla is the only company with all the necessary components in place: autonomous driving technology, OEM manufacturing, the ability to produce vehicles at a cost of goods well below $20,000, and existing infrastructure. He calculates that if a competitor pays $20,000 more per vehicle and wants to manufacture 10 million units, that alone represents $200 billion in additional capital expenditure — making the business model unworkable without vertical integration.

On timing and Tesla stock implications, Joe says there is roughly a 90% probability that the robotaxi inflection point occurs in 2026, but the difference between an April launch and a November launch is enormous for investors — he suggests the stock could range from $300-$400 on the low end to $800-$1,200 on the high end depending on when and whether unsupervised scaling begins. FSD version 14.3, expected around late February but slightly delayed, is identified as the likely decisive software step.

title: Tesla Semi Factory Footage Shows Huge Momentum

facts.text: The assembly line is visibly running. Through factory windows captured in Zangler's 4K drone footage, the hosts can see lights, machinery, and an active assembly line. Brian references a tour guide at Giga Nevada named Cody, who said the public would know production had started "when the new line is moving." The hosts take the footage as confirmation that this threshold has been crossed.

The scrap metal conveyor system is now installed and operational. Zangler had documented this conveyor being assembled outside the building several weeks prior. Its integration inside indicates the factory build-out is progressing rapidly, with Brian noting this system eliminates manual labor steps previously required in metal stamping operations.

Six Tesla Megacharger stalls are now visible and apparently electrified at the factory site. Mark explains that while four could theoretically run at full speed simultaneously, six stalls make practical sense given that trucks arriving at different charge states would create natural staggering in demand. The hosts speculate these chargers may serve the existing Nevada-to-Fremont internal Tesla Semi route, enabling increased capacity at the Nevada end.

The factory still has unused space, currently functioning as parts and wire storage. Brian interprets this as evidence the factory isn't fully complete yet, but is well along. Mark reiterates that the facility is designed for 50,000 units annually, but is likely built with headroom to exceed that if demand warrants.

On Q2 sales projections and financial reporting: The hosts estimate "low hundreds at most" in Q2, citing the caution Tesla will exercise to avoid recalls and verify new manufacturing processes. They note Tesla will likely keep Semi revenue lumped into an "other vehicles" category until volumes are too large to obscure — drawing a parallel to how Megapack revenue was handled in early days. Competitors watching sales figures is cited as another reason for delayed disclosure.

The long-term financial opportunity lies in autonomous trucking, not just vehicle sales. Brian argues that if Tesla charges roughly $3,000 per month for FSD subscriptions on trucks operating near 24/7, the revenue potential becomes "eye-watering," while still saving fleet operators significant money compared to human-driven diesel operations. Mark adds that autonomous capability would also dramatically increase demand for the vehicle itself.

The hosts debunk several skeptical claims made in the comments. They push back on assertions that the Semi loses 40% range in hot conditions (called inaccurate), that charging infrastructure is nonexistent (the network is being built out and announced, and many operators like Pepsi and Frito-Lay charge on-site anyway), and that total cost of ownership is prohibitive. Brian notes Tesla has been running these trucks internally for six to seven years, providing substantial real-world validation data.

On FSD for articulated trucks: A viewer asks whether Tesla might deploy highway-only FSD initially, with human drivers handling first and last miles. Mark acknowledges the software challenge is real but not insurmountable, and suggests Tesla will likely take more development time before any live deployment rather than rolling out a partial system.

On selling car compute power externally: Brian recounts conversations with two engineers who both concluded this is extremely unlikely — one citing data security concerns for commercial clients, the other explaining that latency and geographic distribution of the chips makes them unsuitable for networked inference workloads. Warranty concerns from running chips at full throttle continuously were also raised.

Trucker range concerns are largely a non-issue in practice. Federal regulations cap driving time at 11 hours per day, with a mandatory 30-minute break before 8 hours are reached. At realistic highway speeds, a driver cannot physically cover 1,000 miles in a legal driving day, meaning the Semi's range is sufficient within regulatory constraints. Charging during mandatory rest stops further alleviates range anxiety.
"We've seen the line in motion... the new line is moving. Tada. We're in Q1."
Predicted Q2 sales: "low hundreds at most"
FSD subscription revenue estimate for autonomous trucking: "$3,000 a month... virtually 24/7 driving model"
Legal maximum driving time for truckers: 11 hours, with a mandatory 30-minute break before the 8-hour mark
85 mph speed limit exists on State Route 130 in Texas — the highest in the U.S. for any vehicle
Factory designed for 50,000 units per year, believed to have capacity beyond that figure

title: Why NOW is the Time to WATCH Tesla Stock And the Market LIKE A HAWK (Alpha Update)

facts.text: Portfolio performance for the first 11 days of March 2026 was +1.94%, outperforming the S&P 500 by 3.14%, the NASDAQ by 1.91%, the Dow Jones by 4.47%, and the core Tesla benchmark by 2.91%. The speaker attributes this to two main tactical decisions: anticipating the Iran crisis and rotating part of the portfolio from Tesla into Nvidia at what he viewed as Nvidia's extreme bottom. He describes this as a deliberate, high-conviction move, not a passive result.

The portfolio has been tracked since January 1, 2024, and is up 90% in two years. The speaker notes the Pioneer Reserve bond simulation started September 1 of the prior year and is targeting a stabilized return channel of 30-45% per year, with a "G45 bond" upper limit of 45% and a guaranteed interest bond targeting 30% plus a 10% annual interest payment. He is careful to note these are not guaranteed returns but a function of portfolio performance so far.

The key tactical move was shifting roughly 40% of the portfolio from Tesla into Nvidia via in-the-money short puts, providing synthetic exposure with premium income while remaining margin-efficient. He acknowledges the position briefly pushed total exposure slightly above 100%, which he describes as slightly leveraged but not reckless. This move was motivated by Nvidia's severe post-earnings drop despite what the speaker called "the best earnings I've ever heard."

The speaker's thesis on Tesla is that it is not currently justified by fundamentals but is being held at elevated prices by expectation of robotaxi launch and Optimus scaling. He views Tesla as a "coiled spring" waiting for an active catalyst, specifically unsupervised robotaxi operations at scale, and predicts Optimus will begin scaling meaningfully by 2028. His price target if the robotaxi/Optimus thesis plays out is $2,000-$4,000 per share, compared to the current ~$400 level.

For Nvidia, the speaker identifies the NVIDIA GTC conference (around March 17-18) as a likely "sell the news" pivot point. His tactical plan is to ride Nvidia's rally into the event, then expect a pullback on or around March 18. He sees Nvidia as having the strongest active "forcing function" of the Mag 7, needing to grow 60-70% annually in line with its profit growth, which he sees as fundamentally driven rather than speculative.

His geopolitical read on the Iran situation is that the conflict is a deliberate strategic tool by Trump ahead of a planned meeting with Chinese President Xi, which he places at the end of March or early April. The logic: Trump wants to undermine China's effort to build a petrodollar alternative involving Iran, Venezuela, Russia, and potentially Saudi Arabia. By destabilizing Iran, Trump signals to China that these arrangements are vulnerable. The speaker predicts Trump will not formally end the conflict before the China meeting but will then quickly exit, likely offloading the conflict to Israel while back-channel negotiations secure Iranian non-interference with Gulf oil flows.

Once the Iran situation resolves and the China meeting produces some form of deal, the speaker predicts a "giant rally" in the market, specifically early Q2 2026. He states 90% confidence in this view and says his portfolio positioning reflects it, with over 100% exposure but not at 200%. He describes the broader Mag 7 as a coiled spring being held back by macro noise and what he dismisses as a "fake narrative of AI bubbles."

The speaker compares his performance to MSCI World, which has a 5-year annualized return of approximately 12% and a 1-year return of 21%. He claims Pioneer Alpha outperformed MSCI World by 5% in a single week, which he notes equals roughly half of MSCI World's annual performance. He also highlights that TSLL (the 2x leveraged Tesla ETF) is down 24% year-to-date, while Pioneer Alpha is beating it by approximately 22% year-to-date.

title: Elon Just Announced Tesla’s AI Workforce

facts.text: Macrohard / Digital Optimus announced as joint Tesla-xAI project.
Timeline: usable in 6 months, running on a $650 Tesla AI4 chip.
AI4-equipped cars can do office work while parked.
Supercharger network as distributed data center infrastructure.
Total addressable market analysis — panelists estimate $40-45 trillion combined TAM.
Optimus 3 production start pulled forward to summer 2026, with high-volume production targeted for summer 2027.
The new Texas "North Campus" bot factory is roughly 9.5-10 million square feet.
Cybercab production showing strong signs — over 100 units sighted, crash-testing of steeringwheel-free version underway.
US Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy called autonomous vehicles a national security issue.
SpaceX IPO speculation and Tesla-SpaceX convergence debate.

title: Tesla Whales Are Making Their Move

facts.text: Major institutional investors are quietly loading up on Tesla. Russell Investment Group increased its Tesla stake by 12.4% in Q3, purchasing an additional 195,000 shares to reach a total of approximately 1.7 million shares worth around $800 million, representing close to 1% of their portfolio. Natixis Advisors, an arm of France's second-largest banking group, similarly increased their position by nearly 5%, also holding approximately 1.8 million shares worth $800 million at about 1.2% of their portfolio. Both positions are described as relatively small allocations with significant room to grow.

Institutions are buying Tesla based on a thesis-driven approach, not stock-picking. Joe Bacti explains that large funds construct investment theses around macro trends — in this case, AGI and physical AI — and then fill "buckets" of exposure. Tesla uniquely occupies the physical AI bucket and the energy bucket simultaneously, meaning institutions feel compelled to own it regardless of personal views on Elon Musk or near-term fundamentals. This thesis-driven buying is described as largely decoupled from quarterly earnings results, making it a more durable and additive form of institutional support.

Dan Ives of Wedbush Securities calls Tesla and Nvidia the two undisputed physical AI kings. His core argument is that Tesla's competitive moat in robotics and autonomous vehicles comes down to scale and scope — no competitor, including Chinese firms, comes close to Tesla's production capabilities for humanoid robots. He frames Tesla as a story about robo-taxi scaling, Cybercab production, Optimus production, and a favorable federal regulatory roadmap, not about quarterly vehicle delivery numbers.

Bacti argues Tesla's robotics advantage combines three elements no competitor possesses simultaneously: frontier deep-tech AI capability, massive supply chain and manufacturing scale, and Elon Musk's pioneering aggressive execution style. Hyundai (which now owns Boston Dynamics) has scale but lacks entrepreneurial risk-taking. Chinese competitors show manufacturing intensity but may lack deep intelligence capability. Small startups have pioneering spirit but no scale. Bacti specifically highlights that making a humanoid robot truly effective requires something approaching "half-consciousness" — a frontier AI problem that is unsolved and where Tesla, potentially working with Grok to convert third-person video into first-person training data at scale, is furthest ahead.

William Blair analyst Jed Dorschheimer values Tesla's energy segment at $120 billion, estimating it at $31 per share, driven by 2,100-plus AI data center projects globally representing 174 gigawatts of capacity. Both Herbert and Bacti view this as a significant underestimate because it models Tesla Energy as a business selling megapacks to external customers, without accounting for the probability that Tesla will consume the majority of this energy output internally to power its own AI data centers, rather than selling it on the open market.

Morgan Stanley, under new analyst Andrew Peroco (who replaced Adam Jonas, who moved to cover AI), estimates Tesla's solar expansion could add $20 to $50 billion in upside, with a fully scaled solar operation potentially generating $25 billion in annual revenue and valuing the energy division at roughly $140 to $190 billion. Elon's stated goal is 100 gigawatts of solar cell production to power data centers on Earth and in space. Herbert and Bacti again argue this misses the point — the solar capacity, the Terrafab, and the megapacks are not products for sale; they are internal supply chain components for Tesla's own AI and energy infrastructure.

The broader master plan is described as "sustainable abundance" aimed at replacing all human physical labor, taking over global transportation, and ultimately enabling space colonization via Dyson swarms. Bacti argues that once the infrastructure stack — solar, batteries, Optimus robots, robo-taxis, satellites, and AI compute — is assembled, Tesla gains the ability to rapidly enter and dominate any physical industry, from shipping to aerospace to defense, through a "conveyor belt of industry takeovers." He predicts this capability will be in place before 2030.

Elon's ability to build physical infrastructure at extraordinary speed is cited as an underrated competitive advantage. Bacti contrasts Tesla's reported two-month timeline for building data centers against Oracle and OpenAI's Stargate project, which has reportedly struggled to deliver on schedule over a year-plus timeline. Cortex 2, Tesla's second supercomputer dedicated specifically to Optimus training, is described as coming half-online imminently, while competitors lack equivalent dedicated compute infrastructure.
Russell Investment Group: 12.4% stake increase in Q3, 195,000 additional shares, total ~1.7 million shares, ~$800 million value.
Natixis Advisors: ~5% position increase, 1.8 million shares, ~$800 million value.
William Blair estimate: Tesla Energy worth $120 billion, $31 per share; 2,100+ global data center projects, 174 gigawatt pipeline.
Morgan Stanley estimate: $20-$50 billion solar upside; $25 billion annual revenue potential; energy division value of $140-$190 billion; $40 per share.
Elon's stated target: 100 gigawatts of solar cell production for both SpaceX and Tesla.
Bacti: "Before 2030, you will be able to do things like that" — referring to AI systems that autonomously design and operate manufacturing at civilizational scale.

title: Tesla and Google See the Same Problem

facts.text: A coalition of companies is lobbying politicians about grid underutilization. Google, Tesla, Verus, Carrier, and others are publicly arguing that the grid is producing power that never reaches useful consumption. Their goal is to change policy and public perception around energy management rather than simply building more generation capacity.

The NIMBY backlash against AI data centers is creating real political and logistical friction. Local communities are pushing back against data center construction, citing concerns about energy consumption. In response, major hyperscalers and MAG7 companies appeared before President Trump and signed a pledge committing to match every new data center with sufficient energy supply to cover its needs — and then some.

Gwen Shotwell (COO of SpaceX, representing xAI because SpaceX acquired xAI) made a specific commitment that xAI's Memphis data center would bring in enough power capacity to supply not just the facility but the entire city of Memphis and beyond. This was presented as a model for how large tech players can reassure local communities rather than simply demanding grid access.

There is a critical timing mismatch between data center construction and utility infrastructure. Data centers estimate they can be built in 1 to 3 years, but power utility companies estimate it takes 3 to 5 years to deliver the necessary power. This gap is a core driver of the demand for Megapack-based bridge solutions that can buffer supply while permanent infrastructure catches up.

Tesla Megapacks address the timing gap by storing energy during low-demand periods and releasing it during peak demand. They also serve as a backup when utilities themselves experience brownouts, allowing Megapacks to feed power back into the grid. This makes them attractive not just to data centers but to utilities as a stabilization tool.

Grid frequency must be maintained within an extremely narrow band. Acceptable frequency is 60 Hz, with mitigation triggered above 60.02 Hz and mandatory corrective action below 59.98 Hz. Battery storage helps smooth these fluctuations faster than conventional generation can respond.

Grid battery deployments have grown explosively, from negligible capacity in 2016 to over 40 gigawatts currently. Tesla alone is projected to have manufacturing capacity for 90 gigawatts of Megapack deployment by the end of 2025 (the current year in the video's context). A William Blair analyst named Jed Dorshimer published a report valuing Tesla's energy business at $120 billion, driven by AI data center demand — though both hosts consider this an underestimate.

Massive amounts of electricity are literally burned off as waste using a device grid engineers call "the toaster." This is a resistive capacitor array used to dump excess power when a line goes down and the grid suddenly has a surplus. The example given is Chief Joseph Dam, where approximately 4 gigawatts can be burned in roughly 3 seconds. The hosts use this as a vivid illustration of how much recoverable power is currently thrown away.

Tesla's vertical integration gives it a decisive cost advantage in the charging and storage market. Tesla builds its own Megapacks, its own chargers, and handles its own deployment. Competitors like Electrify America must purchase Megapacks at a markup and contract out installation, which raises consumer-facing prices. Tesla Superchargers are described as the most affordable in the US market as a direct result of this integration.

Elon Musk has floated space-based data centers as a response to NIMBY opposition on Earth. The logic is that orbital facilities powered by solar energy would bypass the need for utility approvals and state permits entirely. Amazon filed a complaint asking NASA to block approval of a million-satellite plan, while simultaneously claiming it would take centuries to execute — a contradiction that was publicly called out by a senior politician.

The next Megapack factory announcement is expected before the end of the year. The hosts believe the two current US factories (including the newly announced Houston facility, which was revealed casually during an earnings call) may be sufficient for the domestic market. The next factory could be in Europe or Australia, depending on battery cell import duties and local EV market conditions. A Megapack factory reportedly costs around $100 million and can recoup that investment within six months.
"You can burn 1.4 gigawatts per second" — describing the grid's toaster dump resistors
"In those 3 seconds, you just burned about 4 gigawatt of power"
Grid battery deployments: from near zero in 2016 to over 40 GW currently
Tesla's projected Megapack deployment capacity by year-end: 90 GW
William Blair analyst values Tesla energy at $120 billion
Megapack factory cost: approximately $100 million, with a projected payback period of six months
Over 2,200 data centers currently under construction globally
Data center builders estimate 1–3 year timelines; utilities estimate 3–5 years to supply them

title: The REAL Reason Behind The Iran War

facts.text: The Iran war fails basic first-principles scrutiny as a genuine military objective. Iran is too large and sophisticated for the US to occupy or achieve regime change against. A full-scale war would devastate the GOP politically, destroy the careers of figures like Rubio and Vance, cost enormous American treasure and lives, and deliver no defensible strategic prize. No rational actor with Trump's political incentives would accept those costs unless the real objective was something else entirely.

The nuclear weapons justification is fabricated. Publicly available US intelligence reports from early 2025 indicate Iran was not close to developing a nuclear weapon. Trump had already declared "total victory" after bombing alleged nuclear facilities mid-last year, which the speaker views as theater. Without nukes, ballistic missiles are only a security concern for Israel, not the United States, making the stated casus belli hollow.

The "Israel controls Trump" narrative is equally implausible. The speaker dismisses the theory that Trump is acting as Netanyahu's puppet or being blackmailed via Epstein files as "tinfoil hat" thinking. He argues Trump would never deliberately destroy his own legacy, party, and political allies simply because of Israeli pressure.

The real motive is the upcoming Trump-Xi summit in China, scheduled for late March. China is, in the speaker's framing, the singular geopolitical rival that matters — ten times more consequential than Russia, Iran, or anyone else. Every element of US foreign policy, including the tariff restructuring initiated in the prior year, is oriented around containing China. The summit is described as the "pivotal endpoint" of that entire strategic arc.

The Iran war is specifically designed to choke off China's oil supply chain and petrodollar workarounds. Iran and Venezuela had begun settling oil trade with China in yuan rather than dollars, directly threatening US dollar reserve currency dominance. By militarily neutralizing Iran and previously sanctioning Venezuela, the US eliminates two nodes of Chinese energy supply. More critically, by seizing effective control of the Strait of Hormuz, the US gains leverage over Saudi Arabia — which supplies approximately 20% of China's oil. The message to Riyadh: do not experiment with yuan settlements.

Russia is the "number one domino" in this strategy. If Putin can be induced to stop selling oil to China and redirect energy flows back toward Europe, China becomes genuinely energy-isolated. The speaker argues a Ukraine settlement is imminent, structured around a larger deal where Russia rejoins European energy markets. Putin is seen as a rational actor who trusts China no more than he trusts the US, and who would benefit from re-engagement with European buyers.

Confirmation from Senator Chris Murphy's March 11 Senate briefing. Murphy, a Democratic senator from Connecticut, emerged from a two-hour classified briefing on the Iran war and disclosed that war goals explicitly do not include destroying Iran's nuclear program, and regime change is also not on the list. The actual stated goals were destroying missiles, boats, and drone factories — with no coherent plan for what happens when Iran restarts production. The speaker treats this as validation of his entire thesis.

The endgame is a deal with China, followed by US tactical withdrawal from the Iran conflict. Trump will arrive at the China summit having demonstrated the ability to choke off Chinese energy lifelines — Iran neutralized, Venezuela blocked, Saudi Arabia disciplined, and potentially Russia flipped. China will be presented with a fait accompli and pressured into concessions on trade, Taiwan, and AI competition. After the summit, the US is expected to quietly de-escalate, leaving Israel exposed and Iran to redirect its anger toward Tel Aviv rather than Washington.

Israel may be the geopolitical loser in this arrangement. The speaker argues Trump may have used Israel as an instrument — allowing or encouraging Israeli strikes on Iranian leadership, desalination plants, and oil refineries — while positioning the US to later blame Israel for the civilian damage and diplomatically distance itself. Iran's public statements already redirect blame toward Israel rather than the US, which the speaker sees as a deliberate off-ramp being constructed.

Market implications are bullish once the maneuver completes. The speaker predicts markets will rally when the conflict winds down, the Strait of Hormuz is secured, and Iran potentially returns to dollar-denominated oil settlements. Geopolitical risk premium will dissipate and the strategic picture will clarify positively for US economic dominance.

title: Something Is Happening Around Tesla Globally
categories: Autonomous Driving, Electric Vehicles, Market & Competition

facts.text: Tesla China February Sales — Best February Ever, But Context Matters:: Giga Shanghai wholesale numbers came in at 58,599 vehicles (Model 3 and Model Y combined) in February 2026, a 91% year-over-year surge. However, Brian White cautions against a "victory lap" — the comparison is flattering primarily because Giga Shanghai was shut down for Model Y retooling in February 2025. Month-over-month, sales were actually down 15%. Within China specifically, Tesla sold approximately 38,000 vehicles domestically, with the Model Y accounting for 66% and Model 3 the remaining 34%. The Model Y was the second best-selling vehicle in China for February by domestic sales. Importantly, BYD itself reported a 30%+ decline in its own market over the same period, making Tesla's performance relatively strong by industry comparison.

Model Y Outsells Xiaomi YU7 in February:: The Xiaomi YU7 — widely hyped as a "Model Y killer" — was outsold by the Model Y in February. Brian White argues the "killer" framing is overblown: China's market is large enough for multiple strong sellers, and Tesla is effectively capacity-constrained at Giga Shanghai without a new factory. He notes Xiaomi has genuine advantages as a domestic brand with deep tech engineering, strong consumer brand recognition, and a manufacturing partnership that helped it avoid "production hell." However, its competition with Tesla is currently limited to China.

Top-Selling Vehicle in China by Wholesale:: On a wholesale basis (including exports from Giga Shanghai), the Model Y was the top-selling vehicle in China in February with 41,440 units. The number one domestic sales vehicle was the Geely Xingyuan, priced at approximately 70,000 RMB (~$10,000) — a completely different market segment. Brian White notes that Tesla's margin on a single Model Y likely exceeds the entire retail cost of the Geely Xingyuan, making direct unit comparisons misleading.

FSD in China — Competitive Dynamics and Brian White's Firsthand Assessment:: Xpeng CEO He Xiaopeng publicly stated that Tesla's unsupervised Full Self-Driving has "not yet been realized" and declared China the global leader in autonomous driving, predicting L4 autonomy will be fully deployed within designated areas in China within 1–3 years. Brian White, who tested Xpeng's XIMO VA 2.0 system twice within six weeks, offers a more nuanced view: the system improved noticeably between visits — less aggressive at stoplights, smoother starts and stops, good merges — and handled complex Chinese traffic scenarios including 30 scooters driving against traffic on a one-way street. His overall assessment: Xpeng's VA 2.0 is comparable to "a very early version 13" of Tesla FSD. He also notes that FSD in China is not as capable as it is in the US due to less localized training data. Xpeng has received a permit for robotaxi testing and is running an internal pilot in Guangzhou, with hopes for a public beta by end of 2026.

FSD vs. Robotaxi — A Key Technical Distinction:: Brian White relays an insight from Xpeng's lead product manager for VA 2.0: robotaxi is actually the *easier* problem compared to full consumer FSD deployment. Robotaxi operates with remote oversight and controlled geofenced areas, while FSD must work reliably at any hour, in any scenario, without warning — such as a mass evacuation during a typhoon at 3 a.m. This framing supports Tesla's broader thesis that its approach to unsupervised FSD is the harder and more defensible technical achievement.
