title: Robotaxi Rollout is HERE
categories: Autonomous Driving, Robotaxi, Electric Vehicles, Financials, Market & Competition

facts.text: FSD v14.3 with Reasoning Is in Internal Testing:: Elon Musk confirmed via X that FSD v14.3 is currently in internal testing with a wide release expected "in a few weeks." The panel considers this the most significant near-term catalyst, as Elon previously described v14.3 as "the last big piece of the puzzle." The key capability being added is AI reasoning, which would allow the car to read and interpret road signs in real time — something the panel identifies as a critical gap in current FSD performance. Larry Goldberg cautioned that like all major FSD releases, v14.3 will likely iterate through multiple sub-releases (14.3.1, 14.3.2, etc.) over a 3–4 month period before achieving wide capability.

Sign-Reading as a Critical Safety Gap:: Multiple panelists shared real-world examples of FSD failing at unusual or context-dependent signs — including pedestrian crossing caution signs on country roads and conditional turn-restriction signs in Washington D.C. Larry noted that as recently as current versions (v14.x), the car still misses certain non-standard signs. The panel agreed that reasoning-based sign comprehension is foundational to safe autonomous operation and is precisely why Elon calls it the "missing piece." This capability is also essential for international expansion, as signs vary across languages and jurisdictions globally.

Grok as the Car's Operating System:: Larry Goldberg described his expectation that Grok will become the primary operating system interface for Tesla vehicles — enabling voice/text control of windows, doors, navigation, and driving style preferences. He suggested FSD revenue sharing between Tesla and xAI for Grok's role in the car will likely be in the 15–25% royalty range of FSD subscription revenue, which he characterized as fair but not material until robotaxi fleet scale is achieved.

NHTSA and Regulatory Momentum:: The panel discussed Secretary Sean Duffy's AV Safety Forum and his formal announcement that vehicles no longer require steering wheels or pedals. A Tesla robotaxi program manager publicly thanked Secretary Duffy and stated that "innovative American-made AVs like the Cybercab will vastly improve roadway safety and we look forward to working with you on launching them at scale this year." Jeff Lutz noted that this represents a "pull" dynamic — the U.S. government proactively accelerating AV adoption out of competitive concern with China — rather than a "push" from industry lobbying.

Safety Data and Media Narrative Challenges:: Tesla's NHTSA-reported robotaxi safety data for January 15–February 15 showed only 1 incident — a low-speed (under 1 mph) rear-end contact when a vehicle ahead stopped suddenly. The panel contrasted this with Waymo's much larger incident count in the same period, which the media largely ignores. Tesla also released FSD supervised safety data showing the system is approximately 9x safer than unassisted driving. However, a viral Fox News story about a Cybertruck crash claimed FSD was engaged; Elon Musk responded that logs show the driver disengaged Autopilot 4 seconds before the crash. The panel expressed frustration with Tesla's reactive PR approach and noted that the section of the screen showing whether FSD was active was deliberately omitted from the Fox News footage.

Fleet Staging in New Cities Confirmed:: Tesla Model Y robotaxi vehicles — identifiable by rear camera washers and Texas manufacturer license plates — have been spotted in fleets in Orlando, Florida; Henderson (Las Vegas), Nevada; and Dallas, Texas. The vehicles were observed simulating pickup/dropoff maneuvers. Additionally, two validation Teslas with testing equipment were spotted in Houston. The panel interpreted this as strong evidence that Tesla is preparing for imminent commercial launches in these cities, consistent with the earnings call roadmap targeting Dallas, Houston, Phoenix, Miami, Orlando, Tampa, and Las Vegas by end of H1 2026.

Cybercab Up Close: Cern Basher's Austin South by Southwest Visit:: Cern Basher attended a Tesla pop-up event in Austin during South by Southwest where a Cybercab, Optimus, and FSD demo vehicles were on display. Key observations: (1) The Cybercab's door jamb contained a self-certification sticker confirming it meets all federal safety standards without a steering wheel or pedals — meaning Tesla chose the conventional certification route rather than seeking an NHTSA exemption for the 2,500-vehicle cap, implying no production limit on how many can be deployed. (2) Seats are designed at wheelchair height for accessibility. (3) The vehicle has a matte finish (one made in February), though Joe Tegtmeyer has indicated production units may be glossier. (4) USB-C ports are located directly under the central screen for easier cleaning. (5) The illuminated "robotaxi" branded sticker on the doors and rear is powered by the vehicle and was described as striking at night. (6) The neutral position of the butterfly doors is likely "up," making them easy to open manually in a power failure. A trailer with a Cybercab and an Optimus seated inside was being towed through the streets of Austin.

Self-Certification Regulatory Implications:: Cern Basher explained that Tesla's decision to self-certify the Cybercab under existing FMVSS standards (rather than apply for an NHTSA exemption) removes any federal cap on how many units can be produced and deployed. The only remaining constraints are state-level autonomous vehicle operating rules. The panel speculated that NHTSA, under the current administration, has likely had informal conversations with Tesla acknowledging that certain legacy requirements (e.g., rearview mirrors) will not be enforced for fully autonomous vehicles. Federal legislation (the Self-Drive Act) would ultimately create a uniform national standard.

Cybercab Production Ramp Projections:: Jeff Lutz projected a very gradual initial ramp: approximately 100 units in April (start-of-production month), scaling to hundreds per month through May, reaching ~1,000 units/month by June — for a total of roughly a few thousand units in Q2 2026. He expects production to accelerate sharply in Q3. Morgan Stanley, after visiting Giga Texas, confirmed start-of-production is on track for April. However, the panel criticized Morgan Stanley's longer-term forecast of only ~30,000 robotaxi fleet vehicles by 2030, with Larry Goldberg suggesting the real figure could be 300,000–500,000 by that timeframe. Morgan Stanley set a price target of $415 for Tesla stock.

Robotaxi Expansion Strategy and Economics:: The panel discussed how Cybercabs will be distributed across new cities versus concentrated in Austin and the Bay Area. Jeff Lutz noted that the profitability of a robotaxi network is maximized during peak demand periods (morning/evening commute), and that the optimal strategy is to spread vehicles across many cities to capture peaks — not flood one city. Larry Goldberg explained that Waymo is constrained by cost structure: adding more vehicles in a single city beyond a certain threshold produces diminishing returns. Tesla, with far lower vehicle costs, can put more Cybercabs in a market than Waymo and still be profitable. The panel also highlighted that Tesla vehicles generate revenue even when parked, via the AI4 chip performing "Digital Optimus" compute work — a unique competitive advantage no other robotaxi operator has.

TAM Expansion Through Pricing:: Jeff Lutz cited a 2022 Uber internal presentation showing that a $1 decrease in per-mile pricing results in a 10x expansion of the total addressable market. The panel agreed that once Cybercab pricing reaches $1–$1.50/mile, the robotaxi market expands beyond urban cores and begins displacing car ownership decisions for a broad consumer base. Tesla controls this TAM expansion lever directly through its pricing strategy. Elon Musk has stated an ambition to have robotaxi presence in 50% of U.S. cities.

New Model Variants and Production Changes:: Morgan Stanley's note, discussed by Larry Goldberg, confirmed that Tesla is not done launching new car variants. The panel noted that the Model Y Long (YL) 6-seat variant is expected to come to the U.S. market, and that additional Cybertruck variants are being discussed. Larry also raised the possibility of selling Cybercabs directly to private consumers — noting a potentially large market for a two-person urban vehicle — with the implicit expectation that private owners would also contribute their cars to the robotaxi fleet as "shepherds" during peak hours.

Terrafab Semiconductor Project Launch:: Elon Musk announced on March 14, 2026 that the Terrafab semiconductor fabrication project would "launch in 7 days," implying a March 21 announcement. The panel was cautious about expectations, with Jeff Lutz noting that complex multi-billion-dollar manufacturing projects rarely have grand opening ceremonies at groundbreaking. He emphasized, however, that the announcement itself immediately gives Tesla leverage in supply chain negotiations — including capacity reservations at TSMC fabs in Taiwan, Texas, and Arizona — even before a single wafer is produced. The panel agreed the strategic signaling value is immediate even if production is years away.

Nvidia-Uber Robotaxi Partnership:: Nvidia and Uber announced a partnership to deploy robotaxis in Los Angeles and San Francisco in H1 2027, expanding to 28 cities across four continents by 2028. The vehicles will use Nvidia's Drive Hyperion hardware and AV foundation models. Jeff Lutz noted that Nvidia has been making similar AV partnership announcements at GTC for over 11 years, and cautioned that deployment timelines given 2+ years out typically lack specificity. He does not believe Rivian is using Nvidia hardware — Rivian reportedly uses custom silicon.

Uber-Rivian R2 Robotaxi Partnership:: Uber announced a partnership with Rivian to deploy up to 50,000 R2 robotaxis across 25 cities in the U.S., Canada, and Europe by end of 2031, with Uber investing up to $1.25 billion in Rivian. The panel was dismissive of the competitive threat, citing: (1) Rivian will be busy ramping R2 consumer production for 12–18 months and likely cannot simultaneously build out a robotaxi network; (2) Rivian has not demonstrated AV software anywhere near Tesla's capability; (3) the R2's estimated price (~$48,000–$58,000) is roughly double the Cybercab's target cost, making unit economics fundamentally uncompetitive; (4) neither Rivian nor Uber has the charging, service, or fleet management infrastructure Tesla already owns globally. Larry Goldberg compared the Nvidia/Uber/Rivian announcements to legacy automakers' (GM, Toyota, BMW) EV announcements from the early 2020s that failed to materialize at scale.

Waymo's Structural Disadvantage:: The panel revisited Waymo's cost-structure problem at length. New Street Research analysis cited by the panel shows Waymo maximizes ROI by deploying fewer cars across more cities — not more cars in fewer cities — because their cost per vehicle is so high that incremental revenue per additional vehicle in a saturated city turns negative quickly. Tesla's lower cost structure inverts this dynamic, allowing it to profitably operate larger fleets per market. The panel credited Waymo with having "opened the door" in multiple cities regulatorily, which now benefits Tesla's expansion.
Elon Musk on FSD v14.3: "It's in testing right now. Wide release in a few weeks."
Elon Musk on the Cybertruck/Fox News crash: "Logs show the driver disengaged Autopilot 4 seconds before crashing."
Larry Goldberg: "I think they're going to have 300,000 vehicles or 500,000 vehicles by [2030]. Morgan Stanley said 30,000 vehicles in like three years time — that doesn't foot to me."
Uber internal data (2022): A $1 decrease in per-mile pricing results in a 10x increase in TAM.
Morgan Stanley price target on Tesla: $415; robotaxi fleet estimate by ~2030: ~30,000 vehicles (panel considers this far too conservative).
Jeff Lutz Cybercab production ramp estimate: ~100 units in April, hundreds/month in May, ~1,000/month in June; a few thousand total in Q2 2026.
Rivian R2 robotaxi deal: Uber investing up to $1.25 billion; up to 50,000 vehicles; 25 cities by end of 2031.
FSD v14.3 expected to iterate through multiple point releases (14.3.1, 14.3.2, etc.) over 3–4 months.
Tesla robotaxi NHTSA incidents Jan 15–Feb 15: 1 incident (sub-1 mph bumper contact).
Tesla FSD supervised safety: approximately 9x safer than unassisted driving (per latest Tesla release cited in video).

title: Nikola Was Hiding Something Big All Along

facts.text: The Nikola semi-truck's actual drivetrain was a natural gas range-extender electric system, not hydrogen.: Bob's company, EV Drive, was contracted in early 2015 to design and build the electric drive system for the Nikola semi-truck prototype. The system consisted of six PM250 inverters driving six motors across all axles (all-wheel drive), producing approximately 2,000 horsepower. The battery pack was 315 kilowatt-hours using LG NCA 18650 cells configured at 800 volts. The truck also incorporated a natural gas turbine range extender producing approximately 350 horsepower running at 92,000–94,000 RPM, which would have enabled a range of approximately 2,000 miles on combined fuel and electric power. The original plan was a natural gas-powered range-extended electric truck — not a hydrogen fuel cell vehicle.

The December 1, 2016 "World Hydrogen Truck" unveil was staged fraud.: Bob was present at the splashout event and personally unplugged the 120-volt shore power cable that was secretly powering the truck's interior electronics — power that Milton claimed the truck was generating itself. An H2 (hydrogen) sticker had been placed on the front bumper just days before the event, with workers told only that "Trevor said to put it on." Milton announced to an audience that included state governors and trucking industry leaders that this was the world's first hydrogen-powered semi-truck — a claim Bob describes as a "big fat lie." The truck lacked critical gearbox components and was not drivable at the time of the unveil.

The infamous "driving" video released approximately one year later was also fabricated.: After investors pressured Milton to demonstrate the truck actually moving, a video was released showing the truck appearing to drive under its own power at highway speed. Bob's colleague Paul identified that the camera had been tilted to disguise a downhill slope; a reservoir visible in the background appeared at an angle. Paul located the exact spot near Salt Lake City, sent someone out to coast a car downhill in neutral, and confirmed it reached highway speed at that location. The truck rolled downhill by gravity with the camera angled to make it appear level.

Trevor Milton stole intellectual property from Bob's engineering team.: After being given a demonstration of Bob's four-motor electric UTV at the Nikola facility, Milton was enthusiastic about the technology and promised to help commercialize it. Upon returning to Salt Lake City, Milton had his staff quickly file five patents based on Bob's designs — including cut-and-pasted drawings from Bob's own battery module schematics — and put his employees' names on them. Bob states the patent filings contained errors because Milton's team did not fully understand the technology, yet the patents were awarded. This IP theft occurred under NDA, which Bob notes is voided when it would require concealing federal crimes.

Milton's other fabrications included claims of producing hydrogen at $4/kg.: Beyond the truck itself, Milton publicly claimed Nikola was producing hydrogen at $4 per kilogram — a cost that, if real, would have made the company extraordinarily valuable. In reality, Nikola was not producing any hydrogen at all. Bob's colleague Paul became the key whistleblower, working with short-seller Hindenburg Research (who shorted Nikola stock and profited significantly when the fraud was exposed) and with government agencies including the SEC. Milton was eventually indicted, convicted, and sentenced to four years in prison for fraud.

Milton was pardoned by President Trump approximately six months before the video's recording, which Bob characterizes as a corrupt outcome.: Milton had donated approximately $1.8 million to Trump's campaign and hired Brad Bondi (husband of then-Attorney General Pam Bondi) to advocate for his pardon. When asked publicly why he pardoned Milton, Trump cited perceived unfair treatment due to the trial being held in New York while Milton was from Utah. Bob dismisses this justification, stating Milton received a normal legal process and was unambiguously a criminal. Milton's victims lost hundreds of millions of dollars; Milton retains his ill-gotten gains. The Nikola prototype truck was reportedly sold at auction approximately six months prior to the video, with Bob submitting a bid but being outbid.

The electric drivetrain itself was genuinely capable engineering that was delivered on time.: Bob emphasizes that the truck's electric drive system — the portion his team built — was fully assembled, tested, and ready. The truck fell short of being drivable primarily because specialty hardened gears for the two-speed electrically switchable transmissions (engineered by Adrian of AMR Racing) were not completed in time. Had those gears arrived, the 315 kWh battery pack alone would have been sufficient to shake out the truck and demonstrate it driving, independent of the unfinished range extender. EV Drive's contract for the electric drive system alone was $750,000.

title: Tesla Stock is CRASHING - Time to Sell?

facts.text: The host reports that Tesla's robotaxi fleet tracker has grown to 474 vehicles total, up from the previously noted 372, representing a meaningful increase over recent days. However, the host acknowledges this is behind his personal prediction of 900 vehicles by end of March, describing the gap as roughly two weeks behind schedule. The number of fully unsupervised vehicles appears stagnant, which the host notes is a concern for some observers. Three Cybercabs are now listed in the tracker as driverless/unsupervised vehicles, which the host flags as a notable change in the tracker's categorization.

The host expresses strong conviction that Cybercab production is "absolutely on track," citing sightings of Cybercabs on California highways (including on the 101 freeway) as evidence of active testing. He frames April as the expected start of mass production, with May as the outer bound, at which point he expects "many hundreds" of units to be produced. He describes this period as a binary inflection point — a collision of the bull and bear "bubbles" — where one side will be definitively proven wrong. He believes that if autonomous transportation infrastructure visibly rolls out starting in May, Tesla's stock will rise significantly.

The host mentions that Ashok Elluswami (referred to as "Ashok") appears to have been repositioned within xAI, which he interprets as a possible signal that Tesla's FSD development work may be sufficiently advanced — suggesting his job at Tesla may be "already done." He frames this as a potential positive surprise for bulls.

Tesla closed down 3.18% on the day — far more than its beta of approximately 2x relative to the NASDAQ would suggest. The NASDAQ itself fell sharply early in the session but recovered, while Tesla failed to participate in the recovery and in fact declined further after a brief intraday bounce to $383. The host attributes this underperformance to a lack of near-term catalysts, consistent with his earlier prediction that Q1 2026 would be a weak period for the stock with no major catalysts until April or May.

The broader market decline is attributed primarily to the escalating Iran conflict and rising oil prices, with a partial intraday recovery driven by an unexplained drop in oil prices. The host argues that geopolitical noise — including Middle East conflict and inflation fears — is largely irrelevant to Tesla's long-term AGI-driven thesis, and that neither geopolitics nor a broader market crash would materially alter Tesla's trajectory.

The host states he is holding his Tesla position and becoming "more greedy" but is tactically waiting — likely until end of the current month — before adding to his position, wanting to see how the Iran situation develops and whether a broader market crash materializes before deploying more capital.
Tesla closed down 3.18%, approximately a $12 loss on the day, well beyond what its ~2x NASDAQ beta would imply
Tesla robotaxi fleet tracker: 474 total vehicles (up from 372), including 3 Cybercabs listed as driverless/unsupervised
Host's prediction: 900 robotaxi vehicles by end of March — currently roughly two weeks behind pace
Cybercab spotted on California Highway 101, cited as evidence of active deployment testing
Tesla reached a recent local high of $402 on March 16th before the current slide
